(b) the MV3 model **replaces background pages with service workers** — service workers have **no DOM access** and **5-minute idle timeout**, which breaks many ad-blocker and inspector workflows; (c) the MV3 `webRequest` blocking API is **more limited** than MV2's — blocking is restricted to the `declarativeNetRequest` API, which is **easier for sites to detect and bypass** (chrome.declarativeNetRequest is fingerprintable); (d) several popular ad-blockers (uBlock Origin is the most notable) **shipped MV3-lite versions** that are **less effective** than their MV2 versions. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) **anti-adblock detection** (existing test S-029 / PC-011 in the library) is now easier for sites to implement via the `declarativeNetRequest` API fingerprinting; (b) the Monetag malvertising pattern (S-038 above) is more dangerous because **MV3 ad-blockers can't fully block** malicious Monetag creatives — they can only block the *initial* ad network request, not the *redirect chain*; (c) game developers using browser-based dev tools to inspect GameZipper may be unable to use MV3-compatible alternatives to MV2 favorites (e.g., WebGL Inspector, Canvas debug tools); (d) GameZipper's own dev-team extension (if any) must be **MV3-compliant** or it will stop working in 2026-2027. **Test**: (a) **MV3-extension compatibility audit**: list all Chrome extensions GameZipper's QA team uses (likely 10-30 extensions including ad-blockers, inspectors, color-pickers); assert that each has an **MV3-compatible version** available; if any are still MV2-only, find an alternative or accept the loss of functionality; (b) **anti-adblock detection update**: extend PC-011 (anti-adblock pattern detection) to detect the MV3-specific `declarativeNetRequest` fingerprinting — sites can detect MV3 ad-blockers via timing differences in `chrome.declarativeNetRequest` API calls; (c) **Monetag + MV3-adblocker test**: in a Kachilu session with uBlock Origin MV3-lite + AdBlock Plus MV3 + AdGuard MV3, load 20 random game pages and assert the Monetag ad either (i) is fully blocked (good), (ii) is partially blocked (acceptable — creative is replaced with a blank), or (iii) is fully shown with a redirect to a malicious landing page (BAD — this is the S-038 malvertising pattern); (d) **GameZipper dev-extension MV3 audit**: if GameZipper ships any Chrome extension (e.g., a "Pin to GameZipper" extension), assert it is MV3-compliant — check `manifest.json` for `manifest_version: 3` and `background.service_worker` instead of `background.scripts`. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: MV3 is not theoretical — it's a 2025-2026 enforcement reality. GameZipper's QA tools, ad-blocker user experience, and dev workflow all depend on MV3 compatibility. The 6-month transition window (Jan 2025 - Jun 2025) is past; we're now in the MV3-stable era and any MV2-dependent code path is at risk. Source: https://developer.chrome.com/docs/extensions/develop/migrate/what-is-mv3 + https://cwskit.khanakia.com/blog/complete-guide-to-manifest-v3-in-2026 + https://orlandoascanio.com/notes/chrome-extension-manifest-v3-2026 + https://dev.to/ryu0705/building-chrome-extensions-in-2026-a-practical-guide-with-manifest-v3-12h2

#### 11.13.8 2026 Dark-Pattern Research in Mobile Games (arXiv 2412.05039) + EU DSA Art. 28 Intersection
- [G-023] **[P3]** 2026 dark-pattern research in mobile games (arXiv 2412.05039 — 1496 games analyzed) + EU DSA Art. 28 enforcement intersection — arXiv 2412.05039 (December 2024, still the most-cited 2026 paper on the topic) confirms: an academic study analyzed **1,496 mobile games** for **dark patterns** — UI/UX choices that exploit players through **temporal, monetary, social, and psychological** means. Categories found: (a) **temporal dark patterns** — countdown timers on offers that create false urgency ("Offer ends in 00:30!"), cooldown timers that prevent natural play pacing; (b) **monetary dark patterns** — currency exchange rate obfuscation (100 gems = $0.99 but worth 5 in-game items), hidden IAP prices, "Pay to skip" wait times; (c) **social dark patterns** — friend invitation spam, public leaderboard shaming, "your friend beat your score" notifications; (d) **psychological dark patterns** — variable-ratio reward schedules (slot machine mechanics), sunk-cost-fallacy UI ("You've spent 50 hours, don't stop now!"), loss-aversion framing ("You'll lose your streak!"). The study found **prevalence rates of 60-90%** across the 1,496 games for at least one dark pattern. **EU DSA Article 28 enforcement (extending S-032 in the library)**: the **EU Digital Services Act (DSA) Article 28** prohibits dark patterns that **materially distort or impair** the ability of recipients of the service to make **free and informed decisions**. The European Commission + EDPB + national DPAs (CNIL, BfDI, AEPD) have been **enforcing DSA Art. 28 since 2024** with fines ranging from €10K-€2M depending on company size. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) GameZipper is a **free-to-play ad-supported** site, not a paid game, so the **monetary dark patterns** are minimal (no IAP), but the **temporal + psychological + social** patterns may exist in ad placements and "play again" CTAs; (b) GameZipper's **ad-network integration** (Monetag) means the ad creatives themselves may contain dark-pattern elements (e.g., "You won! Claim now!" overlays); (c) EU DSA Art. 28 enforcement is **active** in 2026 — GameZipper's user base includes EU residents (per typical 30-40% EU traffic for casual gaming sites), and a single complaint to a national DPA can trigger an investigation. **Test**: (a) **dark-pattern audit of game pages**: scan all 265 game pages for the dark-pattern UI elements identified in arXiv 2412.05039 — for each pattern, assert GameZipper does NOT use it; specific checks: (i) **no false-urgency timers** on game-over screens (e.g., "Bonus expires in 5 seconds!" — accept only if the timer is honest and the bonus is real); (ii) **no fake currency exchange obfuscation** (GameZipper has no in-game currency, so this is N/A — but the ad creatives served by Monetag must be checked); (iii) **no "your friend beat you" notifications** without an opt-in preference; (iv) **no sunk-cost framing** ("You've played 50 levels, don't stop!"); (b) **Monetag ad creative dark-pattern scan**: for the Top 20 games, capture screenshots of the 5 ad creatives served by Monetag per game and assert NONE contain dark-pattern text matching `/you.*(won|winner|selected|chosen)/i` (fake winner selection) or `/expires.*in.*\d+.*sec/i` (false urgency); (c) **EU DSA Art. 28 disclaimer**: add a footer-level note on the homepage and game pages linking to GameZipper's DSA-compliance page (which states: no dark patterns, no false urgency, no hidden costs, opt-in social features); (d) **DPA contact channel**: assert that GameZipper's privacy policy + terms include a contact channel for EU regulatory complaints (a dedicated `dsa@` or `compliance@` email); this is the **first** step in any DSA Art. 28 complaint handling. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: EU DSA Art. 28 is now an **active enforcement regime** — the largest fines in 2025-2026 have been €1-2M for "cookie wall" dark patterns. Even though GameZipper is a small ad-supported site, a single motivated user can file a complaint and trigger an investigation that consumes engineering time. The 1,496-game arXiv study provides **evidence-based** dark-pattern categories GameZipper's QA can use to pre-emptively audit. Source: https://arxiv.org/html/2412.05039v1 (dark patterns in mobile games study)

### 11.14 R100 June 2026 Edge Pwn2Own Chained Exploit + Chrome ANGLE Critical CVE + Android Zero-Day + WebGPU Performance + Non-Major Browser WebGL Compat (R100 — v1.17.0)

#### 11.14.1 Edge Pwn2Own Berlin 2026 — Chained CVE-2026-45492/45494/45495 RCE Exploit ($175K)
- [B-038] **[P0]** Edge Pwn2Own Berlin 2026 chained exploit — CVE-2026-45492 (origin validation bypass, CVSS 4.3) + CVE-2026-45494 (UXSS, CVSS 5.0) + CVE-2026-45495 (directory traversal RCE, CVSS 7.5) — discovered by Orange Tsai (DEVCORE Research Team) at **Pwn2Own Berlin 2026**, earning $175,000. All three vulnerabilities were coordinated through Zero Day Initiative (ZDI-26-329/330/331) and disclosed June 4, 2026. **Chaining pattern**: CVE-2026-45492 (origin bypass) → CVE-2026-45494 (UXSS via navigation event injection) → CVE-2026-45495 (path traversal to RCE) = full system compromise from a single crafted web page. While individual CVEs are tracked as B-009/010/012, the **chaining pattern is NEW**: a game page that embeds cross-origin iframes (Monetag ads, Cloudflare beacons) is the exact attack surface — the attacker lures the user to a game page with a malicious ad creative, which triggers the origin bypass, then injects JavaScript via UXSS, then leverages Edge's feedback-log path for RCE. **Test**: (a) **Edge version gate**: assert `navigator.userAgent` on game page detects Edge < 148.0.3967.70 and shows a **blocking security banner** (unlike Chrome CVEs where banner is non-blocking — these are actively exploited and chainable to RCE); (b) **iframe sandbox hardening**: verify all cross-origin iframes on game pages (Monetag, Cloudflare, analytics) use `sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin"` without `allow-top-navigation` or `allow-popups` — this blocks the UXSS escalation path; (c) **Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors**: assert `frame-ancestors 'none'` or `frame-ancestors 'self'` is set on all game pages via HTTP header or meta tag — prevents the game page from being framed on a malicious site that initiates the chain; (d) **Edge telemetry**: monitor Edge crash/feedback logs (`edge://crashes`) for unexpected entries that could indicate exploitation attempts. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: 265 game pages × embedded Monetag ad iframes = 265 potential entry points for the Pwn2Own chain. Edge users (~15% desktop market share) on pre-148 versions are at full RCE risk. Source: https://cybersecuritytimes.com/microsoft-edge-vulnerability + https://cyberpress.org/microsoft-edge-vulnerability

#### 11.14.2 Chrome ANGLE CVE-2026-10881 — Critical OOB Read+Write in Graphics Engine ($97K Bounty)
- [B-039] **[P0]** Chrome ANGLE CVE-2026-10881 — out-of-bounds read and write in ANGLE (CVSS 9.6, critical) — Forbes + The Hacker News confirm CVE-2026-10881 is the **highest-severity CVE** in Chrome 149's record 429-CVE batch. ANGLE (Almost Native Graphics Layer Engine) is Chrome's cross-platform WebGL implementation layer — it translates WebGL calls to DirectX/OpenGL/Metal/Vulkan. An OOB read+write in ANGLE means a **crafted web page can escape the GPU sandbox and execute code on the host**. Google paid $97,000 bounty for this single CVE (the largest single bounty in Chrome 149). **GameZipper relevance**: 240+ games use WebGL via ANGLE — every game page that initializes a WebGL context (`document.createElement('canvas').getContext('webgl')`) is the exact attack surface. The exploit requires a crafted WebGL shader or texture, which means a **malicious ad creative served by Monetag** could trigger ANGLE OOB if it loads a WebGL-based ad unit. **Test**: (a) **Chrome version gate**: assert game page detects Chrome < 149.0.7827.53 and shows a non-blocking security banner ("Critical graphics security update available — please update Chrome"); (b) **WebGL context isolation**: verify that all game canvases use `getContext('webgl', { failIfMajorPerformanceCaveat: true })` to reject contexts on compromised GPU drivers; (c) **Monetag ad WebGL audit**: in a sandboxed Kachilu session, load 20 game pages and monitor whether any Monetag ad creative creates its own WebGL context (detected via `page.on('console', msg => /webgl/i.test(msg.text()))`); if any ad creates a WebGL context, it is a potential ANGLE exploit vector; (d) **GPU process crash detection**: assert that `chrome://gpu` shows no ANGLE errors on Chrome 149+ after loading 5 Top games. Source: https://forbes.com/sites/daveywinder/2026/06/05/google-chrome-149-new-update-fixes-429-security-flaws-22-critical + https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/ai-agent-uncovers-21-zero-days-in.html

#### 11.14.3 Android Framework Zero-Day CVE-2025-48595 — Actively Exploited Privilege Escalation
- [S-039] **[P1]** Android Framework zero-day CVE-2025-48595 — actively exploited in targeted attacks for local privilege escalation on Android 14+ — BleepingComputer + TechRepublic confirm Google's June 2026 Android security update patches **124 vulnerabilities** including one actively exploited zero-day (CVE-2025-48595). The vulnerability exists in the **Android Framework** component and allows local attackers to gain **code execution and privilege escalation** with no additional execution privileges needed. Similar flaws have been exploited by commercial spyware and nation-state operations. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) ~40% of GameZipper's traffic comes from Android devices (Chrome Android / WebView); (b) the Android Framework vulnerability affects the **WebView component** — any game page loaded in an Android app's WebView (e.g., a "GameZipper" native wrapper app, or any third-party app that embeds game URLs) is at risk; (c) even in standalone Chrome Android, a malicious ad creative could trigger the Framework vulnerability via a specially crafted intent URL. **Test**: (a) **Android WebView version audit**: if GameZipper has or plans a native Android wrapper, assert the embedded WebView is based on Chromium 149+ (which includes the Android Framework patch); (b) **intent URL hardening**: verify game pages do not expose `intent://` scheme URLs that could be leveraged for privilege escalation — `grep -rn 'intent://' --include="*.html" --include="*.js" .` must return 0 matches; (c) **Monetag ad intent-scan**: in Chrome Android emulation (Playwright with `isMobile: true, userAgent: '...'`), load 20 game pages and assert no Monetag ad creative triggers an `intent://` URL redirect; (d) **Samsung-specific patch lag**: Samsung devices (94% of Android market in some regions) typically lag 2-4 weeks behind Google's patch schedule — if GameZipper's analytics show significant Samsung traffic, add a one-time "Security update available for your device" notice for Android users with security patch level < 2026-06-01. Source: https://bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/google-fixes-one-actively-exploited-android-zero-day-124-flaws + https://techrepublic.com/article/news-google-june-2026-android-security-update

#### 11.14.4 Chrome 150 Last Version for macOS 12 Monterey — User Base Fragmentation Risk
- [W-116] **[P2]** Chrome 150 is the last version supporting macOS 12 Monterey — Forbes confirms Google will end Chrome support for macOS 12 (Monterey) after Chrome 150. Users on macOS 12 who don't upgrade to macOS 13+ will stop receiving Chrome security updates, creating a **permanently vulnerable user segment**. macOS 12 was released in 2021 and Apple ended security updates for it in late 2024. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) a subset of Mac users (estimated 5-10% of Mac traffic) will be stuck on Chrome 150 permanently; (b) Chrome 151+ will ship security fixes that Chrome 150 users never receive — GameZipper must decide whether to (i) show a deprecation notice to macOS 12 users, (ii) maintain backward compatibility with Chrome 150 indefinitely, or (iii) drop support for Chrome 150 security features; (c) the **ANGLE CVE-2026-10881** (B-039 above) was patched in Chrome 149 — but **future ANGLE CVEs** patched in Chrome 151+ will NOT reach macOS 12 users on Chrome 150. **Test**: (a) **macOS 12 user detection**: in game page JS, detect macOS 12 via `navigator.userAgentData?.platform || navigator.platform` + `navigator.userAgent` parsing; if macOS 12 detected, show a one-time non-blocking notice "Your operating system is no longer receiving security updates. Please upgrade macOS for the best experience."; (b) **Chrome 150 feature-freeze test**: run all Top 30 games on Chrome 150 (last Monterey-compatible version) and assert no regression; any feature that requires Chrome 151+ must have a fallback path for Chrome 150; (c) **analytics tracking**: add macOS version tracking to GameZipper's analytics (localStorage `gz_aa`) to measure the macOS 12 user percentage — if >2%, treat Chrome 150 as a supported target; if <2%, deprecate with a notice. Source: https://forbes.com/sites/zakdoffman/2026/01/26/googles-chrome-decision-updates-stop-for-millions-of-apple-users

#### 11.14.5 Google I/O 2026 WebMCP Origin Trial — Agent-Game Interaction Standard
- [G-024] **[P2]** Google I/O 2026 WebMCP origin trial — proposed open standard for AI agent-to-website interaction — Chrome for Developers blog (May 19 2026) announces **WebMCP**, a proposed open web standard that lets websites expose structured tools (JS functions, HTML forms) to browser-based AI agents. Origin trial starts in Chrome 149. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) in the "agentic web" era, AI agents may browse gamezipper.com to find games for users ("Find me a puzzle game like 2048"); (b) if GameZipper exposes WebMCP tools, an agent could (i) query `searchGames({category: 'puzzle', difficulty: 'hard'})`, (ii) start a game session, (iii) submit a score to a leaderboard — all without a human clicking; (c) this creates **new testing surface**: game pages must be both human-interactable AND agent-interactable; (d) the risk: an automated agent playing a game at superhuman speed could trigger race conditions in game state (e.g., two simultaneous `gameOver()` calls), or expose exploits in score submission APIs. **Test**: (a) **WebMCP exposure audit**: assert that gamezipper.com does NOT currently expose any WebMCP tools (scan for `navigator.mcp`, `registerProtocolHandler`, or `window.__webMCP`); if none, mark N/A for now; (b) **agent-rate-limiting**: if GameZipper adds WebMCP tools in the future, assert that all agent-callable functions have **rate limiting** (max 10 calls/minute per session) to prevent automated abuse; (c) **agent vs human parity**: if a game is playable via WebMCP, the game outcome must be **identical** whether played by a human or an agent — test by having an agent play 5 games and comparing score ranges to human baselines; (d) **Chrome DevTools for agents**: Chrome 149 ships DevTools APIs for agents (console logs, network traffic, accessibility trees) — GameZipper's QA can use these agent-facing DevTools for automated testing (extend existing deep-play-test.js to use agent DevTools protocol). Source: https://developer.chrome.com/blog/chrome-at-io26

#### 11.14.6 Agentic AI Playtesting Pattern — RL + LLM-Driven Autonomous QA
- [G-025] **[P2]** Agentic AI playtesting for HTML5 games — The Coders Blog (2026) confirms the rise of **agentic AI** (Reinforcement Learning + LLM-driven) for game playtesting: autonomous agents that explore game environments, discover exploits, and generate narrative bug reports. WeTest GDC 2026 showcased an AI Test Agent Platform. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) GameZipper's current QA relies on Playwright scripts + Kachilu sessions + manual testing — all scripted, not autonomous; (b) agentic AI could (i) autonomously explore all 265 games and find edge-case bugs (e.g., "if you click the undo button 50 times rapidly, the game state corrupts"), (ii) generate descriptive bug reports ("In chess, when White promotes a pawn to Queen while the timer is below 3 seconds, the board renders two Queens"), (iii) scale to cover all 265 games × all device profiles × all edge cases in hours instead of weeks; (c) the Google Big Sleep agent found 371 CVEs in Chrome 149 (see G-022) — the same technique can be applied to game logic bugs. **Test**: (a) **agentic QA pilot**: select 5 Top games (2048, chess, sudoku, color-sort, tetris); for each, run a simple RL agent that takes random valid actions for 1000 steps; collect crash count, console error count, and state inconsistency events; compare to existing Playwright test coverage — if the agent finds bugs that Playwright misses, expand the pilot; (b) **LLM bug-report quality**: for each agent-discovered bug, generate an LLM-based narrative report and compare to a human-written report for the same bug — assert the LLM report includes (i) reproduction steps, (ii) expected vs actual behavior, (iii) severity assessment; (c) **scale estimation**: estimate the compute cost of running agentic QA on all 265 games × 1000 steps/game — if < $50/run (achievable with local LLMs), schedule as a weekly cron job; (d) **integration with existing QA pipeline**: add agentic QA results to the `verify-lite.js` output format so R0-R3 verification rounds can include agent-discovered bugs alongside human and scripted findings. Source: https://thecodersblog.com/agentic-ai-for-game-playtesting-2026 + https://wetest.net/blog/wetest-at-gdc-2026-1186.html

#### 11.14.7 p5.js WebGPU Severe Performance Regression — Migration Risk Signal
- [C-039] **[P1]** p5.js WebGPU severe performance regression — GitHub issue processing/p5.js#8471 confirms WebGPU rendering in the popular p5.js creative-coding framework has **severe performance issues** even for simple sketches (drawing 10 concentric circles drops from 60fps to 5-10fps on WebGPU vs 60fps stable on WebGL). The issue was reported in January 2026 and is still open. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) while GameZipper games don't use p5.js directly, the **underlying WebGPU performance pattern** applies to any game considering WebGPU migration — the perf regression is in the **WebGPU pipeline itself** (buffer mapping, command submission overhead), not in p5.js; (b) GameZipper's WebGPU migration plan (C-038 / G-021) must account for the possibility that **simple 2D games may perform WORSE on WebGPU than WebGL** — the overhead of WebGPU's explicit pipeline management (vs WebGL's state machine) can negate performance gains for draw-call-light workloads; (c) the regression is most pronounced on **mobile GPUs** where WebGPU driver overhead is higher. **Test**: (a) **WebGPU vs WebGL A/B performance**: for 5 representative games (2D puzzle, 2D arcade, canvas-only idle, card game, board game), create both WebGL and WebGPU render paths; measure FPS on (i) desktop Chrome 149 with dedicated GPU, (ii) desktop Chrome 149 with integrated GPU, (iii) Chrome Android on mid-range phone; assert WebGPU FPS ≥ WebGL FPS for all 3 device classes; if WebGPU FPS < WebGL FPS on any class, document the regression and mark the game as **"WebGL-preferred"** (do NOT force WebGPU migration); (b) **draw-call threshold**: establish a minimum draw-call count threshold for WebGPU migration — if a game has < 100 draw calls per frame (most 2D casual games), WebGPU's pipeline overhead is likely to exceed its gains; if > 500 draw calls (particle-heavy games), WebGPU should be faster; (c) **mobile GPU overhead measurement**: on Chrome Android, compare `performance.now()` delta between `requestAnimationFrame` callbacks for WebGL vs WebGPU versions of the same game; if WebGPU delta > WebGL delta by > 20%, flag as regression; (d) **p5.js community fix tracking**: monitor processing/p5.js#8471 for resolution — when the p5.js team fixes their WebGPU perf regression, the underlying WebGPU pipeline patterns they change may be applicable to GameZipper's custom game renderers. Source: https://github.com/processing/p5.js/issues/8471

#### 11.14.8 Vivaldi 7.8 WebGL Broken After Chromium Upgrade — Non-Major Browser Compat
- [W-117] **[P1]** Vivaldi 7.8 WebGL broken after Chromium upgrade — Vivaldi Forum topic 115268 reports that Vivaldi 7.8 (based on Chromium 7.8.3925.56) breaks WebGL rendering on specific GPU/driver combinations. Users report that `chrome://gpu` shows "WebGL: Hardware accelerated" but actual WebGL canvases render blank or with visual corruption. The issue is triggered by a Chromium GPU driver bug list entry that incorrectly disables hardware acceleration for certain GPU models. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) Vivaldi is a **niche but significant browser** for power users (~1-2% desktop share, higher among tech-savvy gamers); (b) the Vivaldi WebGL breakage follows a pattern seen in **every Chromium-based browser upgrade** — the GPU driver bug list (`gpu/config/gpu_driver_bug_list.json`) sometimes incorrectly blacklists GPU hardware, causing WebGL to fall back to software rendering (SwiftShader) which is 10-50× slower; (c) the same issue affects **Brave, Opera, and other Chromium derivatives** when they pick up a new Chromium base; (d) GameZipper's QA currently tests Chrome + Edge + Safari + Firefox but **does not test Vivaldi, Brave, or Opera** — these browsers collectively represent ~3-5% of desktop traffic and all share Chromium's WebGL stack. **Test**: (a) **non-major Chromium browser matrix**: add Vivaldi (latest), Brave (latest), and Opera (latest) to the QA browser matrix — at minimum, run Top 10 games smoke test on each; (b) **WebGL fallback detection**: in game page JS, add a check after `canvas.getContext('webgl')` that measures the **renderer string** (`gl.getParameter(gl.RENDERER)`) — if it contains "SwiftShader" or "llvmpipe", the game is running on software rendering and will be slow; log this to analytics; (c) **GPU driver bug list awareness**: when a Chromium-based browser update breaks WebGL, the typical fix is either (i) updating the browser to a version that corrects the bug list entry, or (ii) the user manually disabling the blacklist via `--ignore-gpu-blocklist`. Source: https://forum.vivaldi.net/topic/115268/7.8.3925.56-trouble-with-webgl-due-to-upgrade-to-7.8

### 11.15 R101 June 2026 Firefox Sandbox Escape + Chrome Audio/Canvas CVEs + FFmpeg Zero-Days + HTML-in-Canvas API + Neuro-Inclusive Design

#### 11.15.1 Firefox 151 Sandbox Escape + WebGPU Information Disclosure — Cross-Browser CVE Wave
- [B-040] **[P0]** Firefox 151 sandbox escape CVE-2026-8945 + WebGPU info disclosure CVE-2026-8967 — Firefox 151 (released May 19 2026) fixes a **sandbox escape** in Firefox for Android/Focus (CVE-2026-8945, high severity) where the Android sandbox boundary could be bypassed. Additionally, CVE-2026-8967 is a **WebGPU information disclosure** vulnerability (medium severity) that could leak GPU memory contents. WebRTC privilege escalation CVE-2026-8972 (high severity) affects audio/video communication channels. Multiple audio/video WebCodecs bugs (CVE-2026-8946/8954/8968) affect boundary conditions and integer overflow in media processing. Process Sandboxing information disclosure CVE-2026-8958 (high severity) also affects security boundaries. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) Firefox desktop share ~6% globally but **higher in Europe (8-10%)**; (b) WebGPU info disclosure means a malicious or compromised ad creative served through Monetag could potentially read GPU memory containing game state data; (c) WebRTC privilege escalation could affect future multiplayer HTML5 game features; (d) Firefox Android sandbox escape directly affects mobile gamers on Firefox for Android. **Test**: (a) run Top 30 games on **Firefox 151+ desktop** — verify WebGL/WebGPU canvas renders correctly, no visual corruption, no console errors referencing GPU process; (b) run Top 10 games on **Firefox for Android** — verify no crashes, no GPU process crashes, no sandbox-related errors in `about:crashes`; (c) test WebRTC-based features (if any games use peer-to-peer) on Firefox for privilege escalation regression; (d) verify game audio (Web Audio API BGM + SFX) works correctly on Firefox 151+ — the WebCodecs bugs could affect audio decoding in games using `<audio>` elements. Source: https://www.mozilla.org/en-US/security/advisories/mfsa2026-54/

#### 11.15.2 Chrome 149 Audio UAF + Canvas Policy Bypass — Game Media Pipeline Vulnerability
- [B-041] **[P1]** Chrome 149 Audio UAF CVE-2026-10933 + Canvas policy bypass CVE-2026-11081 — Beyond the 22 critical CVEs in Chrome 149 (B-033), this case specifically tracks **game-relevant high-severity Audio and Canvas vulnerabilities**: (a) CVE-2026-10933 is a **use-after-free in Audio** component (high severity) — directly affects Web Audio API used by HTML5 games for BGM, SFX, and spatial audio; rapid load/unload cycles (navigating between game pages) could trigger UAF crashes; (b) CVE-2026-11081 is a **Canvas policy bypass** (medium severity) — the security boundary around Canvas rendering could be bypassed, potentially allowing cross-origin pixel data access from compromised ad scripts; (c) CVE-2026-11136 is a **Canvas use-after-free** (medium severity) — canvas elements could be freed while still referenced, causing crashes during game rendering. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) every game uses Canvas for rendering — Canvas policy bypass could allow Monetag ad scripts to read game canvas pixel data; (b) games using Web Audio API for sound effects are at risk of UAF crashes during page transitions; (c) rapid navigation between game pages (homepage → game → homepage → different game) stresses the audio/canvas lifecycle and could trigger these bugs. **Test**: (a) **Audio stress test**: navigate between 10 game pages rapidly (< 2s between navigations), monitoring for AudioContext-related crashes or console errors; (b) **Canvas isolation test**: verify that Monetag ad iframes cannot access game canvas pixel data via `getImageData` or `toDataURL` on cross-origin canvas elements; (c) **Canvas UAF test**: open a game, start playing, then navigate away mid-render (during active requestAnimationFrame) — verify no crash or memory leak. Sources: Chrome Releases Blog (https://chromereleases.googleblog.com/2026/06/stable-channel-update-for-desktop.html), Forbes (https://forbes.com/sites/daveywinder/2026/06/05/google-chrome-149-new-update-fixes-429-security-flaws-22-critical)

#### 11.15.3 FFmpeg 21 Zero-Days (AI-Discovered) — Browser Media Pipeline Supply Chain
- [B-042] **[P1]** FFmpeg 21 zero-days (AI-discovered) affecting browser media pipeline — An AI agent (depthfirst) discovered 21 zero-day vulnerabilities in FFmpeg (CVE-2026-39210 through CVE-2026-39218, 12 more pending CVE assignment). The bugs include heap/stack overflows in TS demuxer, VP9 decoder, and service-description-table parsing. The oldest bug has existed for **23 years** (since 2003). Total cost to discover: ~$1,000 using AI agent — demonstrating that AI-driven vulnerability discovery dramatically lowers the cost of finding deep supply-chain bugs. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) FFmpeg is embedded in **browser media stacks** — Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge all use FFmpeg-derived code for media decoding; (b) WebCodecs API and `<video>`/`<audio>` elements in HTML5 games rely on FFmpeg for audio/video decoding; (c) games using video cutscenes, animated backgrounds, or audio files with complex codecs (VP9, AV1, Opus) are in the threat surface; (d) this is a **supply-chain vulnerability** — GameZipper doesn't use FFmpeg directly, but browsers that render GameZipper games do. **Test**: (a) verify all game media assets (video, audio) use widely-supported, well-tested codecs (H.264/AAC preferred over VP9/AV1 for security); (b) test games with media-heavy content on Chrome 149+, Firefox 151+, Safari 26.5+ — verify no media decoder crashes; (c) monitor Chrome crash reports (`chrome://crashes`) after playing games with video/audio content for FFmpeg-related crash signatures. Source: https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/ai-agent-uncovers-21-zero-days-in.html

#### 11.15.4 Chrome HTML-in-Canvas API (Origin Trial) — Game Accessibility + Rendering Hybrid
- [W-118] **[P1]** Chrome HTML-in-Canvas API (origin trial Chrome 149+) — game accessibility + rendering hybrid test — Chrome I/O 2026 announced the **HTML-in-Canvas API** (origin trial in Chrome 149): integrates real DOM elements into Canvas/WebGL/WebGPU contexts, making in-game UI elements **searchable, accessible, and natively translatable**. This is the biggest 2026 API for HTML5 game accessibility: it allows mixing real HTML/DOM elements inside Canvas/WebGL/WebGPU contexts — game UI text (scores, buttons, menus) rendered as actual DOM that screen readers can access and search engines can index, while maintaining GPU-rendered game graphics. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) currently all GameZipper canvas-based games render UI text via Canvas API (`fillText()`), which is **invisible to screen readers and search engines**; (b) HTML-in-Canvas would allow game HUD elements to be real HTML overlaid on GPU-rendered canvas, solving the longstanding accessibility gap; (c) this is an **origin trial** — not yet stable — but early adoption gives GameZipper an accessibility moat. **Test**: (a) identify 3-5 representative canvas-heavy games (puzzle + arcade + idle); (b) for each, create a test build using HTML-in-Canvas API for HUD elements (score, timer, level indicator); (c) verify screen readers (VoiceOver / NVDA / ChromeVox) can read HUD text; (d) verify no rendering artifacts when mixing DOM+Canvas; (e) verify `document.querySelector('canvas *')` returns the embedded HTML elements; (f) benchmark FPS impact of HTML-in-Canvas vs pure fillText(). Source: https://developer.chrome.com/blog/chrome-at-io26

#### 11.15.5 Soft Navigations API — Core Web Vitals Measurement for SPA Game Portals
- [W-119] **[P2]** Soft Navigations API brings Core Web Vitals to SPAs — game portal LCP/INP measurement — Chrome I/O 2026 announced the **Soft Navigations API** for measuring Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) on SPA-style page transitions. GameZipper homepage uses SPA-style category filtering (All/Puzzle/Arcade/Idle/Card/Match-3 buttons) and search. Currently, Core Web Vitals only measure the initial page load — SPA transitions are invisible to performance monitoring. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) category filter transitions are effectively soft navigations — the URL doesn't change but the visible content does; (b) search filtering is also a soft navigation; (c) without Soft Navigations API, these transitions are invisible to Lighthouse/Chrome UX Report, creating a **blind spot in performance monitoring**; (d) if a filter transition causes layout shift (CLS) or slow interaction (high INP), we can't currently detect it. **Test**: (a) enable Soft Navigations API observation on homepage; (b) for each category filter button click, measure: LCP (time to largest contentful paint of new card grid), INP (interaction latency of filter click), CLS (layout shift during filter transition); (c) assert CLS < 0.1 for all filter transitions; (d) assert INP < 200ms for filter click; (e) assert LCP < 2.5s for filtered content rendering. Source: https://developer.chrome.com/blog/chrome-at-io26

#### 11.15.6 60-Second FTUE Churn Window — Casual Game First-Time User Experience
- [G-026] **[P2]** 60-second churn window — FTUE cognitive barrier test for casual games — 2026 industry research (Game Developers Org mobile genre breakdown, $92B market) confirms that **casual players have a 60-second churn window**: any cognitive barrier encountered in the first 60 seconds causes immediate abandonment. The "invisible tutorial" pattern (teach by doing, not text dumps) is the 2026 standard. Day-1 retention benchmark for Top 25% casual games: **28-31%**. Average session length: **5-8 minutes**. Average CPI: **$0.98**. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) every GameZipper game must pass a 60-second FTUE test — the player should be playing the core loop within 60 seconds of page load; (b) splash screens must dismiss within 3 seconds (currently 8-second auto-dismiss may be too slow); (c) text-wall tutorials, multi-step setup wizards, or "loading..." screens > 3 seconds are churn killers; (d) the first game action should produce **satisfying audio/visual feedback** within 3 seconds to establish the "instant gratification" expectation. **Test**: (a) for each game, measure time from page load to first meaningful game action (first move in puzzle, first card play, first click in arcade); assert ≤ 10 seconds; (b) count number of text instructions visible before first action; assert ≤ 2 short labels (no paragraph-length instructions); (c) verify audio/visual feedback on first action (sound plays, score changes, animation triggers); (d) verify splash dismisses in ≤ 3 seconds OR splash is the first interactive element (tap to dismiss → game starts). Source: https://www.game-developers.org/mobile-game-genre-breakdown-2026

#### 11.15.7 Neuro-Inclusive Design 2026 Standard — Accessibility Beyond WCAG
- [A-008] **[P1]** Neuro-inclusive design 2026 standard — reduced motion + single-stick + cognitive assists — 2026 game UX guide (Boundev) establishes **neuro-inclusive design as a standard, not an option**: (a) **reduced motion options** for motion sickness (must respect `prefers-reduced-motion` media query); (b) **single-stick / one-handed controls** for physical accessibility — games must be playable with touch-only (no keyboard required); (c) **cognitive assists** for ADHD/memory challenges — highlighting objectives, minimizing distractions, clear visual hierarchy; (d) **fully remappable inputs** described as the "gold standard for physical accessibility"; (e) AI adaptive difficulty monitoring player frustration (3 consecutive failures → subtle difficulty adjustment) is emerging as a standard pattern. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) all canvas-based games currently use `requestAnimationFrame` continuous rendering — some have no reduced-motion alternative, causing motion sickness for sensitive users; (b) most games already support touch but keyboard-dependent features (e.g., keyboard shortcuts in card games) may not have touch equivalents; (c) objective highlighting is inconsistent — some puzzle games have no visual indicator of the current goal. **Test**: (a) for each game, verify `prefers-reduced-motion` is respected — animations should reduce or stop when the media query matches; (b) verify each game is fully playable with touch-only input (no keyboard required for any core action); (c) verify current objective/goal is visually highlighted within the game canvas (not just in external text); (d) verify audio feedback is provided for every significant action (correct move, wrong move, level complete). Source: https://boundev.ai/blog/game-ux-design-guide-2026

#### 11.15.8 Modl.ai AI Bot Playtesting + Figma AI — New QA Tool Integration
- [C-040] **[P2]** Modl.ai AI bot playtesting + Figma AI text-to-UI — new QA tool evaluation — 2026 game testing tools landscape includes: (a) **Modl.ai** — AI bots that autonomously playtest game levels, covering edge cases human testers miss; bots learn game mechanics through RL and explore state spaces systematically; (b) **Figma AI text-to-UI** — generates wireframes from text descriptions, can be used for rapid game UI prototyping and accessibility review. **GameZipper relevance**: (a) GameZipper's existing `deep-play-test.js` uses scripted 8-action-type patterns — Modl.ai's RL-based exploration could discover bugs that scripted patterns miss (e.g., unusual click sequences, edge-case game states); (b) Figma AI could generate UI mockups for game HUD elements that meet the neuro-inclusive design criteria (A-008) before implementation; (c) Chrome DevTools now includes AI-assisted debugging — Lighthouse data integration + interactive reasoning widgets (LY Corporation reduced manual analysis by **96-98%** using AI DevTools). **Test**: (a) evaluate Modl.ai free tier — can it run against 5 representative GameZipper games? What's the setup cost?; (b) compare Modl.ai bug detection rate vs deep-play-test.js on same 5 games; (c) use Figma AI to generate UI mockups for 3 games' HUD elements — review for accessibility compliance (contrast, size, thumb-zone); (d) benchmark Chrome AI DevTools debugging time vs manual DevTools for 3 common game bugs (canvas blank, JS error, layout shift). Source: https://boundev.ai/blog/game-ux-design-guide-2026

## CATEGORY APPENDIX: R123 New Test Cases (v1.29.0)

### Browser Security — Firefox 150 "Nova" Major Redesign Compatibility
- [B-054] **[P1]** Firefox 150 "Nova" major 5-year redesign compatibility — Firefox 150 (released June 2026) is the biggest redesign in 5 years: enhanced privacy tools visibility, redesigned settings, AI "kill switch" for all AI features, and 9% page load speed improvement. Mozilla confirmed the redesign affects how extensions interact with page content and how CSS is parsed in certain edge cases. **GameZipper relevance**: 10-15% of GameZipper users run Firefox; a major engine change risks rendering regressions on game pages. Test: (a) run Top 30 games on Firefox 150+ and verify zero new console errors; (b) verify canvas rendering is identical to Firefox 149 (screenshot diff); (c) confirm localStorage persistence after Firefox upgrade (save games intact); (d) verify game-footer.js and monetag-manager.js load correctly in redesigned Firefox; (e) check that AI kill switch doesn't break any game AI features or chatbot integrations. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_2136a0fdb1134152

### Performance — WebGPU 22% Frame Rate Stability Benchmark (IDC 2026 Q1)
- [P-037] **[P1]** WebGPU production benchmarks — 22% frame rate stability improvement over WebGL — IDC 2026 Q1 Mobile Game Rendering Report: WebGPU apps show 22% improvement in frame rate stability, achieving stable 60fps vs WebGL's 45fps at equivalent visual quality. 18%+ of mobile games now experimenting with WebGPU pipelines. 67.3% of players prefer apps supporting high frame rates and visual effects. **GameZipper relevance**: With 70%+ mobile traffic and WebGPU Baseline 2026, GameZipper should establish migration priority by game category. Test: (a) benchmark Top 10 canvas-heavy games on WebGPU vs WebGL measuring FPS, frame time variance, and jank rate; (b) classify games into migration tiers: Tier 1 (immediate benefit — particle/physics-heavy), Tier 2 (moderate benefit — 2D canvas games), Tier 3 (no benefit — DOM-based games); (c) verify WebGPU → WebGL2 fallback maintains playable FPS (>30fps) on unsupported devices; (d) measure battery drain: WebGPU vs WebGL for 5-minute sessions on Pixel 6a. Source: https://www.lcxw.cn/39@wiki/ZoCGH (IDC 2026 Q1 Mobile Game Rendering Report)

### Compatibility — Unity 6 WebGPU Export Nginx Black Screen Fallback
- [C-059] **[P2]** Unity 6 WebGPU export Nginx black screen — Unity 6 WebGPU exports cause black screen when served via Nginx (reverts to WebGL 2.0 but fails silently). Firefox Nightly crashes on WebGPU content. This is a known rendering pipeline failure pattern affecting any WebGPU game served from Nginx/CDN infrastructure. **GameZipper relevance**: GameZipper serves games via Cloudflare CDN (Nginx-backed). If any future games use Unity 6 WebGPU export, this black screen bug will affect all users. Test: (a) verify existing games render correctly on Nginx-served pages; (b) if adding Unity 6 WebGPU games, test with Nginx server configuration — check MIME types for .wgpu files, verify response headers; (c) test Firefox Nightly for WebGPU crash on game pages; (d) verify WebGL 2.0 fallback actually renders (not just fails silently to black canvas); (e) establish pre-deployment WebGPU rendering checklist for new games. Source: https://developer.unity.cn/ask/question/6778d29fedbc2a001efec7c3

### Website — Firefox 115 ESR Legacy Browser Support Window
- [W-126] **[P2]** Firefox 115 ESR legacy browser support extended to August 2026 — Mozilla extended Firefox 115 ESR support for Windows 7/8.1 and macOS 10.12-10.14 through August 2026. Users on these legacy OS/browser combinations will receive security updates but no new web platform features. **GameZipper relevance**: Small but non-zero segment of users on Firefox 115 ESR (educational/enterprise environments). Game pages must remain functional on Firefox 115 without relying on post-115 APIs. Test: (a) run Top 30 games on Firefox 115 ESR and verify zero console errors; (b) check for use of APIs not available in Firefox 115 (e.g., View Transitions, Dialog element, CSS container queries); (c) verify all polyfills load correctly for missing APIs; (d) plan end-of-support date: after August 2026, Firefox 115 ESR users should see graceful degradation message. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_66969b2122136352

### Security — Rapid CVE Weaponization 20-Hour Exploit Window
- [S-057] **[P2]** Rapid CVE weaponization — 20-hour exploit window — Langflow CVE-2026-33017 (CVSS 9.3) was exploited in the wild within 20 hours of public disclosure. Docker CVE-2026-34040 (CVSS 8.8) AuthZ bypass also rapidly weaponized. FIRST predicts 2026 total CVEs may reach 100K+ (median ~59K). The window between CVE disclosure and in-the-wild exploitation is shrinking dramatically. **GameZipper relevance**: GameZipper's dependency stack (Playwright, Node.js, Cloudflare Workers, etc.) must be monitored and updated within 24 hours for critical CVEs. Test: (a) establish automated CVE monitoring for all GameZipper dependencies (npm audit, Snyk, Dependabot); (b) set 24-hour SLA for patching critical (CVSS ≥9.0) dependency CVEs; (c) verify dependency lock files are current (no known critical CVEs in production); (d) document rollback procedure for failed dependency updates. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_63969d701e048852 + FIRST 2026 CVE projections

### Security — AI Anti-Detect Browser Fraud Detection
- [S-058] **[P2]** AI anti-detect browsers for ad fraud — 2026 anti-detect browser market (RoxyBrowser ISO 27001 certified, AdsPower) increasingly features AI Agent capabilities for automated multi-account management and fingerprint spoofing. Dual-use tools: legitimate e-commerce management + ad fraud click farms. **GameZipper relevance**: Monetag ad revenue could be impacted by IVT (Invalid Traffic) from anti-detect browsers simulating real game interactions. Test: (a) evaluate Monetag's IVT detection against anti-detect browser fingerprints; (b) analyze traffic patterns for telltale anti-detect signals (uniform session duration, identical device profiles, no natural browsing patterns); (c) establish baseline IVT rate per game category; (d) flag games where IVT rate exceeds 15% threshold for manual review. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_56569de3f5d87052 + https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_86369ddf17966652

### Accessibility — MDN Mobile Accessibility Checklist March 2026
- [A-018] **[P1]** MDN Mobile Accessibility Checklist (updated March 2026) — comprehensive mobile accessibility requirements for web games: contrast ratios 4.5:1 normal text / 3:1 large text, ARIA roles required for non-standard game controls (custom buttons, sliders, joysticks), logical focus order for keyboard navigation, content visibility rules (zero opacity + z-index hiding prohibited as sole visibility method). W3C Mobile Accessibility Task Force actively maintaining guidance (147 commits). **GameZipper relevance**: Mobile accounts for 70%+ of traffic; WCAG 2.2 mobile requirements are enforceable under US Title II and EU EAA. Test: (a) audit Top 30 games for contrast ratio compliance (4.5:1 for game text, 3:1 for large HUD elements); (b) verify custom game controls have appropriate ARIA roles (`role="button"`, `role="slider"`, `aria-label`); (c) check keyboard focus order follows logical game flow (menu → game → controls → exit); (d) verify no game elements use `opacity:0` + `z-index` as sole visibility method (must use visually-hidden pattern); (e) test with mobile screen reader (TalkBack/VoiceOver) for game state announcements. Source: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/Guides/Mobile_accessibility_checklist + https://w3c.github.io/Mobile-A11y-TF-Note/

### Compatibility — Phaser.js AUTO Rendering Mode WebGL+Canvas Dual Fallback
- [C-060] **[P2]** Phaser.js AUTO rendering mode — WebGL with automatic Canvas fallback as recommended HTML5 game compatibility pattern. Phaser.js (most popular HTML5 game framework) recommends AUTO mode: attempts WebGL first, silently falls back to Canvas2D if GPU unavailable. Provides maximum device compatibility with zero code changes. **GameZipper relevance**: When evaluating or building new HTML5 games, Phaser AUTO mode should be the recommended rendering approach to avoid WebGL-only black screens on incompatible devices. Test: (a) audit existing Phaser.js games for rendering mode (WEBGL vs AUTO vs CANVAS); (b) verify AUTO-mode games render correctly on WebGL-disabled devices; (c) compare AUTO vs WEBGL-only FPS on supported devices; (d) establish Phaser AUTO mode as standard for new game development template. Source: https://cloud.tencent.com/developer/article/1467947 (Phaser.js tutorial June 2026) + Phaser.js official documentation

## CATEGORY APPENDIX: R121 New Test Cases (v1.28.0)

### Browser Security — Chrome Gamepad API UAF CVE-2026-11634
- [B-053] **[P1]** Chrome Gamepad API use-after-free CVE-2026-11634 — Critical UAF vulnerability in Chrome's Gamepad API (May 26, 2026). Directly impacts browser games that use gamepad/controller input via `navigator.getGamepads()`. Exploitation could allow arbitrary code execution via crafted gamepad input events. Patched in Chrome 149.x+. **GameZipper relevance**: Games supporting gamepad input (racing, sports, platformer games) must verify crash-free operation on patched Chrome. Test: (a) enumerate all games using `navigator.getGamepads()` or `gamepadconnected` event; (b) verify no UAF crash when connecting/disconnecting gamepads rapidly during gameplay; (c) verify patched Chrome 149+ shows zero console errors for gamepad-enabled games; (d) confirm gamepad input still works correctly post-patch. Source: https://chromereleases.googleblog.com/2026

### Compatibility — AI-Adaptive Testing with Plain-Language Flows
- [C-057] **[P2]** AI-Adaptive Testing — replacing rigid scripted automation with plain-language test flows (HeadSpin ACE 2026). New AI-driven testing tools define test flows in natural language and execute them based on current application state, adjusting dynamically when UI elements change. Eliminates brittle selector-based scripts. **GameZipper relevance**: Current verify-lite.js uses hardcoded DOM selectors that break when game HTML changes. AI-adaptive testing could maintain test coverage across 265+ games without constant selector updates. Test: (a) evaluate HeadSpin ACE or similar AI-adaptive tool for GameZipper QA pipeline; (b) compare flaky-test rate: current verify-lite selector-based vs AI-adaptive approach on Top 30 games; (c) measure test maintenance effort reduction. Source: https://headspin.io/blog/future-of-game-testing

### Performance — WebGPU 70-82% Coverage with WebGL2 Graceful Fallback
- [P-035] **[P1]** WebGPU global browser coverage 70-82% (June 2026) — all major browsers now ship WebGPU (Chrome 113+, Edge, Firefox 147+, Safari 26+). Migration is a two-line code change for Three.js/Babylon.js with automatic WebGL2 fallback. 15-30x performance gains for compute workloads; 10x faster scene rendering. Challenges: ~45% of older devices lack storage buffers in vertex shaders; GPU vendor-specific driver bugs (NVIDIA 572.xx crashes, AMD artifacts, Intel driver hangs). **GameZipper relevance**: WebGL games that could benefit from WebGPU migration should be identified and tested with fallback paths. Test: (a) audit all canvas-heavy games for WebGPU migration readiness; (b) verify WebGL2 fallback works on browsers without WebGPU support; (c) test games with WebGPU renderer on Chrome/Edge/Firefox/Safari for driver-specific bugs; (d) measure FPS improvement on WebGPU vs WebGL for Top 10 canvas-heavy games. Source: https://byteiota.com/webgpu-2026-70-browser-support-15x-performance-gains + https://programming-helper.com/tech/webgpu-2026-next-generation-browser-graphics-api

### Security — AI-Generated Fake Vulnerability Reports Flooding Bounty Programs
- [S-056] **[P2]** AI-generated fake vulnerability reports — Google restructured its Vulnerability Reward Program (VRP) in May 2026 due to flood of AI-generated fake bug reports. Node.js and cURL paused their bounty programs entirely. Growing meta-security concern: AI can generate plausible-looking vulnerability reports at scale, overwhelming security teams. **GameZipper relevance**: If GameZipper runs any bug bounty or responsible disclosure program, AI-generated spam reports could waste engineering time. Test: (a) establish triage criteria for distinguishing real vs AI-generated vulnerability reports; (b) require PoC exploit code or video evidence for submitted reports; (c) monitor for AI-generated reports targeting game page endpoints; (d) verify that automated security scanners (dependency scanning, CSP auditors) are not themselves AI-generated false positives. Source: https://googleprojectzero.blogspot.com (VRP restructuring May 2026)

### Accessibility — WCAG 3.0 Outcomes-Based Scoring Forward-Compatibility
- [A-017] **[P2]** WCAG 3.0 Working Draft (March 2026) forward-compatibility — WCAG 3.0 introduces outcomes-based scoring that replaces A/AA/AAA levels with a 0-4 point scoring system. Fundamentally changes how web games measure accessibility compliance. Not yet finalized but early preparation reduces future migration cost. **GameZipper relevance**: Current WCAG 2.2 AA compliance tests (A-001 through A-016) will need scoring recalculation under WCAG 3.0 outcomes model. Test: (a) review all 17 accessibility test cases (A-001 to A-016) for WCAG 3.0 scoring compatibility; (b) identify which tests would score differently under outcomes model; (c) prepare scoring conversion matrix: WCAG 2.2 AA → WCAG 3.0 outcome points; (d) flag any test that would lose points under new model. Source: https://thewcag.com/wcag-3-0

### Compatibility — Chrome DevTools for Agents Automated Debugging
- [C-058] **[P2]** Chrome DevTools for Agents — announced at Chrome I/O 2026, provides automated agents direct access to console logs, network traffic, and accessibility trees for debugging and verification. LY Corporation already uses it to reduce manual analysis by 96-98%. Potential to fully automate GameZipper QA verification. **GameZipper relevance**: Current QA pipeline uses Kachilu + Playwright for browser testing. DevTools for Agents could replace or supplement both with richer data access (network waterfall, console grouping, a11y tree). Test: (a) evaluate Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) agent integration for GameZipper verify-lite; (b) compare coverage: current verify-lite 30 checks/game vs CDP agent (console + network + a11y + performance); (c) pilot on 5 games measuring reduction in false-positive rate; (d) benchmark: CDP agent time-per-game vs Kachilu time-per-game. Source: https://developer.chrome.com/blog/chrome-at-io26

### Website — Chrome/Edge v148 AI Sidebar Game Content Interaction
- [W-125] **[P1]** Chrome/Edge v148 AI sidebar (Mariner/Gemini) interaction with game content — Chrome 148 and Edge 148 embed AI assistants (Mariner, Gemini) that can analyze, summarize, and interact with page content via sidebar overlays. AI circle-search can highlight and interact with game elements. These AI overlays may interfere with game canvas interactions, capture game content, or conflict with game overlays. **GameZipper relevance**: AI sidebar overlays add a new layer of potential interaction conflicts with game UI (similar to z-index topnav conflict pattern #14). Test: (a) verify game pages render correctly with Chrome/Edge AI sidebar open; (b) verify AI sidebar doesn't intercept click/touch events meant for game canvas; (c) verify AI circle-search doesn't interfere with canvas click coordinates; (d) check if AI sidebar reads game content correctly (accessibility implications); (e) verify game overlays (splash, modals, ads) don't conflict with AI sidebar z-index. Source: https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_379697abb5516352 + https://so.html5.qq.com/page/real/search_news?docid=70000021_41069d589fc29252

### Performance — PerfDog 200+ Mobile Performance Metrics for Casual Games
- [P-036] **[P2]** PerfDog 200+ mobile performance metrics — comprehensive mobile game performance testing methodology (WeTest 2026 guide) targeting 120FPS optimization, covering FPS, Jank (frame stutter), Smooth Index, FPower (frame power), battery drain, thermal throttling, memory pressure, and network latency. Sets new benchmark for casual mobile game performance expectations. **GameZipper relevance**: 70%+ of GameZipper traffic is mobile. Current performance testing (P-001 to P-034) lacks Jank/Smooth Index/thermal metrics. Test: (a) measure Jank rate for Top 30 games on mid-range Android (Pixel 6a level) — target <5% Jank; (b) measure Smooth Index — target >95 for casual games; (c) profile battery drain for 5-minute gameplay sessions — target <2% per session; (d) monitor thermal throttling on extended play (10+ minutes) — flag games where FPS drops >20% due to thermal; (e) compare PerfDog metrics with existing Playwright performance measurements. Source: https://wetest.net/blog/mobile-game-performance-testing-2026-perfdog-guide-1189.html

US Title II enforcement April 2026 + EU EAA 2026. Test: audit all game buttons, controls, and interactive elements; flag any target < 24×24px without spacing. Relevant for identifying WebGL games that should migrate. Test: benchmark Top 10 canvas-heavy games measuring draw-call count; flag games with > 500 draw calls as WebGPU migration candidates.
