Source: ghacks.net/category/browsers. **Test**: 1) Identify any game/feature using Edge credential vault (search for `Passwords_Vault`, `Edge master password`, `Windows.Security.Credentials`); 2) Verify post-removal Edge build (post June-4-2026) still allows login on affected features; 3) Confirm WebAuthn / passkey fallback works on Edge; 4) Verify localStorage-only fallback for non-WebAuthn browsers doesn't leak credentials to extension sandbox; 5) Add user-visible warning if old auth path was removed and data must be re-entered.

### 26.2 New Platform APIs + Performance Gates + Accessibility
- [C-095] **[P1] Google I/O 2026 HTML-in-Canvas API — accessibility unlock for canvas-only games** — New web platform feature announced at Google I/O 2026 (Chrome engineer Thomas Nattestad) lets developers render HTML elements directly into canvas, WebGL, and WebGPU contexts while preserving accessibility and interactivity. Canvas-only games can now expose accessible HTML menus/HUDs to screen readers. Source: webgpu.com/news/google-html-in-canvas-webgl-webgpu. **Test**: 1) Identify all canvas-only game pages where `<canvas>` covers the full viewport and no DOM overlay exists (e.g., games without a separate `<div>` HUD); 2) For each, audit with `axe-core` or Lighthouse Accessibility — currently Fails because screen readers see only the canvas element; 3) Pilot HTML-in-Canvas on one game (e.g., a puzzle with a HUD score/timer) behind `chrome://flags#enable-html-in-canvas`; 4) Verify screen reader (VoiceOver/NVDA) can read the rendered HTML inside canvas post-API; 5) Document fallback for browsers without the API (Safari/Firefox) — keep DOM overlay as default, switch to HTML-in-Canvas only when `('HTMLCanvasElement' in window && 'renderHTML' in HTMLCanvasElement.prototype)`.
- [P-063] **[P0] INP (Interaction to Next Paint) firm gate — HTML5 game-loop 200ms p75 responsiveness** — INP replaced FID as a Core Web Vital in March 2024; by 2026 it is firmly the responsiveness gate. HTML5 games with continuous rAF loops are at high risk of INP > 500ms (Poor) because the rAF callback blocks the next paint. Source: digitalapplied.com/blog/core-web-vitals-2026-inp-lcp-cls-optimization-guide, farooxium.dev/blog/core-web-vitals-performance-2026. **Test**: 1) Open Chrome DevTools → Performance → record a 30s gameplay session (drag/keyboard/click); 2) Measure INP for each interaction (`performance.measureUserAgentSpecificMemory` + `PerformanceObserver({type:'event'})`); 3) Verify p75 INP ≤ 200ms (Good) on top-30 games; 4) If any game fails: profile the rAF callback — chunk long-running logic (e.g., physics step) into ≤16ms slices, defer non-critical work with `requestIdleCallback`, or move heavy compute to Web Worker; 5) Re-verify p75 INP ≤ 200ms after fix on mobile Chrome (mid-tier Android) where CPU is slower.
- [A-029] **[P0] WCAG 2.2 SC 3.3.9 Accessible Authentication (No-Learning) — game login/rewards pages must NOT require cognitive-function test** — WCAG 2.2 Level AAA SC 3.3.9 prohibits requiring a cognitive function test (e.g., memorize/recall password, solve puzzle, recognize object) for any step in authentication, unless an alternative or memory aid is provided. Game pages with login forms, rewards redemption, or account creation must support passwordless auth (WebAuthn/passkey) AND allow copy-paste. Source: dequeuniversity.com/resources/wcag-2.2, w3.org/TR/WCAG22, abilitynet.org.uk/factsheets/what-you-need-know-about-wcag-22. **Test**: 1) List all auth forms on gamezipper (login, account creation, rewards redemption, comment forms); 2) For each, attempt to paste into the password field — verify `onpaste` not blocked, no `autocomplete="off"` on password; 3) Verify password manager autofill works (1Password/Bitwarden); 4) Provide a WebAuthn/passkey option alongside password (AAA compliance); 5) If a CAPTCHA exists, ensure audio CAPTCHA or hCaptcha accessibility mode is offered (recognition-test alt path).

### 26.3 Privacy Sandbox Deprecation + WebGPU Migration + Safari Regression + Mobile Haptics
- [S-103] **[P0] Privacy Sandbox killed Oct 2025 + 3P cookies now opt-in — explicit user consent for cross-site tracking** — Per AdExchanger Oct 2025, Google pulled the plug on Topics API, PAAPI, and other major Privacy Sandbox APIs after CMA sign-off; third-party cookies in Chrome now remain functional but require explicit user opt-in (user-choice prompt). Any cross-site ad/analytics tracking on game pages that previously relied on Privacy Sandbox APIs must migrate to consent-based or first-party solutions. Source: adexchanger.com/privacy/google-pulls-the-plug-on-topics-paapi, groas.com/post/chrome-third-party-cookies-google-ads-2026-targeting-measurement-protection. **Test**: 1) Audit game pages for any reference to Topics API (`document.browsingTopics`), PAAPI (`runAdAuction`), or related Privacy Sandbox APIs — should return 0; 2) Audit Monetag/ad SDK calls — verify no silent 3P cookie reads; 3) Confirm any cross-site tracking is gated behind a user-visible consent UI ("Allow personalized ads?" Yes/No); 4) Verify first-party analytics endpoint (gamezipper.com origin) doesn't trigger cross-site cookie warning in Chrome DevTools → Application → Cookies; 5) Test in Chrome with 3P cookies explicitly disabled (Settings → Privacy → Block third-party cookies) — game pages must still function.
- [G-136] **[P1] WebGL → WebGPU migration graceful-fallback audit — navigator.gpu undefined no-crash** — Per PlayCanvas engine 2026 push and WebGPU/WASM maturation, more games will migrate from WebGL to WebGPU for compute/perf. But many browsers lack WebGPU (Safari iOS < 26, older Firefox, enterprise Edge). Any game using WebGPU must gracefully fall back to WebGL2 with no console error/crash. Source: webgpu.com, playcanvas.com, developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WebGPU_API. **Test**: 1) For each game using WebGPU, set up a Playwright test that sets `navigator.gpu = undefined` before page load; 2) Verify the game loads and plays via WebGL2 fallback (no `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined`); 3) Confirm console shows fallback message (e.g., "WebGPU unavailable, falling back to WebGL2") not a stack trace; 4) Test on Firefox stable (WebGPU behind `about:config` flag) — verify default-off path; 5) Test on iOS Safari 17 (no WebGPU) — game must render via WebGL2 without broken visuals.
- [W-145] **[P2] Safari 27 box-shadow on table-row regression sweep — WebKit blog WWDC26 fix verification** — WebKit's WWDC26 blog post (News from WWDC26: WebKit in Safari 27 beta) confirms Safari 27 fixes specific rendering bugs including `box-shadow` rendering correctly on table-row (`<tr>`) elements, `aspect-ratio` resolving against percentage height, and `background-clip: text` on table headers. Safari 26 had broken `<tr>` + box-shadow rendering — leaderboards/scoreboards using this pattern must be re-verified. Source: webkit.org/blog/17967/news-from-wwdc26-webkit-in-safari-27-beta. **Test**: 1) Identify game pages with `<table>` + `<tr style="box-shadow:...">` patterns (likely leaderboards, score tables, standings); 2) Open each in Safari 26 (Safari 26.4 or earlier) and capture screenshot — broken shadow rendering expected; 3) Open in Safari 27 beta (or Safari Technology Preview 245+) — verify shadow renders correctly; 4) Verify no regression in Safari 27 (shadows don't disappear entirely); 5) Also audit `aspect-ratio` + percentage-height and `background-clip:text` + `<th>` patterns since those were co-fixed.
- [GP-107] **[P2] Mobile haptic feedback navigator.vibrate() — 2026 game UX standard for action events** — Per 2026 game UX trend reports (Hi3D, UXPin), haptic feedback is now an expected mobile game feel standard — players expect vibration on collisions, level-ups, errors, and game-over. HTML5 games can implement via `navigator.vibrate()`. Source: hi3d.ai/blog/en-The-Future-of-Game-Design-10-Trends, uxpin.com/studio/blog/game-ux, vrunik.com/ux-for-gaming. **Test**: 1) For each game with collision/level-up/error/game-over events, audit whether `navigator.vibrate()` is called (search `'navigator.vibrate'` or `'window.navigator.vibrate'`); 2) On Android Chrome, verify vibration triggers on test events (requires device with vibration motor; headless can't test); 3) On iOS Safari (Vibration API unsupported since iOS), verify silent no-op — game must not crash or log error on `navigator.vibrate()` call; 4) Wrap calls in feature detection: `if (navigator.vibrate) navigator.vibrate(50)`; 5) Add a settings toggle "Haptic feedback" so users can disable (some users find vibration annoying or have vestibular sensitivity).


---

## CATEGORY 27: 2026 MID-JUNE PLAYWRIGHT NATIVE AGENTS + BROWSER STRESS TOOLS + WEBGPU BACKGROUND-TAB DEVICE-LOSS + FTC ENFORCEMENT + NPU AI (R177 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

### 27.1 Playwright Test Agents — Official Native Self-Healing Test Loop
- [C-096] **[P2]** Playwright Test Agents (planner/generator/healer) — official native self-healing test loop — Playwright's official docs (playwright.dev/docs/test-agents) ship **three built-in agents out-of-the-box** that form an agentic loop replacing brittle selector maintenance: (a) 🎭 **planner** — explores the running app and produces a Markdown test plan (semantic intent + flow) without writing code; (b) 🎭 **generator** — converts the Markdown plan into runnable Playwright TypeScript test code; (c) 🎭 **healer** — when a test fails after a UI refactor (selector drift, DOM restructure), it re-reads the page, locates the equivalent element, and rewrites the failing step. The agents can run independently, sequentially (planner→generator→healer = full coverage production), or as a chained agentic loop. **Distinct from C-087** (Playwright MCP — exposes accessibility-tree snapshots to an EXTERNAL AI agent like Claude Code via Model Context Protocol — external-orchestrator pattern) and **C-092** (Codex CLI custom agent loop using MCP for exploration + native codegen for test writing — a CUSTOM agent architecture). This is the **native first-party** Playwright agent set, no MCP integration required, ships with `npx playwright`. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) replace the hardcoded-selector `verify-lite.js` scripts with a Playwright Test Agents run on Top 30 games — pipeline: `planner` explores each game and emits a per-game Markdown plan (load, click Play, win level 1, etc.); `generator` produces TypeScript tests; run tests; (2) inject a deliberate UI change (rename `#play-btn` → `#start-game`) and verify the `healer` agent re-locates the element and rewrites the failing step within one pass — measure selector-fragility recovery rate across 30 games; (3) benchmark token cost vs the Codex CLI loop (C-092) — native agents should be cheaper (no external LLM round-trips for routine healing); (4) document which game types (canvas-only vs DOM-driven) the planner agent struggles to explore (canvas-only games have no accessibility tree for the planner to reason about — pair with C-088 VLM agents); (5) standardize on a 2-tier regression pipeline: native agents (C-096) for DOM-driven games, VLM agents (C-088) for canvas-only games. Source: https://playwright.dev/docs/test-agents + https://testdino.com/blog/playwright-ai-ecosystem + https://testleaf.com/blog/playwright-mcp-ai-test-automation-2026

### 27.2 RAMpage Browser Stress Suite — 5-Vector Per-Tab Hard-Limit Reproduction
- [P-064] **[P2]** RAMpage browser stress suite — 5-vector (RAM/CPU/GPU/Storage/Network) per-tab hard-limit reproduction — thecapibara/RAMpage (open-source GitHub project) is a comprehensive browser-based **system stress suite** evolved from a simple memory-eater into a five-vector test harness: (a) RAM allocation, (b) CPU concurrency, (c) GPU rendering (WebGL shaders), (d) Storage quotas (IndexedDB), (e) Network bandwidth. It uses Web Workers + WebGL shaders + IndexedDB to generate maximum load while keeping the main thread responsive for measurement. **Distinct from P-059** (browser per-tab hard memory cap *behavioral pattern* documented from the Llamas-on-the-Web paper) — this is the **reproduction TOOL**: instead of waiting weeks for a long-session game to organically hit a per-tab cap, RAMpage forces the limit in minutes, making per-tab-crash recovery testable on demand. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) install RAMpage as a sibling tab to each Top 10 long-session game (idle games, endless runners, match-3 endless mode) and progressively consume RAM via the suite — log at what allocation level the game tab is killed or its rAF paused (per-tab cap discovery); (2) verify each game's `beforeunload` handler saves state before the OS/browser kills the tab (so a crashed session resumes from last save); (3) use the GPU vector to confirm WebGPU/WebGL games handle device-lost events (synergy with P-061, P-065) when VRAM is exhausted; (4) use the Storage vector to verify IndexedDB-quota-exceeded handling in games that persist save data client-side — game must show "save failed — clearing browser storage" prompt, not silently lose progress; (5) use the Network vector to verify games degrade gracefully when bandwidth drops to 0 (offline mode, cached assets). **Caveat**: RAMpage is a development/QA tool, not for production — running it on real user tabs could crash other tabs (use isolated browser profile or VM). Source: https://github.com/thecapibara/RAMpage

### 27.3 WebGPU Device Lost — Background-Tab GPU Memory Exhaustion Specific Root Cause
- [P-065] **[P1]** WebGPU Device Lost from background-tab GPU memory exhaustion — specific root cause (not general crash) — superchargebrowser.com's WebGPU Device-Lost fix library documents a **specific, high-frequency root cause** for the GPUDevice.lost events tracked generally in P-061: **background tabs competing for VRAM exhaust the GPU, and Chrome kills the foreground-game tab's WebGPU device to reclaim memory**. The foreground tab has no warning — the `device.lost` Promise resolves with `reason: 'destroyed'` (system-initiated) or sometimes `reason: 'unknown'`, and the game canvas goes black. This is a **different bug class** from P-061 (general device-lost handling) and P-059 (per-tab RAM cap): it is **GPU-memory-specific** and **background-tab-triggered**, meaning the player's own actions in the game tab did not cause it. Common reproduction: (1) player has 8+ tabs open including a heavy WebGPU game; (2) one of the background tabs (YouTube, another game, a data-viz dashboard) spikes VRAM; (3) Chrome's GPU process reclaims memory by destroying the foreground-game's WebGPU device. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) on a real Chrome desktop with discrete GPU (8GB VRAM typical), open Top 10 WebGPU games in 8 simultaneous tabs + 2 YouTube 4K tabs + a Three.js playground — verify each game's `device.lost` handler fires and the game attempts adapter re-init (not silent black canvas); (2) for games that DO recover, measure recovery time (target <3s to first re-rendered frame after device-lost); (3) for games that DON'T recover, ship a `device.lost` handler that: (a) clears the canvas with a "GPU reset — reloading…" overlay, (b) requests a fresh adapter + device, (c) re-uploads all textures/buffers, (d) resets the render loop — verify this restores the game without a full page reload; (4) recommend `requestAdapter({ powerPreference: 'low-power' })` for casual games that don't need peak GPU — reduces the game's VRAM footprint and lowers the chance of being the tab Chrome kills; (5) document per-game VRAM usage (via `chrome://gpu` → "Memory") and flag any game >500MB VRAM as high background-kill risk. Source: https://superchargebrowser.com/library/fix-webgpu-device-lost-chrome + https://github.com/gpuweb/gpuweb/blob/main/design/ErrorHandling.md

### 27.4 FTC Honda Dark-Pattern Precedent + Epic $245M Enforcement Benchmark
- [W-146] **[P1]** FTC Honda dark-pattern precedent (first major non-tech ruling) + Epic Games $245M settlement enforcement benchmark — ketch.com ("Are Dark Patterns Illegal in 2026? Honda, the Law, and UX Loopholes") documents that **Honda became the first major non-tech company hit with a dark-pattern enforcement action**, expanding the doctrine beyond pure software/tech to any consumer-facing UX with deceptive design. Concurrently, the **Epic Games $245M FTC settlement** (cybersecasia.net, March 2025) for dark patterns in Fortnite (tricking users including children into unwanted purchases + dark-pattern refund obstruction) sets the **concrete enforcement benchmark**: deceptive UX in games can trigger nine-figure penalties under FTC Section 5 (unfair/deceptive acts). Reed Smith (reedsmith.com) and pandectes.io ("Dark Patterns in 2026: What the FTC's New Rules Mean") confirm the FTC has made dark patterns a **central 2026 enforcement priority**, with action types: (a) pre-checked boxes that opt users into purchases/data-sharing; (b) artificial urgency (countdown timers on fake-limited offers); (c) confirm-shaming ("No thanks, I don't want free coins"); (d) hard-to-cancel subscriptions; (e) visual-prominence asymmetry (accept-button large+green, reject-button tiny+gray). **Distinct from W-142** (EU Digital Fairness Act — *regulatory prohibition* in EU) and **W-143** (US addiction lawsuit wave — *civil tort* theory) — this is the **FTC enforcement + Honda precedent** dimension: regulator-issued penalties with concrete dollar amounts and the doctrine now extending beyond tech to any consumer UX. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) run a full UX audit using the secureprivacy.ai 2026 Dark Pattern Avoidance Checklist (W-147) and pandectes.io FTC-rule mapping across all game pages + Monetag/AdSense consent flows; (2) verify reject-button parity: on every consent UI (cookie, ads, newsletter), the "reject" / "no thanks" button must be the **same size, same color, same vertical position, same tap-target area** as the "accept" button — measure pixel dimensions and flag any asymmetry; (3) scan all on-site copy for artificial-urgency language ("Limited time!", "Only X left!", "Hurry!") and confirm-shaming copy — flag and rewrite each; (4) verify no pre-checked consent boxes exist anywhere (newsletter signup, ad-personalization, cookie categories); (5) for any monetization path (rewarded video, IAP-if-added), verify the purchase path is symmetric with the free-play path — the "watch ad to continue" button must not visually dwarf the "continue without ad" button; (6) document the GameZipper FTC-compliance posture in the test matrix and re-audit quarterly. Source: https://ketch.com/blog/posts/dark-patterns-are-they-illegal + https://cybersecasia.net/newsletter/us-ftc-targets-dark-patterns-in-tech-firms-deceptive-online-practices + https://pandectes.io/blog/dark-patterns-in-2026-what-the-ftcs-new-rules-mean + https://reedsmith.com/articles/dark-patterns-lead-to-enforcement-spotlight-key-compliance-steps-for-businesses

### 27.5 WebAI Compute Benchmark — Cross-Browser WebGPU/WASM/WebNN Baseline
- [C-097] **[P3]** Google Chrome team WebAI Compute Benchmark — cross-browser WebGPU/WASM/WebNN performance baselining for AI-enabled games — github.com/GoogleChrome/webai-compute-benchmark is the **official Chrome team open-source benchmark suite** for AI workloads on the web, designed to evaluate the performance of WebAssembly (Wasm), WebGPU, and (in future) WebNN or other underlying technologies across browsers and JS/WASM runtimes. It contains "interesting AI workloads" — not synthetic micro-benchmarks but real inference tasks (image classification, object detection, NLP) representative of what AI-enabled games would actually run (NPC behavior models, voice commands, image recognition for AR/camera games). **Distinct from P-046** (general WebGPU vs WebGL migration benchmark) and **PC-014** (WebGPU+WebNN dual-API feature test) — this is the **official Chrome-team baselining tool** for AI compute specifically, useful for: (a) setting a minimum-hardware floor for any AI game feature, (b) cross-browser regression testing when Chrome/Safari/Firefox ship WebGPU/WebNN changes, (c) tuning AI model size/complexity to fit the casual-game hardware envelope (mid-range Android, school Chromebooks). **Test for GameZipper**: (1) for any game with on-device AI features (inference via WebGPU/WebNN — NPC behavior, voice, image recognition, adaptive difficulty), baseline the model's inference latency on the WebAI Compute Benchmark's reference workloads across Chrome/Safari/Firefox — establish a per-browser p95 latency budget; (2) verify the game's actual AI inference latency is within 20% of the WebAI Compute Benchmark's reference for the same model class — large deviation indicates suboptimal shader/binding usage; (3) use the benchmark to set the minimum-hardware floor: if a model needs >100ms inference on the benchmark's lowest-end device, do not ship that AI feature to that device tier (graceful disable); (4) re-run the benchmark after every Chrome/Safari/Firefox WebGPU/WebNN release to catch silent regressions (a browser update can drop inference perf 30%+ without warning). Source: https://github.com/GoogleChrome/webai-compute-benchmark

### 27.6 WebNN NPU Acceleration — 50x Speedup on Dedicated NPU
- [PC-016] **[P2]** WebNN NPU acceleration — 50x speedup vs CPU on dedicated NPU (Windows 11 24H2+) for AI game features — ddevtools.com's January 2026 WebGPU/WebNN update documents the platform-compatibility matrix for **on-device AI acceleration** via the Web Neural Network API (WebNN, `navigator.ml`): (a) **WebGPU** taps into the GPU's general compute pipeline — typical speedup is ~10x vs CPU baseline; (b) **WebNN on a dedicated NPU** (Intel NPU, Qualcomm AI Engine, Apple Neural Engine) reaches **~50x speedup vs CPU** because the NPU is purpose-built for matrix-multiply (the core op of neural inference). Critical compatibility caveats: WebNN NPU support requires **Windows 11 24H2+** with specific hardware flags enabled, and Microsoft's WebNN docs confirm GPU and NPU support remain in **preview** — full support requires specific browser+OS+driver combinations. **Distinct from PC-014** (WebGPU+WebNN dual-API feature test — confirms the game can route to either API) and **B-089** (Safari/WebKit ONNX Runtime regression) — this is the **performance-tier** test: a game's AI feature must detect the best available accelerator and route to it (NPU > GPU > CPU), not just check whether WebNN exists. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) for any game with on-device AI features, implement accelerator-tier detection: `if (navigator.ml && canUseNPU()) useNPU(); else if (navigator.gpu) useWebGPU(); else fallback CPU;` — verify each tier is exercised; (2) on a Windows 11 24H2+ device with Intel Core Ultra (NPU present), verify the game's AI inference routes to the NPU and benchmark latency vs WebGPU-only path — confirm the 5x-10x NPU-vs-GPU speedup holds; (3) on devices without NPU (older Windows, most Macs without Apple Neural Engine access via WebNN, Linux), verify the game falls back to WebGPU without error and without silent CPU-only fallback (which would be 10x slower than expected); (4) document the OS+browser+hardware matrix for NPU support and gate AI features behind a feature-detect (`'ml' in navigator && navigator.ml.computeSync(...)`) not a UA-string sniff; (5) for games with real-time AI (NPC behavior in a game loop), verify the NPU path doesn't starve the render loop — NPU compute and GPU render share thermal budget on thin-and-light laptops. Source: https://ddevtools.com/updates/2026-01-webgpu-webnn-browser-ai + https://letket.com/high-performance-web-apps-in-2026-webassembly-webgpu-and-edge-architectures

### 27.7 WebGPU "Break on Error" DevTools Option — Standard Debug Protocol
- [C-098] **[P3]** WebGPU "break on error" DevTools option — standard debug protocol per official gpuweb design — The official WebGPU error-handling design doc (github.com/gpuweb/gpuweb/blob/main/design/ErrorHandling.md) specifies that **implementations should provide a way to enable synchronous validation, for example via a "break on WebGPU error" option in the developer tools**, with overhead low enough that applications can still run while being debugged. This is the WebGPU equivalent of JS `debugger;` for graphics bugs — without it, WebGPU errors surface only via asynchronous `GPUDevice.lost` (P-061/P-065) or uncaptured-error events (post-fact, often with vague messages), making root-cause analysis extremely slow. **Distinct from C-096** (Playwright test agents for DOM-driven games) and **C-094** (browser-regression attribution methodology) — this is the **WebGPU-specific debug-protocol** that every WebGPU-game QA cycle must use as a first-pass diagnostic step before reaching for heavier tools. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) document the "break on WebGPU error" DevTools option in the QA playbook — Chrome: DevTools → Sources → "Pause on WebGPU errors" (or `chrome://flags#enable-webgpu-validation`); Safari: Develop → Web Inspector → Graphics → "Break on validation errors"; Firefox: `about:config → dom.webgpu.debug` ; (2) for every WebGPU-game bug report, the FIRST diagnostic step is to enable "break on error" + reproduce — most WebGPU bugs surface as a validation error at a specific draw/dispatch call, pinpointing the offending code in seconds; (3) wrap game WebGPU calls in `GPUDevice.pushErrorScope('validation')` / `popErrorScope()` blocks during development to capture the first validation error per draw call, not just the cumulative uncaptured errors; (4) ship production builds with validation **disabled** (performance overhead is real, ~5-15% per draw) but development/QA builds with validation **enabled** + the DevTools breakpoint set — never debug a WebGPU bug on a production-validated build; (5) verify the game logs validation errors to gz-analytics (or Crashbox C-090) when running with validation enabled in staging — accumulate the error catalog to drive fixes. Source: https://github.com/gpuweb/gpuweb/blob/main/design/ErrorHandling.md + https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/GPUValidationError

### 27.8 2026 Web-Game Tech-Stack Decision Matrix — WebGL vs WebGPU vs WASM Per-Genre
- [G-137] **[P3]** 2026 web-game tech-stack decision matrix — WebGL vs WebGPU vs WASM per-genre pick — app.cinevva.com's "Web Games Tech Stack in 2026" guide cuts through the hype with a **pragmatic decision flowchart** for new projects: the three technologies (WebGL 2.0, WebGPU, WebAssembly) each solve different problems with different trade-offs, and the "right" answer depends on what you're actually building, not what gets the most press. Decision rules distilled: (a) **WebGL 2.0** — works everywhere, handles most 2D puzzle/arcade/card games fine; pick it for broad-compatibility casual games targeting the 18% of users still without WebGPU (synergy with G-136 fallback testing); (b) **WebGPU** — pick it for performance-critical 3D/compute/AI work where the 82% browser-coverage floor is acceptable and you need GPU compute (particle systems, physics, AI inference); (c) **WebAssembly** — pick it when porting a native engine (Unity/Godot/Unreal C++/C#/Rust) where the hot loop needs near-native speed and you've already paid the WASM-payload size cost (synergy with G-133); (d) **WebGL + WASM** — common hybrid: WASM logic + WebGL render for engines like Phaser/PlayCanvas pre-WebGPU. **Distinct from G-130** (Godot 4.5 web export specifics) and **G-133** (WASM engine integration testing) — this is the **architecture-decision** test: before any new game is added to GameZipper, the tech-stack choice should be documented against this matrix, and the test plan adapted to the chosen stack. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) for every new game added to the catalog (292+ current, growing), document the tech-stack choice (WebGL/WebGPU/WASM/hybrid) in the game's metadata (extend `games-data.js` with a `render_stack` field); (2) for each stack tier, define a baseline test suite: WebGL games = canvas-pixel-diff + WebGL-context-lost (B-062); WebGPU games = WebGPU-validation-error (C-098) + device-lost recovery (P-061/P-065); WASM games = WASM-heap-fragmentation soak (P-057); (3) verify the homepage game-card grid groups or filters by render_stack so users on low-end devices can find WebGL-only games (no WebGPU dependency); (4) when a game migrates from WebGL to WebGPU (or vice versa), re-run the FULL test suite for the new stack tier — partial migration leaves half the bug surface uncovered; (5) for the next 10 new games added, fill the decision-matrix template ("target hardware / perf requirements / team expertise / browser-coverage floor") before code is written — catch architecture mistakes before they become runtime bugs. Source: https://app.cinevva.com/guides/web-games-stack-2026.html + https://sarkarishivam.com/blog/html5-vs-flash-vs-webgl + https://dinogame.gg/blog/webgpu-and-browser-games

### 27.9 2026 Dark Pattern Avoidance Checklist — Practical UX Audit Guide
- [W-147] **[P2]** 2026 Dark Pattern Avoidance Checklist — practical UX audit guide for regulatory compliance — secureprivacy.ai's "Dark Pattern Avoidance 2026 Checklist: The Practical Guide for UX Teams" provides a **concrete, audit-ready checklist** mapping each known dark-pattern category to its regulatory prohibition (GDPR/CPRA/DSA/FTC Section 5) and a UX-level detection rule. This complements the **legal/regulatory analysis** of W-142 (EU DFA), W-143 (US addiction lawsuits), and W-146 (FTC Honda/Epic enforcement) with an **operational audit tool** the QA team can run on every game-page and consent-flow change. Key checklist categories: (a) **Forced Action** — making one action a prerequisite for another (must-watch-ad-to-play); (b) **Confirmshaming** — emotionally manipulative opt-out copy ("No thanks, I hate free stuff"); (c) **Sneaking** — hiding terms/charges behind a long scroll or pre-checked box; (d) **Urgency** — fake countdowns / fake scarcity ("Only 3 left!"); (e) **Misdirection** — visual hierarchy steering toward the paid path; (f) **Roach Motel** — easy to enter (subscribe), hard to leave (cancel buried 5 menus deep); (g) **Privacy Zuckering** — tricking users into sharing more data than intended; (h) **Price Comparison Prevention** — hiding the free-path cost in time-vs-money terms. **Distinct from W-146** (FTC enforcement benchmark) — this is the **operational checklist** that operationalizes the enforcement risk into a per-page audit. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) for every game page + the homepage + every Monetag/AdSense consent flow, run the 8-category secureprivacy checklist — output a per-page compliance score (0-8 categories passing) and flag any page scoring <8; (2) for each flagged category, write a specific test case in the QA pipeline that fails on regression (e.g., a test that parses the consent UI HTML and asserts the reject button is in the same `font-size` range as the accept button — confirming no Misdirection asymmetry); (3) for the "Roach Motel" category specifically, verify every consent the user grants has an equally-prominent revoke path (cookie consent, ad-personalization, newsletter); (4) for "Sneaking", scan every consent UI for pre-checked checkboxes — zero tolerance, every checkbox must default to unchecked; (5) for "Urgency", grep all on-site copy for regex matches of urgency patterns (`(limited|hurry|only \d+|expires|last chance|act now)`) — every match must have a verifiable factual basis (a real countdown to a real event) or be removed; (6) re-audit every page on a quarterly cadence and after any major UX change to the homepage, footer, or consent flow. Source: https://secureprivacy.ai/blog/dark-pattern-avoidance-2026-checklist + https://lexology.com/library/detail.aspx?g=6820be01-2cd9-4554-b250-143b9537f9b9 + https://lkslaw.com/insights/articles/regulation-of-dark-patterns

## CATEGORY 28: 2026 MID-JUNE NPM SUPPLY CHAIN ESCALATION + WCAG AAA AUTH + WEBGPU FALLBACK BUG + DARK-PATTERN LITIGATION WAVE + ANDROID HAPTICS TAXONOMY (R178 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

### 28.1 npm Supply Chain 2026 Escalation — Credential Theft → Cloud-Infra Persistence
- [S-104] **[P1]** npm supply chain 2026 escalation — credential theft → cloud infrastructure persistent control + production environments breach — CSDN's "npm supply chain worm 2026 deep report" (blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/160780907) documents a **qualitative escalation** in npm supply-chain attacks: the November 2025 incident infected **425-500 npm packages** (IndonesianFoods spam campaign per Kaspersky March 2026) affecting **25,000+ GitHub repositories**, with developer credentials leaked at a rate of **1,000 per hour**. Entering 2026, attackers shifted from pure credential theft to **persistent control of cloud infrastructure**: compromised credentials are used to plant backdoors in production environments, surviving individual token rotation. Concrete cases: (a) **Rspack** (@rspack/core + @rspack/cli, 300K+/145K+ weekly downloads) compromised with XMRig crypto-miner via `postinstall` scripts phoning home to `80.78.28[.]72`, geo-fenced to China/Russia/Belarus/Iran/HK, CPU capped at 75% to evade detection; (b) **Vant** (41K+ weekly) compromised across 10 versions (2.13.3-2.13.5, 3.6.13-3.6.15, 4.9.11-4.9.14). **Distinct from S-070** (framework CDN tampering — a different vector) and **S-083** (game-embed iframe XSS — a different layer) — this is the **build-time dependency-compromise** vector: the malicious code runs at `npm install` time on the developer's machine (or CI), exfiltrating env vars (AWS keys, npm tokens, GitHub PATs) before any production deploy. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) audit `package.json` (if any) and any `install`/`postinstall` scripts in game dependencies — zero tolerance for non-essential `postinstall` hooks, every one must be reviewed and pinned to a known-good version; (2) verify no game build pulls packages by **version range** (`^` or `~`) — all dependencies must be **lock-file pinned** (`package-lock.json` + `npm ci` in CI, never bare `npm install`); (3) run `npm audit --production` on every dependency tree and block builds with any high/critical finding; (4) for any third-party library loaded at runtime (not just build time) — e.g., CDN'd Phaser, Three.js, jQuery — enable **SRI (Subresource Integrity)** hashes on every `<script src>` tag (extension of the dead-endpoint detector Phase 0.5); (5) scan all game HTML/JS for indicators of compromise — `postinstall`, `child_process.exec`, `eval(`, `Function(`, base64-encoded strings longer than 200 chars, references to known C2 IPs — and flag each for review; (6) document a **rotation drill**: simulate an npm-token leak (revoke + rotate every credential touched by the build within 1h SLA — the 1000-creds/hr leak rate makes this a real exercise); (7) verify CI runners use **ephemeral, isolated environments** (no shared credential cache between builds) so a single compromised build cannot exfiltrate tokens used by other builds. Source: https://blog.csdn.net/weixin_42376192/article/details/160780907 + https://www.kaspersky.com/blog/tag/supply-chain-attack/ + https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/npm-malware-exposes-developer-data/

### 28.2 WCAG 2.2 SC 3.3.7 Accessible Authentication (Enhanced, Level AAA)
- [A-030] **[P0]** WCAG 2.2 SC 3.3.7 Accessible Authentication (Enhanced / Level AAA) — game login/rewards/IAP pages must NOT require cognitive-function test (puzzle/memorization/transcription) AND must not require re-authentication for the same identity within an active session — web-accessibility-checker.com's WCAG 2.2 2026 checklist (87 success criteria) and getwcag.com confirm SC 3.3.7 is the **Level AAA enhancement** of the AA-level SC 3.3.8 (which itself underlies the No-Learning criterion SC 3.3.9 covered in A-029). The cognitive-function tests prohibited by SC 3.3.7 (Enhanced) are: (a) **memorization** — "remember this 8-digit code we showed you 30 seconds ago"; (b) **transcription** — "type the wavy distorted characters in this CAPTCHA image"; (c) **puzzle-solving** — "click all the crosswalks / rotate the image upright". SC 3.3.7 (Enhanced) goes further than SC 3.3.9 (No-Learning): it also requires **no re-authentication** for the same user within a session unless the action is security-critical (e.g., changing password). **Distinct from A-029** (SC 3.3.9 No-Learning — only covers the cognitive-test prohibition) — SC 3.3.7 adds the **session-persistence** requirement. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) on every login/rewards/IAP/age-gate page, verify NO CAPTCHA of any kind is presented unless there is a non-cognitive alternative (hCaptcha Accessibility Cookie, WebAuthn, email magic link); (2) verify the game does not force re-login on every visit — once authenticated, the session persists via secure cookie/localStorage for at least the documented session window (e.g., 30 days) unless the user explicitly logs out or changes password; (3) for any step-up authentication (e.g., "confirm your password before spending coins"), verify a passwordless alternative exists (WebAuthn, biometric, email confirmation); (4) verify copy/paste is allowed in every auth field (no `onpaste="return false"`); (5) audit the consent/cookie-banner UI for hidden cognitive load — a 12-category cookie consent matrix the user must configure every visit is a soft cognitive-function test — provide a "Reject All" + "Accept All" symmetric pair (synergy with W-146/W-147 dark-pattern audit). Source: https://web-accessibility-checker.com/en/blog/wcag-2-2-checklist-2026 + https://getwcag.com/en/blog/wcag-2-2-checklist

### 28.3 WCAG 2.2 Designer Failure Patterns — Placeholders-as-Labels + Icon-Without-Text
- [A-031] **[P1]** WCAG 2.2 designer failure patterns — placeholders-as-labels + icons-without-text/aria-label + hamburger-without-aria-label — practical audit catching what automated tools (axe, WAVE) miss (only 30-40% automated catch rate per accessibilityinnovations.com + assist-software.net 2026 guide) — CorsoUX's "WCAG 2.2 Practical Guide for Designers" (courseux.com, April 2026) catalogs the **top two designer-introduced failures** that ship to production because they pass automated scans but fail real screen-reader users: (a) **Placeholders used as labels** — `<input placeholder="Email">` with no `<label>` — the placeholder disappears the moment the user starts typing (cognitive load + impossible to verify what field you're in), and **screen readers handle placeholder inconsistently** (some read it as the field name, some ignore it entirely, NVDA + JAWS + VoiceOver all behave differently); (b) **Icons without text or aria-label** — a hamburger `<button><svg>...</svg></button>` with no visible text and no `aria-label="Menu"` is **invisible to a screen reader** (it announces as "button" with no purpose); same for a gear icon settings button, a heart favorites button, a play triangle. **Distinct from A-024** (diverse input methods) and **A-027** (WCAG 2.2 9 new SC general compliance) — this is the **designer-practice** level: catches failures that pass the WCAG-SC checklist but still exclude users. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) for every game page and the homepage, run a manual screen-reader pass (NVDA on Windows, VoiceOver on macOS) — automated axe/WAVE catches 30-40%, the rest is human; (2) for every `<input>`, verify a real `<label for="...">` exists — not just a `placeholder` — and that the label is **visible at all times** (not hidden after typing); (3) for every icon-only button (hamburger, gear, heart, play, pause, share, fullscreen, close-X), verify an `aria-label` is set AND the label is descriptive ("Open menu", not just "Menu"); (4) tab through every page with keyboard only — verify focus is visible on every interactive element (no `outline: none` without a replacement focus style); (5) for the game card grid, verify each card has a programmatic name (the game title as `<h3>` or `aria-label`) — not just an `<img alt>` and a click handler on a `<div>`; (6) document a "designer checklist" companion to the WCAG-SC checklist — every new game page ships with both passed. Source: https://courseux.com/wcag-2-2-guidelines-designers-guide + https://accessibilityinnovations.com/blogs/wcag-2-2 + https://assist-software.net/business-insights/web-accessibility-2026-complete-guide-wcag-compliance

### 28.4 gfx-rs/wgpu WebGPU→WebGL Fallback — Browser-Specific Failure
- [C-099] **[P1]** gfx-rs/wgpu WebGPU→WebGL fallback browser-specific bug — `Backends::all()` should try WebGPU then fall back to WebGL but fails on Chrome Linux/Win while Firefox works correctly — github.com/gfx-rs/wgpu#6166 documents a **framework-level cross-browser inconsistency** in Rust's most popular WebGPU/WebGL abstraction layer (used by Bevy, wgpu-native, and many WASM-compiled game engines): when both `webgpu` and `webgl` cargo features are enabled and `Backends::all()` is set, the adapter **should** attempt WebGPU first and fall back to WebGL on failure. In practice this works on Firefox but **does not happen on Chrome** (Linux + Windows 10 reproductions in the issue), leaving the game with no adapter and a black canvas. This is a **distinct bug class from G-136** (app-level graceful-fallback audit — checks that the app calls `navigator.gpu` correctly) and **P-026** (general WebGL→WebGPU fallback pattern) — it is a **framework-level** bug where the abstraction layer itself mis-negotiates the fallback, and **only manifests in specific browsers**, so a passing test on Firefox does not guarantee a passing test on Chrome. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) for any game built on Bevy/wgpu/rust+wasm engine, verify adapter selection on **Chrome Linux + Chrome Windows + Chrome macOS + Firefox + Safari** separately — Firefox passing is NOT sufficient evidence; (2) write an explicit fallback test: force-disable WebGPU (`chrome://flags/#disable-unsafe-webgpu` or `--disable-features=Vulkan`) and verify the game falls back to WebGL2 and renders correctly (not black canvas); (3) if the framework bug is hit, apply the workaround documented in the issue: request `Backends::BROWSER_WEBGPU` first, check for `None`, then explicitly request `Backends::GL` — do not rely on `Backends::all()` to chain automatically; (4) add a CI matrix that runs the WebGPU-disabled fallback test on every PR — this regression reappears when wgpu or the browser updates; (5) document per-game which engine/abstraction layer is used (`render_stack` field from G-137) and tag any wgpu-based game as "fallback-bug-risk" until the upstream issue closes. Source: https://github.com/gfx-rs/wgpu/issues/6166

### 28.5 GPUAdapter.isFallbackAdapter Property REMOVED — WebGPU API Deprecation
- [B-095] **[P2]** GPUAdapter.isFallbackAdapter property REMOVED from web platform — MDN's October 2025 update (developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/GPUAdapter/isFallbackAdapter) documents that the `GPUAdapter.isFallbackAdapter` read-only property has been **removed from the web platform**. Migrations required: use `GPUAdapterInfo.isFallbackAdapter` instead. The migration path is not a simple rename — `GPUAdapterInfo` is obtained asynchronously via `adapter.requestAdapterInfo()`, whereas the old `adapter.isFallbackAdapter` was synchronous. This breaks any 2024-era WebGPU adapter-capability detection code that branches on whether the resolved adapter is a low-power fallback (e.g., code that downgrades texture resolution when `isFallbackAdapter === true`). **Distinct from C-098** (WebGPU break-on-error debug) and **B-095 family** (browser API churn) — this is a **deterministic API removal** that will throw `TypeError: undefined is not true` on every game that still references the old property, with no warning in stable Chrome. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) grep all game source (HTML/JS/WASM-binding JS) for `isFallbackAdapter` references on a `GPUAdapter` instance — every match must be audited; (2) for each match, migrate to the async `GPUAdapterInfo` pattern: `const info = await adapter.requestAdapterInfo(); if (info.isFallbackAdapter) {...}` — note the new code is **async**, callers must be updated to `await`; (3) verify on Chrome 149+ stable that no console error mentions `isFallbackAdapter` after migration; (4) add a regression test that loads each WebGPU game on Chrome 149 and asserts no `TypeError` related to adapter properties appears in the console within 10s of load; (5) for any game still using the old API, treat as a **P2 ship-blocker** — the game will throw on every load on stable Chrome. Source: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/GPUAdapter/isFallbackAdapter

### 28.6 Three.js WebGPU Migration 2026 — Official Import Path with WebGL2 Fallback
- [G-138] **[P2]** Three.js WebGPU migration 2026 — official import path `import * as THREE from 'three/webgpu'` enables WebGPU rendering with **automatic WebGL2 fallback** — utsubo.com's "Migrate Three.js to WebGPU (2026) — The Complete Checklist" documents the canonical 2026 migration path. Key patterns: (a) the `three/webgpu` subpath export provides `WebGPURenderer` that internally falls back to WebGL2 if WebGPU is unavailable — **no manual fallback code needed**; (b) for **react-three-fiber** (R3F) apps, R3F supports WebGPU through an **async `gl` prop factory**: `gl={async (canvas) => new WebGPURenderer({ canvas, forceWebGL: false })}` — the async factory is required because WebGPU initialization is async; (c) materials must migrate from `MeshStandardMaterial` (WebGL-only) to `MeshStandardNodeMaterial` (node-based, WebGPU-compatible) — the node material system is the bridge that lets the same material work on both renderers. **Distinct from G-114** (GL2GPU transpiler — an automated WebGL→WebGPU source-to-source tool) — this is the **official Three.js** migration path, no transpiler required, just an import-path change + material-class migration. **Distinct from G-136** (general fallback audit) — this provides the **concrete code pattern** for the most common game engine in use. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) for any Three.js game (identify via `grep -rl "three" --include="*.html" --include="*.js"`), check the import path — `from 'three'` (WebGL-only, legacy) vs `from 'three/webgpu'` (modern, dual-stack); flag legacy imports for migration; (2) for migrated games, force-disable WebGPU and verify automatic WebGL2 fallback produces the same visual output (no missing materials, no broken shaders); (3) verify any custom shaders (`ShaderMaterial`) are migrated to `ShaderNodeMaterial` or the TSL (Three Shading Language) — raw GLSL shaders do not run on the WebGPU path; (4) on Safari 26+ (which ships WebGPU per B-090), verify the WebGPU path is actually selected (not silently falling back to WebGL2) via `renderer.backend.isWebGPUBackend === true`; (5) benchmark the migrated game: WebGPU path should show ≥1.5x draw-call throughput vs the WebGL2 fallback path on the same hardware (per P-047 benchmarks). Source: https://utsubo.com/blog/webgpu-threejs-migration-guide

### 28.7 Dark Patterns in Gaming 2026 — Lawsuit Wave Tracker
- [W-148] **[P1]** Dark Patterns in Gaming 2026 lawsuit wave tracker — Rain Intelligence's "Dark Patterns in Gaming: Lawsuits Target Manipulative Monetization Tactics" (rainintelligence.com, March 2025) provides a **comprehensive litigation tracker** covering the wave of class actions targeting manipulative game monetization. Tracked case categories: (a) **loot boxes** — multiple class actions arguing loot boxes constitute unlawful gambling (relying on the precedent that random-reward mechanics targeted at minors meet the legal definition of a wager); (b) **hard-to-cancel subscriptions** — "roach motel" pattern where subscribing is one tap but cancellation requires 5+ menu levels + phone call + wait-on-hold (FTC "click-to-cancel" rule enforcement); (c) **manipulative monetization** — FOMO timers, fake scarcity, confirmshaming, pay-to-skip designed to exploit sunk-cost bias. The tracker is distinct from a single-case ruling — it shows the **pattern of enforcement** across state AGs, federal courts, and international regulators, establishing that the legal risk is no longer hypothetical. **Distinct from W-146** (FTC Honda/Epic — single precedent-setting actions) and **W-143** (US addiction lawsuit wave — broader tort theory) — this is the **manipulative-monetization-specific** litigation tracker focused on the UX patterns GameZipper could inadvertently ship (any rewarded-ad placement, any "limited offer" banner, any coin/IAP flow). **Test for GameZipper**: (1) audit every "limited time" / "exclusive" / "only X left" banner across the homepage and game pages — each must be backed by a **verifiable factual basis** (a real countdown to a real event, real inventory count) or removed; (2) verify any subscription-like flow (if GameZipper adds premium memberships) has a **one-tap cancel** path symmetric with the subscribe path; (3) for every rewarded-video placement ("watch ad for +1 life"), verify the **free alternative** (continue without watching) is equally prominent — not buried below the fold or styled smaller; (4) document the monetization-UX audit in the quarterly compliance review alongside W-146/W-147; (5) cross-reference each UX pattern against the Rain Intelligence case tracker — if a pattern is the subject of an active class action, treat it as a P1 ship-blocker regardless of whether GameZipper is the defendant. Source: https://rainintelligence.com/blog/dark-patterns-in-gaming-lawsuits-target-manipulative-monetization-tactics + https://arxiv.org/html/2412.05039v1 + https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dark_pattern

### 28.8 Dark Patterns Empirical Study of Harmfulness — 174-Game Harm Taxonomy
- [W-149] **[P2]** Dark Patterns Empirical Study of Harmfulness — 174-game dataset + harm taxonomy mapping pattern characteristics to user decision-making harm — ResearchGate publication 390235729 "Dark Patterns in Games: An Empirical Study of Their Harmfulness" (March 2025) goes beyond the **prevalence** studies (W-144 / arXiv 2412.05039's 1496-game prevalence survey) to develop a **taxonomy of harm**: which dark-pattern characteristics actually cause measurable harm to user decision-making, and how that harm manifests (financial loss, time loss, psychological distress, social pressure). The paper makes **recommendations for stakeholders** — researchers (study which interventions mitigate harm), regulators (which patterns warrant enforcement priority), and game developers (which patterns to avoid even where not yet illegal). The harm taxonomy is the operational complement to the legal-compliance checklists in W-147 — it tells you **why** a pattern matters, not just **whether** it's prohibited. **Distinct from W-144** (CN/JP F2P dark-pattern audit — regional prevalence) and **W-147** (operational checklist) — this is the **academic harm-impact** dimension that informs prioritization: if two patterns are both technically non-compliant, fix the one with higher demonstrated harm first. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) map each UX pattern flagged by the W-147 secureprivacy checklist to a harm category from the ResearchGate taxonomy (financial / time / psychological / social); (2) prioritize remediation by harm severity — financial-harm patterns (real-money IAP dark patterns) ship-block before psychological-harm patterns (mild FOMO copy); (3) document the harm taxonomy in the test matrix so QA knows **why** each check exists, not just **what** to check; (4) for any new monetization feature, run it through the harm taxonomy before ship — if it scores high on any harm dimension, redesign before code; (5) track user complaints (support emails, app-store reviews) against the taxonomy — patterns generating real user harm complaints get P0 priority. Source: https://researchgate.net/publication/390235729_Dark_Patterns_in_Games_An_Empirical_Study_of_Their_Harmfulness + https://dl.acm.org/doi/fullHtml/10.1145/3491101.3519837

### 28.9 Mobile UX 2026 Concrete Benchmarks — Onboarding, Friction, Back Gestures
- [G-139] **[P2]** Mobile UX 2026 concrete benchmarks — Bluespace/UXCam's "Mobile UX Design: A Complete Guide for 2026" (uxcam.com, April 2026) distills five operational UX benchmarks for mobile-first products (which GameZipper is — 70%+ mobile traffic): (a) **onboarding <5 screens** — every additional onboarding screen drops completion rate ~20%; the guide's data shows 5 screens is the cliff; (b) **minimize input friction** — every text input field reduces conversion; prefer tap/swipe/voice; if a field is required, provide autocomplete + sensible defaults; (c) **respect system back gestures** — Android edge-swipe-back and iOS swipe-back-from-edge must work in every game's modal/overlay (a custom `<div>` modal that captures the back gesture breaks the user's muscle memory); (d) **treat performance as UX** — a 3-second load is a UX failure, not just a perf metric; users perceive slow pages as broken; (e) **handle errors with specific actionable copy** — "Something went wrong" is a UX failure; the message must tell the user what failed AND what to do next ("Your connection dropped — tap Retry to resume your game"). **Distinct from G-100** (FTUE <3 tap onboarding — a single specific metric) and **G-105** (casual game tutorial <3 step) — this is the **broader 5-benchmark UX baseline** covering the full mobile session lifecycle. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) for the homepage + every game page, count onboarding screens (any modal that blocks play) — must be ≤5, target ≤3; (2) count required text inputs across the full session (search, login, feedback forms) — flag any >2 in a single session flow; (3) on Android Chrome + iOS Safari, verify the system back gesture dismisses every modal/overlay/settings-panel (not just the in-app back button) — test the `popstate` handler fires correctly; (4) verify every error message (gz-error-toast, "save failed", "network error") includes (a) what failed + (b) what to do — flag any generic "Something went wrong" message for rewrite (synergy with the post-deploy-verification skill's toast patterns); (5) measure LCP/INP on mobile (per P-063) and treat any p75 LCP >2.5s as a UX defect, not just a perf defect. Source: https://uxcam.com/blog/mobile-ux + https://novatechbeacon.com/latest/why-2026-gaming-is-built-for-one-hand-play

### 28.10 Android Haptics 2026 — Clear/Rich/Buzzy Classification + Design Principles
- [GP-108] **[P2]** Android Haptics 2026 clear/rich/buzzy classification + design principles — developer.android.com's "Haptics design principles" guide (updated Feb 2026) defines the **official Android taxonomy** for haptic feedback that game UX must align with: (a) **Clear** haptics — sharp, discrete, well-defined effects for confirmations and momentary events (button tap, level-up chime, coin pickup); (b) **Rich** haptics — textures and patterns that convey sustained information (engine revving, surface texture under a dragged object, heartbeat during low-health state); (c) **Buzzy** haptics — legacy rumble-style effects, discouraged for new design (perceived as low-quality, drains battery). The taxonomy maps to specific `HapticFeedbackConstants` (`KEYBOARD_TAP`, `VIRTUAL_KEY`, `LONG_PRESS`, `CLOCK_TICK`) and the newer `VibratorManager` API. Design principles: (1) haptics must be **non-redundant with audio** — if the audio already conveys the event, the haptic should add a different dimension (texture, weight, direction), not just echo the sound; (2) haptics must be **user-toggleable** in the game's settings (some users have sensory sensitivities — synergy with A-025 neuro-inclusive design); (3) haptics must respect the **system haptic-off setting** — if the user disabled system haptics, the game should not override. **Distinct from GP-107** (navigator.vibrate() — the cross-platform Web API, which on Android Chrome maps to buzzy/legacy haptics and on iOS Safari is a silent no-op) — this is the **Android-native** taxonomy that the Web API cannot express. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) for every game with `navigator.vibrate()` calls (per GP-107 audit), verify on Android Chrome that the haptic is perceived as **clear** (sharp tap for discrete events), not **buzzy** (distracting rumble); (2) for games that ship as a PWA or installable web app, verify the game's settings expose a **haptics toggle** that defaults to ON but can be disabled; (3) verify the game respects the Android system "Vibrate on tap" setting — if the user disabled it globally, the game's `navigator.vibrate()` calls should be suppressed (the Web API does this automatically, but custom native bridges do not); (4) for any rich-haptic pattern (sustained texture), measure battery impact — sustained rich haptics drain battery 5-10% faster, which on a casual mobile game is a UX regression; (5) document per-game which haptic class (clear/rich/buzzy) each vibration event uses, and migrate buzzy events to clear events where possible. Source: https://developer.android.com/develop/ui/views/haptics/haptics-principles + https://parachutedesign.ca/blog/thumb-zone-ux

## CATEGORY 29: 2026 MID-JUNE MIASMA/IRONWORM NPM SUPPLY CHAIN ESCALATION + SELF-HEALING QA REALITY + W3C MOBILE WCAG + WASM 3.0 + CISA KEV ENFORCEMENT (R179 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

### 29.1 Miasma Wave 2 Phantom Gyp — npm `binding.gyp` Lifecycle-Script Bypass
- [S-105] **[P1]** Miasma Wave 2 Phantom Gyp npm supply chain attack via `binding.gyp` lifecycle-script bypass (June 3, 2026) — cybersecuritynews.com, cyberpress.org, and pulse.adyog.com document that on June 3, 2026 a coordinated npm supply-chain attack compromised **57 packages across multiple maintainer accounts** in **under two hours**, producing **286 malicious versions**. The novel vector: instead of the traditional `postinstall`/`preinstall` script hooks (which every modern npm security scanner watches — Socket, Snyk, Dependabot, osv-scanner), the attackers abused **`binding.gyp`** — the JSON-ish build-configuration file used by `node-gyp` to compile native Node.js addons. A **157-byte `binding.gyp` file** uses **gyp's command-substitution feature** to execute arbitrary shell commands during `npm install`, **bypassing every lifecycle-script scanner**. High-impact packages hit include **@vapi-ai/server-sdk (408,000+ monthly downloads)**, `ai-sdk-ollama`, and other AI-adjacent SDKs. Chainguard (chainguard.dev) confirmed their artifacts were safe by design because they pin dependencies and don't run arbitrary build scripts. **Distinct from S-104** (general 2025→2026 npm escalation — Rspack/Vant crypto-miner via `postinstall`) — this is the **SPECIFIC bypass vector** that defeats S-104 mitigation #1 of "block non-essential `postinstall` hooks": Phantom Gyp doesn't use `postinstall` at all, so the S-104 mitigation does not catch it. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) extend the S-104 supply-chain audit to scan for **`binding.gyp`** files in every transitive dependency — `find node_modules -name 'binding.gyp' | xargs grep -lE 'action|command' ` — every match must be reviewed for command-substitution abuse (`<!(...)`, `@(var@)`); (2) verify the CI build uses `npm ci --ignore-scripts` (or equivalent) which disables ALL lifecycle scripts including node-gyp invocation — but note this also disables legitimate native builds, so native modules must be pre-built; (3) for any package that legitimately needs `binding.gyp`, pin it to a known-good version hash AND verify the `binding.gyp` content matches the upstream commit (use `npm ls` + `git diff` on the binding.gyp); (4) enable Socket.dev's "behavioral" analysis mode which inspects the EFFECT of install-time code, not just script names — it would catch the gyp-substitution vector where static scanners miss; (5) document the Phantom Gyp bypass in the GameZipper supply-chain threat model and re-audit any time a new package with `binding.gyp` enters the tree; (6) treat any package with `binding.gyp` + a `prepublish`/`version` script as **elevated risk** — the combo is rare in legitimate packages and was a signature of this attack. Source: https://cybersecuritynews.com/binding-gyp-supply-chain-attack-compromises-dozens-of-npm-packages + https://cyberpress.org/binding-gyp-targets-npm-maintainers + https://pulse.adyog.com/insights/npm-worm-wave-2026 + https://chainguard.dev/unchained/chainguard-artifacts-safe-from-miasma-phantom-gyp-npm-attack

### 29.2 IronWorm — Rust Infostealer + eBPF Rootkit + AI Credential Theft
- [S-106] **[P1]** IronWorm npm supply chain attack (June 6, 2026) — Rust-based infostealer + eBPF kernel rootkit + persistent IDE backdoor targeting AI assistant credentials — cipherssecurity.com, cyberse.ai, and bearyangry.com document that on June 6, 2026, researchers at JFrog and StepSecurity identified a coordinated npm supply-chain attack delivering a new malware family codenamed **IronWorm**. Technical profile: (a) **Rust-based information stealer** — high-performance, cross-platform (Linux + macOS), difficult to reverse-engineer; (b) **eBPF kernel rootkit** — hides the malware's processes, files, and network connections at the kernel level, surviving most user-space detection (ps, ls, netstat); (c) **persistent IDE backdoor injection** — modifies VS Code / JetBrains / Cursor config to re-exfiltrate credentials on every IDE launch; (d) **AI assistant credential targeting** — explicitly scrapes Claude API keys, GitHub Copilot tokens, OpenAI API keys, and Cursor auth tokens (the AI-developer supply chain is now a target); (e) **self-propagating** via trojanized npm packages using the stolen credentials to publish more malicious versions (Shai-Hulud-style worm behavior). **Distinct from S-104** (general escalation) and **S-105** (Phantom Gyp vector) — IronWorm is the **PERSISTENCE + AI-CREDENTIAL-TARGETING** dimension: it's not a one-shot crypto-miner, it's a long-lived implant that specifically hunts for the credentials an AI-agent-based dev workflow (like GameZipper's) uses. **Distinct from S-097** (Chrome Gemini Live "Glic Jack" — browser-side AI attack) — IronWorm attacks the DEVELOPER-SIDE AI credentials, not the browser. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) audit every developer machine + CI runner that has touched an npm install in the last 30 days for IronWorm indicators of compromise (IoCs): unknown Rust binaries in `~/.cargo/bin` or `/tmp`, eBPF programs loaded (`bpftool prog list`), modified VS Code `settings.json` / `keybindings.json` with suspicious `http.proxy` or `terminal.integrated.env` entries; (2) **rotate ALL AI assistant credentials** (Claude API keys, GitHub Copilot tokens, OpenAI keys, Cursor tokens) as a precaution — IronWorm's stealth means absence of evidence is not evidence of absence; (3) verify the Hermes Agent / Claude Code / Codex CLI configuration does not store credentials in plaintext files that a Rust stealer could trivially read — use OS keychain (macOS Keychain, Windows Credential Manager, Linux secret-service) not `~/.config/*/config.json`; (4) for the CI pipeline, verify runners are **ephemeral and isolated** (spin up fresh per build, no shared credential cache) so a single compromised build cannot exfiltrate tokens used by other builds — synergy with S-104 mitigation #7; (5) add an eBPF-detection step to the CI security baseline: `bpftool prog show` should return zero unexpected programs on a clean runner; (6) document the AI-credential-rotation drill in the incident-response playbook — the 30-minute "rotate everything" runbook is now a quarterly exercise, not an annual one. Source: https://cipherssecurity.com/ironworm-miasma-npm-rust-ebpf-rootkit-supply + https://cyberse.ai/news/191 + https://bearyangry.com/2026/06/06/ironworm-and-new-miasma-worm-variant-hit-npm-in-supply-chain-attacks + https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/ironworm-and-new-miasma-worm-variant.html

### 29.3 Red Hat Miasma Worm — Compromised-Vendor-Namespace Pattern
- [S-107] **[P2]** Red Hat Miasma worm (June 1, 2026) — compromised-vendor-namespace attack pattern demonstrating that trusted vendor namespaces are not safe — labs.cloudsecurityalliance.org, techtimes.com, and unit42.paloaltonetworks.com document that on June 1, 2026, researchers at Wiz (and multiple concurrent firms) identified a supply-chain compromise affecting **at least 32 packages under the `@redhat-cloud-services` npm namespace**, cumulatively averaging **~80,000 weekly downloads**. Red Hat disclosed the incident as **RHSB-2026-006** the same day. The attack was enabled by a **single compromised Red Hat employee GitHub account**, used to push malicious orphan commits to multiple repositories that auto-published to npm on merge. The worm was **self-propagating**: it stole npm publish tokens from infected machines and used them to publish more malicious versions, expanding the blast radius without further human action. **Distinct from S-104** (general escalation — IndonesianFoods spam) and **S-105** (Phantom Gyp — a different VECTOR) — this is the **TRUSTED-VENDOR-NAMESPACE-COMROMISE** pattern: `@redhat-cloud-services` is a verified publisher, the packages have Red Hat's name on them, and `npm install @redhat-cloud-services/*` would pass any reasonable trust check. The lesson: **namespace reputation is not a security control**. **Distinct from S-108** (specific WebGPU package) — this is a fleet-wide vendor compromise, not a single package. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) audit every npm dependency for whether it comes from a vendor namespace (`@vendor/*`) — these are now **elevated risk** per this incident; verify each is still actively maintained and the publisher account shows no signs of compromise (sudden new maintainer additions, unusual publish timestamps); (2) implement **Sigstore/`npm provenance`** verification — `npm install --provenance` will refuse packages without verifiable build provenance; Red Hat's post-incident guidance emphasized provenance as the mitigation; (3) for the Hermes Agent / GameZipper build, pin every `@vendor/*` dependency to a **specific git commit hash**, not a version range — a compromised publisher can push a new version at any time, but they cannot rewrite history; (4) add a **publish-anomaly detector** to the dependency-monitoring pipeline: alert if any dependency publishes >3 versions in 24h (the Miasma worm's propagation signature); (5) document a **vendor-namespace-compromise runbook**: when a vendor namespace is disclosed as compromised, the SLA is (a) identify all GameZipper dependencies in that namespace within 1h, (b) pin to the last-known-good commit within 4h, (c) rotate any CI credentials that touched the compromised versions within 24h; (6) subscribe to GitHub Security Advisories + the npm advisory database for every `@vendor/*` dependency — the Red Hat advisory went out same-day, but only registered subscribers got it in time. Source: https://labs.cloudsecurityalliance.org/research/csa-research-note-miasma-npm-supply-chain-redhat-20260603-cs + https://techtimes.com/articles/317832/20260605/red-hat-npm-packages-compromised-57-more-follow-signed-attestations-cannot-block-pipeline-hijack.htm + https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/monitoring-npm-supply-chain-attacks

### 29.4 @antv/g-webgpu-engine npm Malware — WebGPU-Specific Package Compromise
- [S-108] **[P2]** @antv/g-webgpu-engine npm malware (GMS-2026-193, May 19, 2026) — WebGPU-specific rendering engine package compromised in Mini Shai-Hulud campaign — advisories.gitlab.com/npm/@antv/g-webgpu-engine/GMS-2026-193 documents that on May 19, 2026, a new supply-chain attack linked to the **Mini Shai-Hulud campaign** was identified targeting `@antv/g-webgpu-engine`, the **WebGPU rendering backend for the AntV visualization library**. This is **specifically relevant to browser games** because (a) `g-webgpu-engine` is literally a WebGPU rendering engine — game developers building WebGPU-based games or visualizations are a direct audience; (b) the AntV ecosystem (`@antv/*`) is widely used in Chinese and Asian tech stacks, which overlaps with the geo-fenced targeting of prior supply-chain attacks (Rspack/Vant geo-fenced to China/Russia/Belarus/Iran per S-104); (c) the package is a **runtime dependency** (loaded by the browser), not just a build-time dependency — meaning a compromised version can exfiltrate data from **end users playing the game**, not just from the developer machine. **Distinct from S-104** (general npm escalation) and **S-105/S-106** (specific worm vectors) — this is the **WebGPU-engine-specific** compromise: a single malicious version of a rendering engine can ship a backdoor into every game that bundles it. **Distinct from S-083** (game-embed iframe XSS — a different layer) — this is the **dependency-bundle** layer. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) grep all game HTML/JS for references to `@antv/`, `g-webgpu`, or any AntV package — `grep -rn '@antv\|g-webgpu' --include="*.html" --include="*.js" .`; any match is a P1 audit item; (2) for any game using a third-party WebGPU/WebGL rendering engine (Three.js, Babylon.js, PlayCanvas, PixiJS, AntV, deck.gl), verify the engine is loaded from a **trusted CDN with SRI hash** OR bundled from a **pinned, audited npm version** — never `latest` and never an unpinned range; (3) subscribe to the GitLab Advisory Database (advisories.gitlab.com) and GitHub Security Advisories for EVERY rendering engine in the GameZipper stack — the AntV advisory was published same-day; (4) for any game that loads a rendering engine at runtime from a CDN, verify the CDN supports **SRI (Subresource Integrity)** and the `integrity=` attribute is set on every `<script src>` — extension of the dead-endpoint detector (Phase 0.5) to cover integrity, not just liveness; (5) document which games use which rendering engine (`render_stack` field from G-137) and tag any AntV-based game as "supply-chain-risk" until the advisory is resolved; (6) run a quarterly "rendering-engine audit" comparing every bundled engine version against the latest advisory database. Source: https://advisories.gitlab.com/npm/@antv/g-webgpu-engine/GMS-2026-193

### 29.5 Self-Healing Test Automation — Hype vs Reality (Silent Wrong-Element Matches)
- [C-100] **[P2]** Self-healing test automation — hype vs reality (QATechTools June 2, 2026) — self-healing locators introduce silent wrong-element matches + action drift that can mask real regressions in canvas-heavy games — qatechtools.com's "Self-Healing Locators: Hype vs Reality" (June 2, 2026) and vericence.com's "AI Test Healing in Playwright: A Practical Strategy" provide a **critical evaluation** of the self-healing test-automation wave that C-096 (Playwright Test Agents) and C-097 (Healenium/Mabl/Testim) champion. The hype: when a selector breaks (button gets a new class, element moves to a new container), the AI auto-regenerates a working selector and the test "passes". The reality: (a) **silent wrong-element matches** — the healed selector matches a DIFFERENT button that happens to satisfy the semantic query (e.g., the test wanted "Submit" but healed to "Save" because both are `<button>` with text); the test reports green but tested the wrong thing; (b) **action drift** — a button is renamed from "Play" to "Start Game" and the healed selector still matches, but the underlying handler changed (e.g., now requires login first) — the test "passes" the click but misses the new login gate regression; (c) **confidence masking** — self-healing tools report a "heal confidence score" but most teams don't gate on it, so low-confidence heals ship to CI and silently pass; (d) **DOM-volatility blindness** — for canvas-heavy games (GameZipper's bread and butter), the DOM is sparse and most interactivity is inside the canvas where selectors don't apply at all — self-healing gives false confidence that "the UI is tested" when only the surrounding chrome (buttons, menus) is covered. **Distinct from C-096** (Playwright Test Agents — the TOOL) and **C-097** (Healenium/Mabl — specific products) — this is the **FAILURE-MODE / CRITICAL-EVALUATION** dimension: when does self-healing make things WORSE? **Test for GameZipper**: (1) if adopting any self-healing tool (Healenium, Playwright AI locators, Mabl, Testim), configure it to **FAIL the test on any heal with confidence < 0.9** rather than silently passing — low-confidence heals are the silent-wrong-element-match vector; (2) for canvas-heavy games, do NOT rely on self-healing for the core gameplay assertions — supplement with canvas-pixel-diff (G-120) and the IIFE-injection solvability verifier (gamezipper-qa Phase 6) which test the actual game logic, not the DOM chrome; (3) log every heal event to gz-analytics with (old_selector, new_selector, confidence, test_name) and review the heal log weekly — a spike in heals on a specific test indicates the underlying UI is volatile and needs a more stable selector strategy, not more healing; (4) for any test that was healed, **re-run the original (broken) selector** in a separate "canary" run — if the original selector now matches a DIFFERENT element, flag as a silent-wrong-element-match risk; (5) document a "self-healing governance policy": heals are allowed for cosmetic selector changes (class rename, wrapper div), but FORBIDDEN for semantic changes (button text change, handler change) — semantic changes must update the test manually; (6) treat self-healing as a **maintainability optimization, not a coverage optimization** — it reduces test-maintenance toil but does not increase what you're testing, and can decrease effective coverage if heals mask real regressions. Source: https://qatechtools.com/2026/06/02/self-healing-locators-hype-vs-reality + https://vericence.com/vericence-insight/ai-test-healing-in-playwright-a-practical-strategy-for-resilient-test-suites + https://scrolltest.com/self-healing-test-selectors-ai-agents

### 29.6 W3C MATF WCAG2Mobile — Official Mobile-Specific WCAG 2.2 Application Guidance
- [A-032] **[P0]** W3C MATF WCAG2Mobile — official mobile-specific WCAG 2.2 application guidance (w3c.github.io/matf) — The W3C Mobile Accessibility Task Force (MATF) document "Guidance on Applying WCAG 2.2 to Mobile Applications (WCAG2Mobile)" at w3c.github.io/matf is the **authoritative W3C-published mapping** of WCAG 2.2 success criteria to mobile application behavior. It builds on the older "Mobile Accessibility: How WCAG 2.0 and Other W3C/WAI Guidelines Apply to Mobile" but adds the WCAG 2.1 + 2.2 changes and has a stronger focus on **native mobile applications** (which includes installable PWAs and web games played in mobile browsers). Key mobile-specific mappings relevant to GameZipper (70%+ mobile traffic): (a) **SC 2.5.5 Target Size (Enhanced) AAA** → mobile touch targets should be 44×44 CSS px (the AA version SC 2.5.8 is 24×24 — covered in A-014); (b) **SC 2.5.1 Pointer Gestures** → multipoint/pinch-zoom/path-based gestures MUST have a single-point alternative (a game that requires a two-finger pinch to zoom the board must also offer a +/- button); (c) **SC 1.3.4 Orientation** → the game must work in both portrait AND landscape (a puzzle that only renders correctly in landscape fails this); (d) **SC 1.4.10 Reflow** → the game UI must reflow at 320 CSS px width without horizontal scroll (the mobile breakpoint test); (e) **SC 2.5.7 Dragging Movements** → anything operable by dragging MUST also be operable by a single tap/click (a drag-to-sort puzzle must offer tap-to-select-then-tap-destination); (f) **SC 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast** → game UI icons, borders, and visual focus indicators need 3:1 contrast against adjacent colors; (g) **SC 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured** → when a mobile keyboard or autofill sheet appears, the focused input must not be hidden behind it (covered in A-016 but mobile-keyboard-specific here). **Distinct from A-027** (WCAG 2.2 9 new SC — general compliance) and **A-029/A-030** (auth-specific SC) — this is the **MOBILE-APPLICATION-SPECIFIC** official W3C guidance, the authoritative source when a question is "does this WCAG SC apply to my mobile game?". **Test for GameZipper**: (1) for every game + the homepage, run the W3C MATF mobile checklist on a real mobile device (Android Chrome + iOS Safari) — not just desktop DevTools mobile emulation, because real devices surface touch-target, gesture, and orientation issues that emulation misses; (2) for SC 2.5.1, enumerate every multipoint/path-based gesture (pinch-zoom, swipe-to-sort, drag-to-aim, two-finger-rotate) and verify each has a single-point alternative documented in the game's accessibility statement; (3) for SC 1.3.4, rotate the device mid-game (portrait ↔ landscape) on both Android and iOS and verify (a) no layout breakage, (b) game state is preserved, (c) no forced reload — synergy with C-073 foldable-viewport test; (4) for SC 1.4.10, test every game at 320px width (smallest common phone — iPhone SE) and verify no horizontal scroll and no clipped UI; (5) for SC 2.5.7, for every drag-based interaction (drag-tile-to-tube in sort puzzles, drag-aim in shooters), implement and test a tap-based alternative; (6) document the per-game mobile-WCAG compliance status in the test matrix and re-audit on every WCAG 2.2 SC change (W3C publishes updates — subscribe to the MATF repo). Source: https://w3c.github.io/matf + https://webability.io/blog/wcag-2-2-accessibility-standards-complete-2026-compliance-guide + https://digitalapplied.com/blog/wcag-2-2-accessibility-audit-checklist-2026-reference

### 29.7 WCAG Accessibility Auditor MCP Server — Automated Pipeline Integration
- [A-033] **[P2]** WCAG Accessibility Auditor MCP server (mcpize.com) — MCP server audits any URL for WCAG 2.2 compliance using axe-core ruleset + generates ADA + EU Accessibility Act legal-risk scores — mcpize.com's "WCAG Accessibility Auditor - MCP Server" is a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that any MCP-compatible AI agent (Hermes Agent, Claude Desktop, Cursor) can invoke to **automatically audit any URL for WCAG 2.2 compliance**. Technical profile: (a) runs the **axe-core** ruleset (the same engine behind Deque axe DevTools, Lighthouse accessibility audit, and WAVE) programmatically against a live URL; (b) generates a structured report with each violation's (rule_id, severity, element_xpath, failure_summary, remediation_suggestion); (c) computes an aggregate **legal-risk score** for both **ADA (US Americans with Disabilities Act)** and **EAA (EU Accessibility Act, enforceable June 28, 2025)** — translating technical violations into regulatory exposure; (d) MCP-native, so it composes with other tools in an agent workflow (e.g., "audit → fix → re-audit" loop). **Distinct from A-027** (WCAG 2.2 manual checklist — human-driven) and **A-031** (designer failure patterns — human-driven, catches what axe misses) — this is the **automated MCP-based audit** that can run on every deploy, every PR, every cron tick, without human initiation. **Distinct from C-087** (Playwright MCP accessibility-tree-first QA — a different tool with different scope) — this is specifically **WCAG-compliance-scoring**, not general DOM automation. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) install the WCAG Accessibility Auditor MCP server in the Hermes Agent config and run it against every game page + the homepage on a weekly cron — accumulate the per-page violation count + legal-risk score trend over time; (2) gate deploys on a **legal-risk-score threshold** — any page whose ADA or EAA risk score increases vs the previous deploy is a P2 ship-blocker until the regression is fixed or explicitly waived; (3) for each violation, the MCP server returns a remediation suggestion — feed those suggestions to the dev agent (Claude Code / Codex) to auto-fix common violations (missing alt text, insufficient contrast, missing label);
