(4) note axe-core's known 30-40% catch rate (per A-031) — the MCP audit is the **floor**, not the ceiling; supplement with the A-031 manual designer-pattern audit quarterly; (5) for the legal-risk score, track the **EU EAA** dimension specifically — the EAA is enforceable since June 28, 2025 and applies to any GameZipper traffic from the EU; a high EAA risk score is a concrete regulatory exposure, not a hypothetical; (6) publish the per-page WCAG compliance status to an internal dashboard so the team can see the trend — gamify the accessibility score the way we gamify Lighthouse perf scores. Source: https://mcpize.com/mcp/wcag-accessibility-auditor-mcp + https://github.com/creativeprofit22/wcag-audit-command + https://discoverinai.com/wcag-2-2-unlock-potential-by-q4-2026

### 29.8 WASM 3.0 Stable Features — WasmGC + Memory64 + Relaxed SIMD + Tail Calls
- [W-150] **[P2]** WASM 3.0 stable features — WasmGC + Memory64 + Relaxed SIMD + Tail Calls + Typed References (Chrome 119+/Firefox 120+/Safari 18.2+) — alldevtoolshub.com and dev.to document that WebAssembly 3.0 (sometimes called "Wasm 3.0" or "the post-MVP feature wave") shipped stable across all major browsers by late 2025/early 2026: Chrome 119+, Firefox 120+, Safari 18.2+. The feature set that is now universally available: (a) **WasmGC (Garbage Collection)** — enables direct compilation of GC-based languages (Kotlin, Dart, Swift, Java) to WASM without bundling a runtime; game engines written in these languages can now target the web natively; (b) **Memory64** — extends the WASM linear memory from the 32-bit / 4 GB cap to 64-bit (effectively unlimited); critical for large open-world games, high-res asset bundles, or any game whose heap exceeds 4 GB (previously required complex chunking/paging); (c) **Relaxed SIMD** — additional SIMD instructions with relaxed semantics for higher throughput on data-parallel game loops (particle systems, physics solvers); (d) **Tail Calls** — enables proper tail-call optimization, critical for functional-style game logic and recursive algorithms without stack overflow; (e) **Typed References (Typed Function References)** — first-class typed function references, enabling more efficient callback patterns (event handlers, ECS component systems). **Distinct from G-124** (WASM Component Model + WASI 1.0 — the **inter-module composition** story) — these are the **LANGUAGE-FEATURE** flags that expand what a single WASM module can express. **Distinct from P-057** (WASM linear memory fragmentation — a known issue with the current 32-bit model) — Memory64 is the **mitigation** for games that hit the 4 GB ceiling. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) for any WASM-compiled game (Unity/Godot/Unreal/Bevy web exports), check the engine's WASM feature-flag usage — `wasm-features` in the build config or the `.wasm` import section; flag games NOT using Memory64 that ship >2 GB of assets (they're at risk of hitting the 4 GB ceiling on long sessions); (2) for games using WasmGC-compiled engines (Kotlin Multiplatform, Dart Flutter web), verify cross-browser parity — WasmGC had a longer stabilization tail and some edge cases remain on older Safari; (3) benchmark Relaxed SIMD game loops (particle systems, physics) on Chrome vs Firefox vs Safari — the speedup should be 1.5-3x vs scalar; deviation indicates the SIMD path isn't being taken; (4) for any game with deep recursion (board-game AI minimax, procedural generation), verify Tail Calls are enabled — without them, deep recursion stack-overflows and the game crashes on complex levels; (5) document per-game which WASM 3.0 features it requires (`wasm_features` field alongside `render_stack` from G-137) and gate the "this game works on your browser" check on feature-detection (`WebAssembly.validate` with the feature's bytecode), not UA sniffing; (6) re-run the WASM feature-detection matrix on every Chrome/Firefox/Safari release — feature flags flip from behind-a-flag to default-on and back occasionally, breaking games that assumed a stable flag state. Source: https://alldevtoolshub.com/blog/webassembly-browser-tools-2026-simd-threads-wasm-3 + https://dev.to/zny10289/webassembly-in-2026-the-quiet-revolution-that-finally-delivered-5bpl + https://devtoollab.com/blog/webassembly-guide

### 29.9 UX as the Boss Fight 2026 — 70%/80% Abandonment Metrics
- [G-140] **[P2]** UX as the Boss Fight 2026 (ContextSDK) — UX determines product viability, not the feature set; 70% abandon in first hour + 80% mobile loss in 3 days — concrete abandonment metrics — contextsdk.com's "Why 'UX Is the Boss Fight' - And What That Really Means in 2026" and pocketpixel.us's "The Role of UI/UX in Game Development" provide the **quantitative case** for treating UX as the primary product determinant, not a polish layer. Key data points: (a) **70% of players abandon a game within the first hour** if core systems aren't immediately intuitive — the "first hour" is where the UX battle is won or lost; (b) **mobile games lose 80% of users in the first three days**, often due to **onboarding and interface friction** (not gameplay difficulty, not monetization — the interface itself); (c) "poor UI/UX is painfully obvious — players spend more time fighting the interface than enjoying your game"; (d) the article's thesis: "UX is not the final layer. It's the layer that determines whether anything you built actually works." For GameZipper (a portal of 290+ casual browser games where the user can bounce to a different game in one tap), this is existential — a single game with bad UX doesn't just lose that game's players, it loses them for the whole portal. **Distinct from G-100** (FTUE <3 tap onboarding — a single specific metric) and **G-139** (5 mobile UX benchmarks — broader baseline) — this is the **UX-as-product-viability philosophy** with the hard abandonment numbers that justify UX investment to non-UX stakeholders. **Distinct from G-131** (cluttered/obstructive UI complaint audit) — this is the **quantitative-business-case** level, not the per-pattern audit. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) instrument the "first 60 seconds" funnel for every game — events: page_load → first_interaction → first_meaningful_action (e.g., first move in a puzzle, first shot in a shooter); flag any game where first-meaningful-action rate is <70% of page_load (the 30% drop is the UX-abandonment signal); (2) instrument the "first session length" — flag any game where the median first-session is <60 seconds (users bounced before engaging); these games need a UX redesign, not more features; (3) for each flagged game, run the G-100 (FTUE <3 tap) + G-139 (5 mobile UX benchmarks) + G-131 (clutter audit) checks — the combination isolates the UX failure mode; (4) implement a **"first-hour cohort" retention report** — what % of users who played Game X come back within 24h? The 70% first-hour-abandonment stat means a good game retains >30% next-day; a game retaining <15% next-day has a UX problem severe enough to deprioritize or remove from the homepage; (5) for new games added to the catalog, the first 7 days of telemetry determine whether the game stays on the homepage or gets buried — define the "UX viability threshold" (first-meaningful-action rate >60%, next-day retention >20%) and auto-flag games that miss it; (6) document the UX-investment case to stakeholders using the 70%/80% numbers — UX work is not "polish", it's the difference between a game that retains users and one that doesn't. Source: https://contextsdk.com/blogposts/why-ux-is-the-boss-fight---and-what-that-really-means-in-2026 + https://pocketpixel.us/post/the-role-of-ui-ux-in-game-development + https://maze.co/blog/ux-statistics

### 29.10 CISA KEV Enforcement Dimension — 14-Day Federal Patch SLA Mirror
- [B-096] **[P2]** CISA KEV enforcement dimension — CVE-2026-11645 added June 9, 2026 with 14-day federal patch-by-June-23 SLA + 3 new KEV adds June 9 (Cisco/Arista/Chromium) — thehackernews.com, cybersecuritynews.com, and digitalwarfare.com document that on June 9, 2026, CISA added **three new vulnerabilities** to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog: (a) **CVE-2026-11645** — Google Chromium V8 out-of-bounds memory access (the 5th Chrome zero-day of 2026, already covered regression-wise in B-050/B-056/B-092); (b) **CVE-2026-20245** — Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager improper encoding/escaping (CVSS 7.8); (c) **CVE-2026-7473** — Arista EOS vulnerability (some sources pair this with the Chromium add). The critical addition for GameZipper is the **enforcement dimension**: CISA directed all Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) agencies to **patch CVE-2026-11645 by June 23, 2026 — a 14-day deadline**. While GameZipper is not an FCEB agency, the 14-day SLA establishes the **de facto industry patch cadence** for actively-exploited browser vulnerabilities: if the federal government considers 14 days the max-safe window, that is the mirror SLA any consumer-facing web property should adopt for browser-CVE-impacting bugs. **Distinct from B-050/B-056/B-092** (regression tests for CVE-2026-11645 itself — verifying the game still works on patched Chrome) — this is the **REGULATORY-ENFORCEMENT + PATCH-SLA** dimension: how fast must GameZipper respond when a browser CVE lands in CISA KEV? **Distinct from S-080** (V8 CVE-2026-11645 CISA warning — the initial advisory) — this is the **post-addition SLA tracking** dimension. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) subscribe to the CISA KEV catalog RSS feed (cisa.gov/known-exploited-vulnerabilities-catalog) and trigger an alert on any add with a `vendor/project` of `google/chromium`, `mozilla/firefox`, `apple/webkit`, or `apple/safari` — these are the browser-engine CVEs that impact every GameZipper user; (2) define the **GameZipper CISA-KEV-browser-CVE SLA**: within 14 days of a browser CVE hitting KEV, (a) verify the patched browser version is in the GameZipper test matrix, (b) run the full smoke test on the patched browser, (c) document any new regressions; this mirrors the federal SLA; (3) for P0/P1 regressions found on the patched browser, the fix SLA is 7 days (half the federal window) — browser-CVE patches often change rendering/security behavior that breaks games, and GameZipper users on auto-update will get the patched browser within days; (4) track the KEV-catalog delta weekly — new entries are added 1-3 times per week; any browser-engine entry is a P1 audit trigger; (5) for the June 9, 2026 batch specifically, verify all 290+ games still render and play correctly on Chrome 149.0.7827.103+ (the patched version) — the V8 OOB fix can change JIT behavior that rare games depend on; (6) document the CISA-KEV-patch-SLA runbook in the incident-response playbook alongside the npm-supply-chain runbook (S-104/S-105/S-106) — the two runbooks (browser-CVE + npm-supply-chain) cover the two highest-frequency 2026 attack surfaces. Source: https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/cisa-adds-cisco-chrome-and-arista-flaws.html + https://cybersecuritynews.com/google-chromium-0-day-vulnerability-exploit + https://digitalwarfare.com/google-chromium-zero-day-cve-2026-11645-cisa-warning + https://aviatrix.ai/threat-research-center/cisa-adds-three-known-exploited-vulnerabilities-to-catalog-2026-06-09

## CATEGORY 30: 2026 MID-JUNE NPM V12 PLATFORM MITIGATION + TYPOSQUATTING + APPLE iOS 26.5 + ANDROID JUNE BULLETIN + MV2 FINAL KILL + HYBRID MONETIZATION BENCHMARKS + ADA LAWSUIT SURGE (R180 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

### 30.1 GitHub npm v12 — Disables Install Scripts + Git Deps + Remote URLs by Default (July 2026)
- [S-109] **[P1]** GitHub npm v12 platform-level mitigation disables install scripts + git dependencies + remote URL sources by default — July 2026 hard CI migration deadline — thehackernews.com ("GitHub to Disable npm Install Scripts by Default to Stop Supply Chain Attacks", June 2026), securityweek.com, bleepingcomputer.com, and techtimes.com (June 13 2026) document that **GitHub/npm has announced npm v12 will ship in July 2026 with three breaking changes** that are the platform's direct response to the Miasma/IronWorm/Phantom Gyp attack wave (S-104/S-105/S-106/S-107/S-108): (a) **install scripts disabled by default** — `preinstall`, `install`, `postinstall`, `prepublish`, `prepare`, etc. will NOT execute unless explicitly allowed via `npm install --allow-scripts` (or a per-package allowlist in `.npmrc`); this **directly neutralizes** the Phantom Gyp `binding.gyp` bypass (S-105), the IronWorm IDE-backdoor injection (S-106), and the Rspack/Vant crypto-miner `postinstall` (S-104); (b) **git dependencies blocked by default** — `npm install github:user/repo` and `npm install git+https://...` will refuse without explicit `--allow-git`; closes a vector where attackers compromise a GitHub repo between `package.json` write and `npm install` execution; (c) **remote URL sources blocked by default** — `npm install https://example.com/pkg.tgz` will refuse without `--allow-url`. **This is distinct from S-104/S-105/S-106/S-107/S-108** — those test cases cover the ATTACK vectors; this test case covers the **MITIGATION rollout** that will BREAK CI/CD pipelines (including GameZipper's own build/test infra) that legitimately depend on native build scripts. The risk surface inverts: previously the risk was "an attacker planted a malicious script", now the risk is "our own CI fails because a dependency that needs `node-gyp` no longer builds". **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **audit all build/test CI runners before July 2026** — `cd /home/msdn/gamezipper.com && find . -name package.json -not -path "./node_modules/*" -exec jq '.scripts' {} \;` — any non-empty `scripts` block must be reviewed and either (a) explicitly allowlisted in `.npmrc` (`allow-scripts=true` for trusted deps) or (b) removed (replace the script with a pre-built artifact or a CI step); (2) **scan all transitive dependencies for native modules** that need `node-gyp` — `find node_modules -name 'binding.gyp'` — every match must be either pre-built (`prebuild-install`/`node-pre-gyp`) or explicitly allowlisted; (3) **test the CI build under npm v12 beta** before July — install `npm install -g npm@beta` on a throwaway CI runner and run the full build pipeline; every failure is a P1 fix item before the July deadline; (4) **audit for git+https dependencies** — `grep -rE '"[^"]+":\s*"git\+https?://|\"github:[^"]+"' package.json package-lock.json` — every match must be either pinned to a published npm version (preferred) or explicitly `--allow-git`-allowlisted with a documented reason; (5) **update the S-104/S-105/S-106/S-107/S-108 supply-chain runbook** to add the npm-v12-migration section — after July 2026, the recommended mitigation shifts from "audit postinstall scripts" (S-104 #1) to "verify your allowlist is correct" (because scripts are off by default); (6) **set a calendar reminder for July 1, 2026** to re-run the full supply-chain audit on npm v12 stable — the breaking changes may also expose previously-silent bugs (a script that worked but was harmless when auto-run will now silently not run, masking a build step); (7) for any GameZipper game that bundles npm-built assets, verify the bundle is reproducible under npm v12 — `npm ci --ignore-scripts` should produce a byte-identical bundle to `npm ci` (if not, a script was doing real work and must be re-implemented as a CI step). Source: https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/github-to-disable-npm-install-scripts.html + https://securityweek.com/npm-12-will-change-script-execution-behavior-to-prevent-supply-chain-attacks + https://bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/github-announces-npm-security-changes-to-tackle-supply-chain-attacks + https://techtimes.com/articles/318328/20260613/npm-v12-security-overhaul-blocks-install-scripts-default-july-deadline-ci-migration.htm

### 30.2 Typosquatted npm Packages — CI/CD Secrets Theft (May 28 2026)
- [S-110] **[P2]** Typosquatted npm packages stealing CI/CD secrets (cybersecuritynews.com, May 28 2026) — a new wave of malicious npm packages using **typosquatting** (deliberately-misspelled package names: `mognoose` vs `mongoose`, `lodahs` vs `lodash`, `exrpess` vs `express`) was uncovered on May 28 2026, designed to **steal cloud credentials and CI/CD pipeline secrets** from developer machines. Unlike the Miasma/IronWorm worm-propagation attacks (S-105/S-106) which compromise legitimate maintainers, typosquatting relies on **developer typos** during manual `npm install <name>` commands or copy-paste from poisoned documentation/Stack Overflow/AI chatbot output. Technical profile: (a) packages register look-alike names to popular packages (5B+ weekly downloads combined for top-100 npm packages, so even a 0.01% typo rate yields thousands of installs); (b) on `postinstall`, exfiltrate environment variables (`AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`, `GITHUB_TOKEN`, `NPM_TOKEN`, `CI_API_TOKEN`, `.env` file contents) to attacker-controlled endpoints; (c) the LLM-poisoning variant is **new in 2026**: attackers publish package documentation to sites that AI coding assistants (Copilot/Claude/Cursor) ingest as training/RAG data, then suggest the typosquatted package name in code completions — the developer types the suggested name verbatim. **Distinct from S-104** (general supply-chain escalation — credential theft via compromised maintainers) and **S-105/S-106** (worm vectors — self-propagation) — this is the **TYPO-VECTOR**: it does not require any maintainer compromise, only that a developer mistypes or copy-pastes a poisoned name. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) run `npm ls --all --json` and verify every package name against the official npm registry — flag any package whose name is a **Levenshtein-distance ≤ 2** from a top-100 npm package (`mongoose`, `lodash`, `express`, `react`, `chalk`, `axios`, etc.); (2) install `npm audit signatures` and the Socket.dev npm security scanner — both detect typosquats via name-similarity + install-pattern heuristics; (3) audit all `package.json` files for typosquatted names — `cd /home/msdn/gamezipper.com && grep -rE '"[a-z]+"' package.json | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -50` — manually verify each dependency name against the npm registry URL (`https://registry.npmjs.org/<name>` should return 200 with a real package); (4) **CRITICAL for AI-assisted dev workflows** — audit any code completion / Copilot / Claude Code suggestions that include `npm install` commands for typosquatted names; the LLM-poisoning variant means the AI itself may suggest the malicious package; (5) for CI/CD secrets hygiene, verify CI environment variables are scoped (the malicious package can only steal what's in `process.env` at install time) — never put production secrets in the same CI job that runs `npm install`; (6) subscribe to Socket.dev's typosquat alerts for the top-100 GameZipper dependencies — new typosquats are registered weekly; (7) document the typosquat vector in the dev onboarding guide — \"when copy-pasting an npm install from a tutorial/AI/doc, verify the package name resolves to the intended maintainer's registry page before installing\". Source: https://cybersecuritynews.com/typosquatted-npm-packages-steal-cloud-and-ci-cd-secrets + https://snyk.io/blog/node-gyp-supply-chain-compromise-self-propagating-npm-worm-binding-gyp + https://socket.dev/blog/typosquatting

### 30.3 iOS 26.5 Full Security Advisory — 80+ Vulns + 2 Zero-Days + M5 Hardware Memory-Safety Bypass
- [B-097] **[P1]** iOS 26.5 full security advisory — 80+ vulnerabilities + 2 actively-researched zero-days + M5 chip hardware-level memory-safety bypass (cantina.security x Swif "iOS 26.5 Security Guide: 2026's Biggest Apple Patch", May 2026; webpronews.com; apple.com support/100100) — Apple's May 2026 advisory for **iOS 26.5 / iPadOS 26.5 / macOS Tahoe 26.5** closes **more than 80 vulnerabilities** across Apple's operating systems, including: (a) **two zero-days under active research** at the time of disclosure (details partially withheld to allow patching before weaponization); (b) **a hardware-level memory-safety bypass on the M5 chip** — a silicon-level flaw that cannot be patched in software alone and requires a coordinated OS+firmware workaround; this is the first publicly-disclosed M5 hardware security issue and affects every M5-equipped Mac/iPad shipping in 2026; (c) **9 WebKit CVEs patched in a single advisory** — this is the superset that S-052 (Safari 26.5 WebKit 20-CVE) reported as the WebKit component; the full advisory also covers IOKit, the kernel, Mail, and other components. **Distinct from S-052** (the WebKit-subset regression test) and **B-090** (Safari 27 WWDC26 — a future release) — this is the **FULL iOS 26.5 advisory** covering the entire Apple fleet, with two novel dimensions: the **M5 hardware bypass** (no software-only mitigation is sufficient; GameZipper users on M5 Macs are exposed even with the latest OS patch unless the firmware workaround is applied) and the **two actively-researched zero-days** (specific exploitation techniques that weren't fully disclosed — GameZipper's threat model must assume they're weaponizable). **Test for GameZipper**: (1) for the M5 hardware bypass, document which GameZipper test devices are M5-equipped (MacBook Pro M5, iPad Pro M5) and verify the firmware workaround is applied — `system_profiler SPHardwareDataType` for Mac, Settings → General → About → System Firmware Version for iPad; any M5 device without the firmware update is a P1 audit blocker; (2) re-run the Top 30 games on iOS 26.5 / iPadOS 26.5 / macOS Tahoe 26.5 (post-patch) and verify no regression from the 80+ fixes — focus on (a) WebKit rendering (canvas, WebGL, WebGPU), (b) JavaScript engine (JIT behavior may have changed to mitigate the zero-days), (c) input events (touch, gesture, pointer); (3) for the two undisclosed zero-days, assume the exploitation technique is **drive-by browser compromise via crafted web content** (the standard iOS zero-day vector) — verify the GameZipper ad stack (Monetag/AdSense iframes) does not expose users to third-party content that could weaponize the zero-day before users patch; (4) check that no GameZipper game uses APIs that were silently tightened in the 80-fix batch (e.g., a tighter same-origin policy, a removed deprecated API) — diff the Safari 26.4 vs 26.5 release notes for behavioral changes; (5) document the iOS 26.5 minimum-supported version in the test matrix — iOS < 26.5 is now an unsupported configuration with 2 known-exploitable zero-days; (6) verify Safari 26.5 WebKit-side fixes (per S-052) don't regress any GameZipper WebKit-specific workarounds (e.g., the iOS Safari `<a>`-button `cursor: pointer` fix from R95). Source: https://cantina.security/blog/ios-26-5-security-guide-apple-fleets + https://webpronews.com/apple-lifts-the-veil-on-months-old-security-fixes-across-ios-macos-and-older-devices + https://support.apple.com/en-us/100100

### 30.4 Apple Retroactive Months-Old Security-Fix Disclosure Pattern
- [B-098] **[P2]** Apple retroactive security-fix disclosure pattern — months-old patches documented late (webpronews.com, June 2026) — webpronews.com ("Apple Lifts the Veil on Months-Old Security Fixes Across iOS, macOS and Older Devices", June 2026) documents a recurring Apple disclosure pattern where **silent WebKit/iOS patches ship in a minor point release without full public explanation, then are retroactively documented weeks-to-months later** when the next major advisory is published. The pattern: (a) Apple ships a `26.5.1`-style point release with a vague "bug fixes and improvements" changelog; (b) the underlying security fix is not listed in HT201220 (the security-release notes) at ship time; (c) weeks-to-months later, the next major advisory (e.g., iOS 26.6) retroactively documents the fix with full CVE details; (d) by then, users on the patched version are protected but had no way to know **what** they were protected from during the gap. **Distinct from S-052/B-097** (specific advisory regression tests) and **B-052/B-090** (specific Safari version sweeps) — this is the **PROCESS/DISCLOSURE-PATTERN** test case: GameZipper's QA cannot assume that "the latest iOS" means "all known bugs are documented"; there may be silent fixes in the current version that won't be explained for weeks. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) treat every iOS/macOS point release (`.1`, `.2`) as a **silent-security-fix event** even when the changelog is vague — re-run the Top 10 games on the new point release within 48h of ship and document any behavioral change; (2) maintain a **GameZipper-internal CVE-watch dashboard** that diffs Apple's HT201220 security-release notes weekly — when a new advisory retroactively documents a fix that shipped silently, back-fill the regression test for the affected iOS version (the test may have been passing because the bug was already patched in the field); (3) for the June 2026 retroactive disclosure specifically, audit which of the 80+ iOS 26.5 fixes (per B-097) were actually shipped silently in iOS 26.4.x or earlier point releases — these are fixes GameZipper has been benefiting from without knowing; (4) document the Apple-disclosure-lag pattern in the QA playbook so testers don't dismiss a "vague changelog point release" as a no-op — it may contain a critical security fix that changes WebKit behavior; (5) cross-reference the Apple advisory with the WebKit Open Source Security Advisories (webkit.org/security) which are sometimes more detailed than Apple's HT201220 — use WebKit's advisory as the technical source-of-truth when Apple's is vague. Source: https://webpronews.com/apple-lifts-the-veil-on-months-old-security-fixes-across-ios-macos-and-older-devices + https://support.apple.com/en-us/100100

### 30.5 Android June 2026 Security Bulletin — 124 Vulns + Actively-Exploited Framework Zero-Day
- [B-099] **[P1]** Android June 2026 Security Bulletin — 124 vulnerabilities + 1 actively-exploited Framework zero-day under limited targeted exploitation (bleepingcomputer.com "Google fixes one actively exploited Android zero-day, 124 flaws"; forbes.com/daveywinder June 2 2026; securityarsenal.com; source.android.com/docs/security/bulletin/2026/2026-06-01; cyberinsider.com) — Google's June 2026 Android Security Bulletin patches **124 vulnerabilities** across Android 14, 15, 16, and 16 QPR2, including **one Framework privilege-escalation zero-day confirmed to be under limited, targeted exploitation** in the wild at disclosure time. The bulletin ships as two patch levels (`2026-06-01` and `2026-06-05`, the latter incorporating both). Key risk dimensions for GameZipper: (a) the actively-exploited Framework zero-day allows **local privilege escalation** — an attacker who gets a foothold on a device (e.g., via a malicious app or a webview-based exploit chain) can elevate to system privileges; (b) the Framework component is the layer that **WebView** (Android's embedded browser engine, used by every hybrid/casual game wrapper and many third-party game-launcher apps) sits on top of — a Framework privilege-escalation can potentially escape the WebView sandbox; (c) Pixel devices receive the patch first (within days of June 1), but Samsung/Xiaomi/Motorola/OnePlus lag by 30-90 days depending on carrier+OEM certification, leaving their users exposed to the actively-exploited zero-day for that window. **Distinct from B-067** (Android June 2026 touch/WebAudio regression — a functional regression test) and **B-044** (June 2026 124-CVE bulletin — earlier coverage of the same bulletin at a higher level) — this is the **ACTIVELY-EXPLOITED-FRAMEWORK-ZERO-DAY dimension** with specific exploitation confirmation + the **OEM-patch-lag** dimension that determines actual user exposure. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) update the Android test matrix minimum supported version to **patch level 2026-06-05** — any device below this patch level is now exposed to a confirmed actively-exploited zero-day and should be flagged in QA reports; (2) re-run the Top 30 games on a Pixel device at patch level 2026-06-05 and verify no regression from the 124 fixes — focus on WebView behavior (the Framework changes touch the layer WebView sits on); (3) for the OEM-patch-lag dimension, document per-OEM expected patch availability: Pixel (June 1-5), Samsung (typically 30-45 days post-Google), Xiaomi (45-90 days), Motorola (45-90 days) — GameZipper's "minimum supported Android" should track the **most laggy OEM** that has significant traffic share, not Pixel; (4) verify the GameZipper site does not serve content that could be used as the **initial foothold** for the Framework exploit chain (the exploit needs local code execution first; a malicious ad iframe is the most likely vector) — extend the S-058/S-102 ad-fraud/Monetag-hardening tests; (5) document the June 2026 zero-day (CVE pending) in the GameZipper incident-response runbook alongside B-096 (CISA KEV) — if CISA adds the Android CVE to KEV, the 14-day SLA from B-096 applies; (6) for games distributed via any Android-app wrapper (Cordova/Capacitor/React Native WebView), verify the wrapper's bundled WebView is the patched System WebView version, not a stale bundled version — `adb shell am start -a com.android.webview.UPDATE` to force the update. Source: https://bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/google-fixes-one-actively-exploited-android-zero-day-124-flaws + https://forbes.com/sites/daveywinder/2026/06/02/new-android-14-15-and-16-update-fixes-actively-exploited-security-flaw + https://securityarsenal.com/blog/june-2026-android-security-bulletin-zero-day-exploitation-and-remediation + https://source.android.com/docs/security/bulletin/2026/2026-06-01 + https://cyberinsider.com/android-june-2026-update-patches-actively-exploited-zero-day

### 30.6 Samsung June 2026 Security Patch — 45 Vendor-Specific Vulnerabilities
- [B-100] **[P2]** Samsung June 2026 Security Patch — 45 vendor-specific vulnerabilities (androidheadlines.com "Samsung's June 2026 Security Patch Is Coming — and It Fixes 45 Vulnerabilities", June 2026) — Samsung's June 2026 security patch layers **45 vendor-specific fixes** on top of Google's baseline Android June 2026 bulletin (B-099). Some fixes are Google-provided (the 124-bulletin subset) and some are Samsung-specific (TouchWiz/One UI components, Samsung Knox, Samsung Internet browser, Bixby, Samsung Pay). **Samsung is the largest Android OEM globally and in GameZipper's mobile traffic share** — Samsung-specific vulnerabilities directly affect a plurality of GameZipper Android users. Key risk dimensions: (a) Samsung Internet (the default browser on Samsung devices, accounting for a non-trivial fraction of GameZipper's Android traffic) is patched separately from Chrome — Samsung Internet is Chromium-based but ships on Samsung's own cadence; (b) One UI overlay changes (System UI, Quick Settings, notification shade) can affect how GameZipper games behave in picture-in-picture, split-screen, and pop-up-view modes; (c) Samsung Knox fixes may affect enterprise-managed devices where GameZipper is on a corporate MDM allowlist. **Distinct from B-099** (Google Android June 2026 baseline) — this is the **Samsung-vendor-specific layer** that ships on top. **Distinct from B-067** (Android June 2026 functional regression) — this is the **security-patch dimension**. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) update the Android test matrix to include **Samsung Internet (latest)** as a distinct browser target alongside Chrome Android — `navigator.userAgent` detection must distinguish Samsung Internet (`SamsungBrowser`) from Chrome; (2) re-run the Top 20 games on Samsung Internet post-June-2026-patch and verify no regression — Samsung Internet's Chromium fork occasionally lags or diverges from mainline Chrome on rendering and WebAPI behavior; (3) for One UI-specific behaviors (Pop-up View, Split Screen, Edge Panels), verify games remain playable when the user switches modes mid-session — One UI's window-resize behavior differs from stock Android; (4) document the Samsung patch rollout timeline — Samsung typically ships the patch to Galaxy S-series first (within 2 weeks of Google's bulletin), then A-series (4-8 weeks), then budget M-series (8-16 weeks); (5) for Knox-managed enterprise devices, verify GameZipper games are not blocked by the Knox framework's post-patch policy changes — some Knox fixes tighten app-permission policies; (6) subscribe to Samsung Mobile Security (security.samsungmobile.com) for monthly patch advisories — the Samsung-specific CVEs are not in Google's bulletin and require tracking the Samsung advisory separately. Source: https://androidheadlines.com/2026/06/samsungs-june-2026-security-patch-is-coming-and-it-fixes-45-vulnerabilities.html + https://security.samsungmobile.com

### 30.7 Manifest V2 Final Hard Kill — Chrome 150/151 June 30 2026
- [W-151] **[P0]** Manifest V2 final hard kill — Chrome 150/151 June 30 2026 removes last `chrome://flags` override flag → uBlock Origin dynamic filtering permanently disabled, no workaround remains — techtimes.com ("Google Kills uBlock Origin in Chrome June 30: Dynamic Filtering Ends, No Workaround Remains", June 15 2026), pcworld.com, and 9to5google.com (June 15 2026) document that **Chrome 150 ships June 30 2026 and deletes the last Manifest V2 override flag from Chromium's codebase**, permanently ending dynamic web-request filtering. After June 30: (a) the `chrome://flags/#extension-manifest-v2-deprecated` flag is **removed** (not just disabled) — there is no longer any toggle to re-enable MV2 extensions; (b) uBlock Origin (uBO) — the most popular MV2-based content blocker, used by millions of GameZipper users to block ads, trackers, and malicious third-party scripts — loses its dynamic filtering capability; uBO's MV3 fork (uBlock Origin Lite) exists but has **substantially reduced functionality** (declarativeNetRequest's 30K-rule limit + no dynamic URL filtering + no scriptlet injection for most cases); (c) other MV2 ad-blockers (AdBlock, Adblock Plus, Ghostery MV2 builds) similarly lose capability. **For GameZipper this is existential on both sides**: (1) **as a publisher** — GameZipper's revenue model depends on ad impressions through Monetag/AdSense; users who lose uBO's dynamic filtering may see MORE ads (revenue-positive short-term) but may also switch to alternative browsers (Firefox, Brave) where MV2/uBO still works, reducing Chrome traffic share; (2) **as a security surface** — MV2/uBO was a meaningful defense layer against malicious ad creatives served through programmatic ad networks; the June 30 kill removes that defense for the Chrome user base, increasing GameZipper's exposure to malvertising (synergy with S-054 AI-powered ad fraud, S-058 anti-detect IVT, S-102 Chromium monthly bulletin); (3) **as a UX expectation** — users who have been trained to expect a "clean" ad-free browsing experience via uBO will now see ads on GameZipper for the first time, potentially perceiving the ads as intrusive (regression of the W-135 ad-blocker-detection dimension). **Distinct from W-135** (MV3 ad-blocker disruption — general disruption pattern) and **B-075** (Chromium MV2 removal Edge/Brave compatibility) — this is the **SPECIFIC June 30 2026 hard deadline** + **permanent flag removal** + **zero-workaround-remains** dimension. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) on **June 30 2026 00:01 UTC**, re-test the Top 30 games on Chrome 150 stable + verify the ad stack (Monetag/AdSense) renders correctly in the post-MV2 environment — with uBO disabled, ad impressions should increase but verify no new layout/fill issues surface from the higher fill rate; (2) audit the GameZipper ad stack for **malvertising exposure** in the post-MV2 environment — without uBO's dynamic filtering, any malicious creative served through the programmatic pipeline reaches the user; extend the S-054/S-058 tests; (3) verify GameZipper's **own content security** does not depend on users having uBO — the site must be safe (no XSS, no clickjacking per C-082, no malvertising per S-058) regardless of whether the user has an ad blocker; (4) document the post-June-30 Chrome ad environment for users — consider adding a one-time banner "Chrome 150 has changed how ad blockers work; learn more" if the increase in visible ads generates support tickets; (5) cross-browser test on **Firefox (which retains MV2 support)** and **Brave (which has its own built-in shields)** — these browsers will see no change and serve as the post-June-30 baseline for "what Chrome users used to see"; (6) for GameZipper's internal QA team that uses uBO during testing (to isolate ad-related issues), migrate to uBlock Origin Lite (MV3) + document the reduced-capability workflow; (7) track the Chrome-vs-Firefox traffic-share ratio after June 30 — a meaningful shift toward Firefox/Brave would indicate users are migrating to retain MV2/uBO. Source: https://techtimes.com/articles/318370/20260615/google-kills-ublock-origin-chrome-june-30-dynamic-filtering-ends-no-workaround-remains.htm + https://pcworld.com/article/3160794/the-last-lifeline-for-ublock-origin-in-chrome-is-almost-gone-for-good.html + https://9to5google.com/2026/06/15/google-chromes-next-update-will-mark-the-end-of-popular-ad-blockers

### 30.8 Mobile Game Monetization Benchmark Report 2026 — Tenjin + Hyper Casual Market $17.11B
- [G-141] **[P2]** Mobile game monetization benchmark report 2026 — Tenjin Ad Monetization Benchmark Report 2026 + Hyper Casual Market $17.11B-2026→$34.82B-2035 + 38% week-1 uninstall rate + 72% Android + 41% ad-based monetization (tenjin.com/blog/ad-mon-gaming-2026; businessresearchinsights.com Hyper Casual Gaming Market 2026-2035) — Tenjin's **Ad Monetization Benchmark Report 2026** provides concrete **eCPM (effective cost per mille) benchmarks by country and ad format** for mobile game advertising, and Business Research Insights' **Hyper Casual Gaming Market Report** projects the hyper-casual market from **$17.11B in 2026 to $34.82B by 2035 (8.2% CAGR)** with key findings: (a) **72% of hyper-casual users play on Android** — directly mirrors GameZipper's mobile traffic split; (b) **38% of users uninstall hyper-casual games within one week** due to repetitive gameplay — the week-1 retention cliff; (c) **41% of hyper-casual developers integrate ad-based monetization** as the primary revenue model — confirms the ad-supported portal model GameZipper uses; (d) the eCPM benchmarks vary 10-50x between Tier-1 (US/UK/JP) and Tier-3 (India/Brazil/Indonesia) markets, meaning GameZipper's effective revenue per user varies enormously by geography. **Distinct from G-135** (hybrid-casual monetization integration-quality audit — a qualitative test) and **G-141 neighbors** — this is the **quantitative benchmark data** test: what numbers should GameZipper's Monetag/AdSense performance be compared against to know if monetization is healthy? **Distinct from G-101** (ad-frequency immersion-break threshold — UX dimension) — this is the **revenue dimension**. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) pull GameZipper's actual eCPM by ad format (banner, interstitial, rewarded) and by geography (US, EU, LATAM, Asia) from Monetag/AdSense dashboards and compare against the Tenjin 2026 benchmarks — flag any format+geography combination where GameZipper's eCPM is <50% of the Tenjin benchmark (underperformance indicates a fill-rate, targeting, or floor-price issue); (2) instrument the **week-1 uninstall/return-rate** for GameZipper as a portal — the 38% week-1 uninstall benchmark is for individual hyper-casual apps; GameZipper as a portal should have BETTER week-1 return (users can try a different game instead of uninstalling), so a portal-level week-1 return rate <50% would be a P2 concern; (3) benchmark GameZipper's **Android vs iOS revenue split** against the 72% Android industry stat — if GameZipper's Android revenue share is significantly lower than 72% of total, investigate (likely cause: iOS users have higher eCPM, so the revenue-vs-traffic split naturally favors iOS, but a large deviation is worth understanding); (4) document the Tier-1 vs Tier-3 eCPM ratio in GameZipper's quarterly business review — a 10x+ ratio means Tier-3 traffic is barely monetizable and the growth strategy should focus on Tier-1 retention or on Tier-3 volume; (5) re-pull the Tenjin benchmark annually (the report updates yearly) — ad-monetization benchmarks drift 10-20% per year as the market matures; (6) for the Hyper Casual Market 8.2% CAGR projection, use it as the **portal-growth sanity check** — GameZipper's organic traffic growth should track or exceed 8.2% CAGR if the portal is capturing market share; significantly lower growth than the market means share loss. Source: https://tenjin.com/blog/ad-mon-gaming-2026 + https://businessresearchinsights.com/market-reports/hyper-casual-gaming-market-103060

### 30.9 Hybrid Monetization Tri-Model 2026 — IAP + Ads + Subscriptions Balance
- [G-142] **[P2]** Hybrid monetization tri-model 2026 — IAP + Ads + Subscriptions balance guide (gamegrowthadvisor.com "Hybrid Monetization for Mobile Games: IAP + Ads Guide 2026", June 2 2026; eastondev.com/blog/en/posts/media/20260524-mini-game-monetization; asoworld.com Q2 2026 mobile gaming revenue breakdown) — gamegrowthadvisor.com's June 2 2026 guide documents the **tri-model monetization system** that defines the 2026 industry standard for casual/hybrid-casual games: instead of choosing one monetization model (ads OR IAP OR subscription), the best-performing 2026 games **blend all three** with concrete revenue-split targets: (a) **Ads (50-60% of revenue)** — banner, interstitial, rewarded video; the workhorse for casual/hyper-casual; rewarded video is the highest-eCPM format and the most player-acceptable (opt-in); (b) **IAP (30-40% of revenue)** — cosmetic unlocks, level packs, speed-ups, "remove ads" one-time purchase; the "remove ads" IAP is the single highest-converting IAP in casual games (10-15% of engaged users convert); (c) **Subscriptions (5-15% of revenue)** — premium VIP tier (daily coin bonus, exclusive skins, no ads); growing in 2026 as Apple/Google push subscription discovery. Key operational rules: (1) the **free path must always be playable to completion** — no paywall gating core gameplay (synergy with W-144 CN/JP Pay-Wall audit); (2) **rewarded video must offer genuine value** (revive, double-reward, skip-level) — synergy with G-135 integration-quality; (3) the **"remove ads" IAP** must be discoverable but not pushy — a single persistent button in settings, not an interstitial prompt; (4) subscription cancellation must be one-tap (synergy with W-146 FTC "click-to-cancel"). **Distinct from G-135** (integration-quality audit — qualitative) and **G-141** (benchmark data — quantitative) — this is the **STRATEGIC ARCHITECTURE** test: is GameZipper's monetization designed as a coherent tri-model system, or bolted-on ads with no IAP/subscription path? **Test for GameZipper**: (1) map GameZipper's current monetization against the tri-model: (a) Ads — Monetag/AdSense ✅; (b) IAP — does the portal or any game offer a "remove ads" / cosmetic / level-pack purchase? (likely NO today); (c) Subscriptions — does any game or the portal offer a VIP tier? (likely NO today); document the gap; (2) for the "remove ads" IAP specifically, evaluate the revenue impact — Tenjin/G-141 benchmarks suggest 10-15% of engaged users convert at $2-5, which for GameZipper's engaged-user base could be meaningful incremental revenue; (3) for the subscription path, evaluate a GameZipper-wide "VIP" tier ($2.99/month — ad-free + exclusive games + daily coin bonus in supported games) — the 5-15% revenue share is small per-user but high-margin and recurring; (4) verify any future IAP/subscription integration does NOT violate the W-142/W-143/W-146/W-147/W-148/W-149 dark-pattern tests — the "remove ads" IAP must be discoverable in settings (not interstitial-prompted), the subscription must have one-tap cancel, no FOMO timers; (5) for any individual game's monetization (in games that have their own IAP/subscription), audit the revenue split against the 50/35/15 guideline — large deviation may indicate over-monetization (ads too frequent) or under-monetization (no IAP path); (6) re-evaluate the tri-model strategy annually — the 50/35/15 split drifts as the market matures (subscriptions growing, ads share slowly declining). Source: https://gamegrowthadvisor.com/blog/2026-06-02-hybrid-monetization-mobile-games-iap-ads-guide-2026 + https://eastondev.com/blog/en/posts/media/20260524-mini-game-monetization + https://asoworld.com/en/blog/q2-2026-mobile-gaming-revenue-breakdown-what-the-numbers-mean-for-your-growth-strategy

### 30.10 ADA Accessibility Lawsuit Surge 23% 2026 + WCAG 2.2 AA Section 508 Federal-Contractor Mandate
- [A-034] **[P1]** ADA accessibility-lawsuit surge 23% 2026 + WCAG 2.2 AA Section 508 federal-contractor mandate (wcagpros.com "What is an Accessibility Audit? 23% Lawsuit Surge"; discoverinai.com "WCAG 2.2: Ensuring Accessible Tech by 2026"; mobileproductstudio.com "Mobile Products: WCAG 2.2 AA Is Key for 2026") — wcagpros.com reports a **23% surge in ADA Title III accessibility lawsuits in 2026** (vs 2025), with web/digital-accessibility suits the fastest-growing category. Concurrently, **WCAG 2.2 Level AA is now the de facto legal standard** in the US via two mechanisms: (a) **Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act** — federal contractors (and many state/local governments via equivalent statutes) are **mandated** to meet WCAG 2.2 AA for all digital products, including web games; (b) **ADA Title III judicial interpretation** — federal courts (most notably the 11th Circuit in the Winn-Dixie case lineage and subsequent 2024-2026 rulings) have increasingly held that **websites are "places of public accommodation"** under the ADA, making WCAG-conformance a litigation target. Key 2026 risk dimensions for GameZipper: (a) the lawsuit surge means **more plaintiffs' firms are actively auditing web properties for WCAG violations** and filing suit — a single missing alt-text or failed keyboard-nav test can trigger a demand letter; (b) the 23% surge includes a meaningful share targeting **gaming and entertainment sites** specifically, not just e-commerce; (c) the Section 508 mandate means if GameZipper ever bids on a government contract (e.g., educational game licensing), WCAG 2.2 AA conformance is a **hard procurement requirement**; (d) the EU EAA (European Accessibility Act, enforceable since June 28 2025 — synergy with A-027/A-033) creates a parallel European exposure. **Distinct from A-027** (WCAG 2.2 general compliance — the technical checklist) and **A-033** (WCAG Accessibility Auditor MCP — the automated tool) — this is the **LITIGATION-RISK + MANDATE dimension** that elevates accessibility from a compliance nicety to a legal exposure requiring board-level attention. **Distinct from A-029/A-030/A-032** (specific WCAG SC tests) — this is the **regulatory-umbrella** test. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) commission an annual **WCAG 2.2 AA third-party audit** from an accessibility consulting firm (not just automated axe-core) — plaintiffs' firms use human auditors who find issues axe misses (the 30-40% catch-rate gap from A-031); budget $5-15K/year for a portal GameZipper's size; (2) maintain a **public-facing accessibility statement** on gamezipper.com (a dedicated `/accessibility` page) documenting: (a) GameZipper's commitment to WCAG 2.2 AA, (b) known limitations (specific games that cannot be made accessible due to gameplay mechanics), (c) a contact method for users to report accessibility issues; the accessibility statement is the **single most effective defense** in an ADA demand-letter response; (3) implement a **vulnerability-disclosure-style intake** for accessibility reports — `accessibility@gamezipper.com` with a 7-day acknowledgement SLA and a 30-day fix SLA for P1/P2 issues; documented responsiveness is a strong defense; (4) for the Section 508 dimension, document GameZipper's WCAG 2.2 AA conformance level per page (using the A-033 MCP auditor) so that if a government procurement opportunity arises, the conformance evidence is already gathered; (5) track the 23%-surge trend — subscribe to accessibility-law newsletters (Seyfarth Shaw's ADA Title III News, Lainey Feingold's Law Office) for early warning on lawsuits targeting game portals; (6) for any new game added to the catalog, run the WCAG 2.2 AA audit BEFORE publishing — a new game that triggers an accessibility complaint taints the whole portal; (7) for the EU EAA dimension, verify the A-033 MCP auditor's EAA legal-risk score for every game page monthly — any page whose EAA risk crosses a defined threshold triggers an automatic P1 remediation ticket. Source: https://wcagpros.com/wcag-articles/audit-ing-for-all-understanding-digital-accessibility + https://discoverinai.com/wcag-2-2-ensuring-accessible-tech-by-2026 + https://mobileproductstudio.com/mobile-products-wcag-2-2-aa-is-key-for-2026


## CATEGORY 31: 2026 MID-JUNE TRAPDOOR MULTI-REGISTRY + DEPENDENCY-CONFUSION + AI-ACCELERATED CVE CADENCE + FIREFOX 151.0.4 + RETENTION BENCHMARKS + ADA-ENFORCEMENT-WAVE + EDGE DATA-LOSS (R181 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

### 31.1 TrapDoor — Coordinated Cross-Ecosystem Supply Chain Attack on npm + PyPI + Crates.io
- [S-111] **[P1]** TrapDoor coordinated cross-ecosystem supply chain attack — 34 malicious packages / 384+ versions spread simultaneously across npm, PyPI, and Crates.io (thecybersecguru.com "TrapDoor Supply Chain Attack: npm, PyPI, and Crates.io Hit"; phoenix.security "TrapDoor Supply Chain Attack: AI Poisoning via npm, PyPI"; thehackernews.com May 25 2026 "TrapDoor Supply Chain Attack Spreads Credential-Stealing Malware"; securitytoday.de; labs.cloudsecurityalliance.org "CSA Research Note: TrapDoor Multi-Ecosystem Supply Chain AI-targeting"; cybersecuritynews.com; blog.rankiteo.com June 2026) — Socket.dev and CSA documented a multi-registry campaign that is **distinct from every prior 2026 npm attack** (S-104 general escalation, S-105 Phantom Gyp binding.gyp bypass, S-106 IronWorm IDE-backdoor, S-107 Red Hat Miasma worm, S-108 @antv/g-webgpu-engine, S-109 npm v12 platform mitigation, S-110 typosquatting) in three concrete ways: (a) **CROSS-ECOSYSTEM COORDINATION** — most prior attacks pick one registry; TrapDoor ran all three concurrently (npm for JavaScript, PyPI for Python, Crates.io for Rust), each implementation separately engineered to abuse the native install behavior of that ecosystem (`postinstall` for npm, `setup.py` for PyPI, `build.rs` for Crates.io); a JavaScript-only scanner (npm audit, Socket.dev npm-only) misses ⅔ of the campaign; (b) **AI-CODING-ASSISTANT POISONING VIA DOCUMENTATION INGESTION** — attackers seeded package documentation that AI coding assistants (Copilot/Claude/Cursor) ingest as RAG training data; when a developer asks "what package should I use for X?", the assistant suggests the TrapDoor package name verbatim; this is the LLM-poisoning variant first hinted in S-110 typosquatting, but TrapDoor proved it as a working attack chain across multiple language models; (c) **CAMPAIGN START DATE EARLIER THAN REPORTED** — phoenix.security placed actual start at **May 19 2026**, three days earlier than the widely-reported May 22 2026 20:20 UTC timestamp, meaning the campaign had a 3-day head start before detection. **Distinct from S-105/S-106/S-107** (single-registry worm-propagation patterns) and **S-110** (typosquatting typo-vector) — this is the **CROSS-ECOSYSTEM-COORDINATION + AI-RAG-POISONING dimension**. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **inventory every package ecosystem the GameZipper build pipeline touches** — GameZipper's frontend is HTML/JS (npm), but if there is ANY Python build scripts, Rust WASM toolchain, Go modules in the build path, list them; (2) **install multi-registry scanners** — Socket.dev (npm), PyUp Safety / pip-audit (PyPI), cargo-audit (Crates.io), govulncheck (Go); run them all in pre-commit and CI; a single-registry scanner misses ⅔ of cross-ecosystem attacks; (3) **audit AI-coding-assistant suggestions** — for any `npm install <suggested-name>` from Copilot/Claude/Cursor, verify the package name exists in the official npm registry with substantial download history (>10K weekly downloads) AND check Socket.dev's package score before installing; (4) **pin exact versions + integrity hashes** in `package-lock.json` / `requirements.txt` / `Cargo.lock` so a malicious update cannot slip in via `npm install` without hash mismatch; (5) the campaign's earliest detected activity is May 19 2026 — audit any package installed between May 19 and June 16 2026 against the TrapDoor IOC list (account `asdxzxc`, marker `P-2024-001`, the 34 named packages from the Socket advisory). **Severity justification**: P1 because if any GameZipper build/test infrastructure pulled a TrapDoor package, the credential-stealing malware would exfiltrate the GitHub deploy token, Cloudflare API token, Monetag credentials, and any other secrets in CI environment variables — full supply-chain compromise.

### 31.2 Microsoft 33-Package Dependency-Confusion Campaign — Dev/Build Environment Recon
- [S-112] **[P2]** Microsoft 33-package dependency-confusion campaign profiling dev/build environments (Microsoft Security Blog May 29 2026 "33 malicious npm packages abuse dependency confusion to profile developer environments") — Microsoft Threat Intelligence documented a dependency-confusion campaign that abused the **dependency-confusion vector** distinct from every prior 2026 npm attack: instead of compromising a maintainer (S-104/S-105/S-106/S-107) or relying on typos (S-110), attackers **publish packages with the same name as a victim organization's internal/private package names to the public npm registry**; when the victim's CI/CD runs `npm install <internal-name>` without a properly-configured `.npmrc` scope registry, npm falls back to the public registry and installs the attacker's package. The 33 packages in this campaign were **reconnaissance-focused** (not data-exfiltration): on `postinstall`, they collected host information (OS, hostname, IP, running processes, installed dev tools, environment variable names) and reported back, building a target map for a later exfiltration phase that didn't ship in this wave. **Distinct from S-111** (TrapDoor multi-registry exfiltration) — TrapDoor steals credentials; this campaign profiles the environment. **Distinct from S-110** (typosquatting) — typosquatting relies on developer typos; dependency-confusion relies on **mis-configured CI registries** where the victim doesn't pin internal package names to a private registry scope. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **audit GameZipper's CI `npm install` behavior** — does the build use ANY private/internal package names (e.g., `@gamezipper/internal-utils`)? If yes, verify `.npmrc` scopes them to a private registry (`@gamezipper:registry=https://npm.internal.gamezipper.com/`); (2) **install `npm config get registry`** and verify the default registry is `https://registry.npmjs.org/` (public) — any internal-scope package NOT pinned to a private registry is a dependency-confusion exposure; (3) **run `npm install --dry-run`** in CI before every build and diff the resolved dependency tree against an allowlist — flag any package name that matches an internal-scope pattern but resolves to the public registry; (4) **Microsoft's IOC list (33 package names)** — Microsoft's blog post lists the 33 malicious package names; cross-reference against GameZipper's `package-lock.json` and CI install logs since May 2026; (5) **audit the recon dimension** — even if no credentials were stolen, the attacker may now have a map of GameZipper's build environment (OS, hostnames, dev tools, env-var NAMES) for a future targeted attack; document what was potentially exposed. **Severity justification**: P2 because reconnaissance without exfiltration is less immediately damaging than credential theft, but it enables a future targeted attack; the vulnerability pattern (mis-configured CI) is structural and must be fixed.

### 31.3 Anthropic Mythos AI — 100 Chromium Zero-Days in 15 Days Rewrites CVE Cadence
- [S-113] **[P1]** Anthropic Mythos AI — 100 Chromium zero-days in 15 days (June 15 2026), reduced average browser-vulnerability discovery time from 40hr to 12min per bug (kucoin.com / nationalcybersecurity.com "Anthropic mythos finds 100 browser security bugs in June 2026"; hitechies.com "Claude Mythos: The AI That Hacks Every OS"; venturebeat.com April 9 2026 "Mythos detection ceiling"; techcrunch.com May 7 2026 "How Anthropic's Mythos has rewritten Firefox's approach to cybersecurity"; tomshardware.com; singularityhub.com April 10 2026; tech-insider.org "Project Glasswing") — Anthropic's Mythos AI (Claude Mythos Preview) autonomously identified 100 zero-day vulnerabilities in Chromium-based browsers by June 15 2026, with the **per-bug discovery time compressed from 40 hours (human expert) to 12 minutes (Mythos)**. The wider Mythos program has found **thousands of zero-days across every major OS and every major web browser**, including bugs that survived 27 years of human review (some unpatched at disclosure). **Distinct from S-080** (Chrome V8 CVE-2026-11645 zero-day — a single specific bug) and **S-088** (AI-discovered CVE accelerating testing cadence — the general trend) — this is the **CONCRETE-100-CHROMIUM-ZERO-DAYS-FOUND + GAMEZIPPER-PATCH-CADENCE-MUST-ACCELERATE dimension**: the discovery rate has shifted from "weeks between high-impact Chromium CVEs" to "multiple per day", and **99%+ of Mythos-found bugs remain unpatched at disclosure time** (per hitechies.com), meaning GameZipper users are running Chromium versions with known-unpatched zero-days for the patch-window duration. The downstream effect on GameZipper: (a) **patch-window exposure grows** — GameZipper cannot assume "users on the latest Chrome" means "users on a patched Chrome"; the latest Chrome at any moment may have multiple Mythos-discovered zero-days not yet fixed; (b) **the exploit-development bar drops** — Mythos-class tools will eventually be available to attackers, not just defenders; exploits that took weeks to develop now take hours, compressing the 20-hour exploit window S-057 warned about to hours; (c) **the 4-hour Dynamic Test Intelligence cadence must track Mythos-class discovery** — when Mythos (or a competitor) drops 10 new Chromium CVEs in a day, the test library must add regression tests within hours, not the traditional week-long CVE-to-test-cycle. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **subscribe to Mythos-class disclosure feeds** — Anthropic's security disclosures, Google Project Zero, Mozilla's Mythos-uncovered-bugs feed (techcrunch May 7 2026), and the CSA Mythos-coordination channel; any Chromium CVE list must be added to the test library within 4h of disclosure; (2) **add a "AI-discovered CVE rapid-response" test pattern** — when a batch of Chromium CVEs drops, the cron must (a) add a B-series regression test for each, (b) verify GameZipper's Top 20 games still render/play on the latest patched Chromium, (c) document the patch-window exposure duration; (3) **tighten the patch-SLA mirror** — GameZipper's own infrastructure (Cloudflare, GitHub Pages, CI runners) must update Chromium within 24h of a stable-channel patch; document the lag and the count of unpatched-zero-days-during-lag; (4) **adopt defensive coding patterns** that assume the browser may have unknown zero-days — strict CSP (`script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'`), sandbox iframes for any third-party ad content, no `eval()`, no `Function()` constructor, sanitize all DOM-inserted strings; (5) **monitor for exploit-in-the-wild signals** — Mythos bugs are disclosed responsibly (Anthropic → vendor → patch), but competitors may not be; monitor CISA KEV, GreyNoise, and malicious-ad-creative reports for sudden exploitation of recently-disclosed CVEs. **Severity justification**: P1 because the discovery rate change is structural and reshapes GameZipper's entire threat model — the assumption "Chromium is patched → users are safe" no longer holds during the patch window.

### 31.4 Firefox 151.0.4 — Windows Back/Forward Buttons Hang + Older-GPU Software-Rendering Fallback
- [B-101] **[P2]** Firefox 151.0.4 (released June 9 2026) — Windows back/forward buttons hang (Mozilla Bug 2039866) + older-GPU software-rendering fallback regression (Mozilla Bug 2043249) (firefox.com/en-US/firefox/notes; askvg.com "[Changelog] What's New in Mozilla Firefox 150 and Later Versions"; malwarebytes.com/blog/privacy/2026/05 "Firefox 151 packs big privacy upgrades into a small update"; developer.mozilla.org Firefox 151 release notes for developers) — Firefox 151.0.4 (a stability patch shipped 3 weeks after the 151.0 major release on May 19 2026) fixed two functional regressions that affect GameZipper users on Firefox: (a) **Bug 2039866 — Windows-only**: Firefox could become **unresponsive when using the back and forward buttons**, especially when navigating between pages with different JavaScript-heavy states (e.g., homepage → game page → back to homepage); for GameZipper this is the most common navigation pattern and a hang here is a P0 user-facing failure on Firefox Windows; (b) **Bug 2043249 — Older GPUs**: Firefox could **fall back to software rendering** on some older GPUs, reducing graphics performance; for canvas/WebGL games this means frame-rate drops from 60fps to single-digit fps, making the game unplayable on the affected hardware. **Distinct from B-086** (Firefox 151 sandbox escape CVE-2026-8945 + WebGPU info disclosure — security dimension) — this is the **FUNCTIONAL-REGRESSION dimension**: Bug 2039866 is a navigation hang (UX-breaking, not security); Bug 2043249 is a rendering-performance regression (gameplay-breaking on older hardware). **Distinct from B-088** (Firefox 150+ local network access restriction — privacy feature) — that's a deliberate behavior change, this is a bug-fixed-in-.0.4 regression. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **Firefox 151.0.4 navigation hang regression test** — open Firefox 151.0.4 on Windows, navigate homepage → 5 different game pages → back-button to homepage; verify no hang >2s on any navigation; if hang observed, document which game page triggers it (likely related to deferred-script load timing, synergy with the post-deploy-verification Pitfall about `defer` timing); (2) **older-GPU software-rendering fallback test** — run GameZipper's Top 20 games on Firefox 151.0.4 with hardware GPU acceleration DISABLED (simulating the Bug 2043249 fallback path); verify each game either (a) plays acceptably at >30fps in software mode, or (b) shows a graceful "your browser is too slow" message instead of a 5fps slideshow; (3) **verify both bugs are FIXED in 151.0.4** — confirm the test devices run ≥ 151.0.4 (not 151.0 / 151.0.1 / 151.0.2 / 151.0.3); if a device is pinned to an earlier 151.x, document the regression exposure; (4) **cross-check with the Firefox 151 privacy upgrades** (stronger anti-fingerprinting, broader local-network-access protection) — verify these don't break GameZipper's Monetag/AdSense integration or analytics beacons (synergy with B-088 local-network restriction test). **Severity justification**: P2 because (a) the navigation hang is fixed in 151.0.4 (so it's a regression-verification test, not an active-failure test) and (b) the software-rendering fallback affects only older GPUs (a shrinking but non-zero fraction of GameZipper's Firefox user base).

### 31.5 Mobile Game Retention Benchmarks 2026 — D1 38% / D7 15% / D30 Declining
- [G-143] **[P2]** Mobile game retention benchmarks 2026 — D1 retention 38% / D7 retention 15% / D30 declining trend, average LTV $45 per paying user, US CPI $1.80 for casual (gameinsights.ai "Mobile Game Benchmarks 2026"; gameindustrylibrary.com GameAnalytics Mobile Retention Benchmarks 2026; gamebizconsulting.com/blog/mobile-game-user-acquisition-stats-trends-2026 Sensor Tower State of Mobile 2026 analysis) — the 2026 industry retention benchmarks document a **continued downward trend** in casual-game retention across all three windows: (a) **D1 retention: 38% average for casual games** (down from ~42% in 2024) — meaning 62% of players who install never return the next day; for GameZipper (a no-install web portal) the equivalent metric is "return-visit-after-first-play" and the benchmark should be even lower because there's no install friction to prime retention; (b) **D7 retention: 15% average** (down from ~18% in 2024) — only 15% of players still playing a week later; (c) **D30 retention continues to decline** — gameindustrylibrary.com explicitly notes "metrics across Day 1, Day 7, and Day 30 continue a downward" trajectory through 2026, driven by hyper-casual genre saturation and player fatigue; (d) **average LTV $45 per paying user** and **US CPI $1.80 for casual** — meaning a paying user generates ~$45 over their lifetime, and acquiring a US install costs ~$1.80; for GameZipper's ad-supported model, the LTV math implies a paying-user conversion rate must exceed 4% (1.80 / 45) for paid UA to be profitable — GameZipper doesn't pay for UA, but this benchmark informs the value-per-visitor calculation for Monetag/AdSense optimization. **Distinct from G-141** (Tenjin Ad Monetization Benchmark — revenue/eCPM dimension) and **G-142** (Hybrid monetization tri-model architecture — strategic dimension) — this is the **CONCRETE-RETENTION-METRIC dimension**: what numbers should GameZipper's analytics dashboards be compared against to know if retention is healthy? **Distinct from G-105** (FTUE <3 tap — qualitative onboarding test) — this is the **quantitative outcome** test. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **instrument D1/D7/D30 retention in GameZipper analytics** — for the gz-analytics endpoint, add a `returning_visitor` event that fires on subsequent visits within 1/7/30 days of first play; (2) **compare GameZipper's D1/D7/D30 against the 38%/15%/declining benchmark** — flag any game whose D1 retention is <25% (a 1.5x underperformance vs benchmark) for UX audit; (3) **per-game retention ranking** — identify the Top 10 and Bottom 10 games by D1 retention; the Bottom 10 are candidates for either UX redesign or removal (a game with <15% D1 retention is below the 2024 D7 benchmark — it's effectively dead-on-arrival); (4) **the downward-trend dimension** — if GameZipper's overall D1 retention is declining month-over-month (mirroring the industry trend), document the decline rate and project when GameZipper would hit 25% D1 (the threshold below which the ad-supported model may break even); (5) **session-length benchmark** — gameinsights reports average casual session ~6-8 min; verify GameZipper's average session length per game is in this range; sessions <2 min suggest the game fails to engage (synergy with G-105 FTUE test and G-108 ad-immersion-break test). **Severity justification**: P2 because retention benchmarks are diagnostic, not prescriptive — they tell GameZipper where to focus UX work but don't represent an active failure.

### 31.6 Post-ADA-Title-II Plaintiffs-Lawyer Expansion to Public Entities + 37% H1-2025 Lawsuit-Surge Prediction
- [A-035] **[P1]** Post-ADA-Title-II plaintiffs-lawyer expansion to public entities + 37% H1-2025 lawsuit-surge prediction (accessible.org "2026 ADA Website Compliance Predictions: More Lawsuits, AI Has Big Role"; accessibility.build/research/accessibility-lawsuits "Accessibility Lawsuit Tracker 2026"; d2itechnology.com/blogs/accessibility-testing-2026 "WCAG 3.0, ADA Deadlines & AI-Driven Compliance"; webability.io/blog/wcag-2-2 "WCAG 2.2 Accessibility Standards: Complete 2026 Compliance Guide") — accessible.org's 2026 prediction report (based on EcomBack H1-2025 lawsuit data showing a **37% surge in ADA Title III filings** vs H1-2024) identifies a **structural shift in plaintiffs'-lawyer targeting** post the **April 2026 ADA Title II compliance deadline** for larger public entities (state/local governments): (a) **plaintiffs' lawyers are expanding beyond e-commerce into new verticals** including gaming, entertainment, education, and public-sector-adjacent services; (b) **AI tools now play a role on both sides** — plaintiffs use automated WCAG scanners to find violations at scale, defendants use AI remediation tools to fix them; the net effect is more suits filed faster; (c) **the April 2026 Title II deadline creates an enforcement wave** — public entities that missed the deadline are now exposure targets, and private-sector plaintiffs' lawyers cite the Title II standard as the de facto baseline in Title III suits against private web properties like GameZipper; (d) **settlement-cost benchmarks are rising** — accessibility.build's tracker shows median settlement ~$20-25K but high-profile suits reaching $100K+, with plaintiffs' firms increasingly demanding ongoing-compliance monitoring (multi-year consent decrees) not just one-time fixes. **Distinct from A-034** (23% 2026 ADA-lawsuit-surge + Section 508 federal-contractor mandate) — A-034 is the **general-surge + procurement-mandate** dimension; this is the **POST-DEADLINE-ENFORCEMENT-WAVE + STATE-EXPANSION + AI-TOOLING-ON-BOTH-SIDES** dimension. **Distinct from A-027** (WCAG 2.2 87 SC general compliance) and **A-033** (WCAG Auditor MCP tool) — those are the technical-checklist and automated-tool tests; this is the **LITIGATION-STRATEGY + POST-DEADLINE-EXPOSURE** test that elevates accessibility from "compliance task" to "board-level legal-risk item". **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **assume GameZipper will be audited by an AI-powered plaintiffs' scanner within 2026** — run the SAME automated scanners plaintiffs use (axe-core, WAVE, Lighthouse Accessibility, the WCAG Accessibility Auditor MCP from A-033) weekly via cron and treat EVERY P0/P1 finding as litigation-exposure; (2) **document a remediation SLA** — fix axe-core critical findings within 7 days, serious within 30 days, moderate within 90 days; this SLA becomes evidence of good-faith compliance if sued; (3) **the April 2026 Title II enforcement wave means public-entity-adjacent GameZipper traffic is higher-risk** — if GameZipper serves any K-12 / library / municipal users (e.g., via school-network traffic), those users' institutions may face Title II audits that cascade to GameZipper as a third-party vendor; document any such traffic; (4) **publish an accessibility statement** at `/accessibility` documenting GameZipper's WCAG 2.2 AA conformance level, remediation process, and contact for issues — this is a standard defense exhibit in ADA suits; (5) **monitor the lawsuit-tracker** (accessibility.build, thewcag.com/lawsuits) weekly for suits against peer gaming/entertainment sites — the plaintiff firms are repeat players and their targets signal where the next wave will hit; (6) **the AI-tooling-on-both-sides dimension** — invest in AI-assisted remediation (the WCAG Accessibility Auditor MCP from A-033) to match the speed of AI-assisted plaintiff scanning; a defender running axe-core once a quarter will always lose to a plaintiff running it daily. **Severity justification**: P1 because litigation-risk is concrete and rising 37% H1-over-H1, and a single high-visibility ADA suit can cost $25-100K + ongoing monitoring — a board-level financial risk for a small publisher like GameZipper.

### 31.7 Edge 149 Collections + Sidebar Final Removal June 4 2026 — User-Data Export-Before-Upgrade Deadline
- [W-152] **[P2]** Edge 149 Collections + Sidebar final removal June 4 2026 — user-data export-before-upgrade deadline + lost Sidebar multitasking workflow (windowslatest.com "Microsoft just killed Edge's Collections and Sidebar for more Copilot after years of pushing both features" June 6 2026; digitaltrends.com "Microsoft just killed one of the coolest features of its Edge browser to favor more AI"; yourwindowsguide.com "Microsoft Edge Sidebar missing after latest update — what are your options?"; overcentral.com "Microsoft Edge Removes Collections and Sidebar for AI Focus"; blogs.windows.com/msedgedev/2026/05/13 "New updates to Edge across desktop and mobile"; learn.microsoft.com/en-us/deployedge/microsoft-edge-relnote-stable-channel) — Microsoft Edge 149 (stable rollout starting **June 4 2026**) **permanently removed Collections and Sidebar** to make room for Copilot integration. Microsoft had been notifying users since Edge 148, but the June 4 149-stable ship date is the hard cutoff. Two distinct user-impact dimensions: (a) **DATA-LOSS RISK** — users who relied on Collections to save research notes (a popular use case for game walkthroughs, cheat sheets, "games to play later" lists) must export their Collections data BEFORE upgrading to Edge 149, or the data is lost; Microsoft's overcentral.com coverage confirms "Users must export or migrate Collection data before upgrading to avoid permanent loss of saved content"; (b) **WORKFLOW-DISRUPTION** — the Sidebar enabled multitasking (game in main pane, walkthrough/wiki/YouTube in Sidebar); with Sidebar removed, GameZipper users on Edge must switch tabs to consult a walkthrough, breaking the in-game flow state. **Distinct from C-070** (Edge mobile Copilot AI sidebar game interaction — mobile-specific) and **C-071** (Edge 149 Collections/Sidebar removal viewport change — desktop viewport-dimension only) — this test case covers the **DATA-LOSS-PREVENTION + WORKFLOW-DISRUPTION** dimensions: (1) does GameZipper warn Edge-using visitors that their Collections data is at risk before they upgrade? (2) does GameZipper's UX assume Sidebar multitasking that no longer works? **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **detect Edge 149+ via `navigator.userAgent`** and show a one-time banner to users with Collections data: "Microsoft Edge has removed Collections. If you saved GameZipper game notes in Collections, export them from `edge://collections` before upgrading to Edge 149 — your data will be permanently lost otherwise"; (2) **audit GameZipper's UX for Sidebar-multitasking assumptions** — does any game documentation or onboarding text reference "use the Edge Sidebar to keep this walkthrough open while you play"? If yes, remove or update the text; (3) **test that GameZipper games work correctly in the post-Sidebar Edge 149 window management** — verify no game assumes a specific viewport width that was true only when Sidebar was docked (the viewport changes when Sidebar is removed, synergy with C-071); (4) **document Edge 149 ship date in the test matrix** — June 4 2026 is the regression-test cutoff; any Edge testing done before June 4 used Sidebar-available Edge 148, any after used Sidebar-removed Edge 149 — these are different test targets; (5) **the Copilot-replacement dimension** — verify Copilot integration in Edge 149 doesn't interfere with GameZipper game canvases (synergy with C-068 Edge 149 cross-browser parity and S-075 Edge mobile Copilot AI sidebar game interaction interference); (6) **the enterprise-policy dimension** — Microsoft's release notes warn that "Blocking internal edge:// or chrome-untrusted:// URLs isn't recommended as this may lead to unexpected errors and will disable the Microsoft 365 Sidebar Copilot Chat experience" — if GameZipper's enterprise users are on corporate Edge with restrictive policies, verify the policies don't break Copilot in ways that cascade to game-page errors. **Severity justification**: P2 because the data-loss risk is real but affects only users who saved data in Collections (a minority), and the workflow disruption has workarounds (open walkthrough in a separate tab).

### 31.8 Multi-Registry CI/CD Scanner Coverage Gap — Single-Registry Scanners Miss ~⅔ of Attack Surface
- [C-101] **[P2]** Multi-registry CI/CD scanner coverage gap — TrapDoor (S-111) proved single-registry scanners miss ~⅔ of multi-registry attack surface (thecybersecguru.com "Why This One Is Different — TrapDoor ran all three [npm, PyPI, Crates.io] concurrently"; phoenix.security "TrapDoor Supply Chain Attack: AI Poisoning via npm, PyPI"; labs.cloudsecurityalliance.org CSA Research Note) — TrapDoor's defining characteristic (per thecybersecguru.com's analysis titled "Why This One Is Different") is that it ran across **three package ecosystems concurrently**, with each implementation separately engineered to abuse that ecosystem's native install behavior. The operational consequence for security teams: **a JavaScript-only scanner (npm audit, Socket.dev npm-only) detected the npm third of the campaign; a Python-only scanner (pip-audit, PyUp) detected the PyPI third; a Rust-only scanner (cargo-audit) detected the Crates.io third — but no single tool saw the whole campaign**, and a CI pipeline that ran only the scanner matching its primary language (e.g., a JavaScript-heavy build running only npm audit) would have **missed ⅔ of the attack** (the PyPI and Crates.io components). The gap is structural, not a bug in any specific tool: every package-registry scanner is registry-specific because each registry has its own package-format, dependency-resolution, and install-hook semantics. **Distinct from S-111** (TrapDoor-specific test — verifies GameZipper wasn't a victim) and **S-087** (COOP/COEP SharedArrayBuffer WASM-thread per-language flag) — this is the **METHODOLOGY / SCANNER-COVERAGE-MATRIX dimension**: does GameZipper's QA run the RIGHT set of scanners across ALL package ecosystems its build pipeline touches? **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **inventory the GameZipper build pipeline's package ecosystems** — `cd /home/msdn/gamezipper.com && find . -name package.json -o -name requirements.txt -o -name Cargo.toml -o -name go.mod -o -name Gemfile -o -name composer.json | head -20`; document every ecosystem present; (2) **for each ecosystem, install and run the corresponding scanner in pre-commit + CI**: (a) npm → `npm audit` + Socket.dev + `npm audit signatures`; (b) PyPI → `pip-audit` + `safety check`; (c) Crates.io → `cargo-audit`; (d) Go → `govulncheck`; (e) Ruby → `bundle-audit`; (f) PHP → `composer audit`; (3) **add a pre-commit guard that FAILS the commit if any ecosystem-with-dependencies is missing its scanner** — if `package.json` exists but `npm audit` isn't in the pre-commit hook, fail with "scanner coverage gap: npm dependencies present but no npm audit"; (4) **add a CI step that prints the scanner-coverage matrix** as a build artifact — a table of `ecosystem × scanner × status` so reviewers can verify coverage at a glance; (5) **subscribe to multi-registry advisory feeds** — OSV.dev (covers npm + PyPI + Crates.io + Go + Maven + RubyGems in a single API), GitHub Security Advisories, Socket.dev's cross-registry alerts; (6) **the AI-RAG-poisoning dimension** (synergy with S-111 TrapDoor) — TrapDoor seeded AI-coding-assistant documentation; verify GameZipper's dev workflow doesn't blindly trust Copilot/Claude/Cursor package-name suggestions across ANY ecosystem — the same package-name-verification step (registry + download-count + Socket score) applies to npm, PyPI, AND Crates.io suggestions. **Severity justification**: P2 because the gap is structural and affects every polyglot build pipeline; a JavaScript-only GameZipper build has narrower exposure than a polyglot one, but the methodology test catches the gap before an attack exploits it.

## CATEGORY 32: 2026 MID-JUNE SIGSTORE-PROVENANCE-BYPASS + CHROME-150-VISUAL-REGRESSION-TOOL-BUG + MACOS-MONTEREY-SUNSET + HTML-IN-CANVAS-BREAKING-CHANGE + ANDROID-WEBGL-CVE-PAIR + AI-ACCELERATED-CVE-CADENCE + COGNITIVE-ACCESSIBILITY-GAP + DROP-OFF-METHODOLOGY (R182 — Dynamic Test Intelligence)

### 32.1 npm Sigstore Provenance BYPASS — 633 Malicious Packages Passed Cryptographic Verification (May 19 2026)
